STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT [ESRS 2 SBM-2]

Eni considers stakeholder engagement and dialogue to be a key enabler for achieving its sustainability objectives, as set out in its Strategic 

and long‑term Plan, as well as for preventing and mitigating potential negative impacts of the energy transition, including impacts on the 

workforce. In line with the Code of Ethics, Eni fosters relationships with stakeholders based on fairness, legality, transparency, traceability, 

respect  for  human  rights,  inclusion,  gender  equality,  and  the  protection  of  the  environment  and  local  communities.  Operating  in  62 

Countries  characterised  by  diverse  socioeconomic  contexts,  understanding  the  expectations  of  stakeholders  affected  by  the  Group’s 

activities, particularly vulnerable groups, and maintaining continuous, transparent dialogue are essential to building strong and trust‑based 

relationships. This dialogue is supported by the disclosure of corporate objectives and results and by ongoing stakeholder participation. 

This approach is consistent with the recommendations of the Corporate Governance Code adopted by Eni, under which the BoD promotes 

dialogue with shareholders and other relevant stakeholders through appropriate channels, based on the principles defined by the Board 

itself, the Code of Ethics and the Policy on investor engagement. Eni’s commitment to Carbon Neutrality and to a just energy transition 

requires structured and inclusive engagement with key stakeholder groups, including: (i) the 

 Workforce, also through appropriate social 

dialogue and listening initiatives as well as reskilling and upskilling programmes including to support potential redeployment processes; 

(ii) 

 Suppliers, to identify and manage the impacts of the energy transition, with particular attention to supporting small and medium‑sized 

enterprises in their transformation pathways and in maintaining competitiveness; (iii) 

 Local Communities, to contribute to economic and 

social development and to maximise the positive local impacts in the areas where Eni operates; (iv) 

 Consumers, to promote responsible 

and efficient energy consumption and to offer innovative energy solutions. In relation to these groups of right-holders, Eni has developed a 

 human rights management model that is integrated into its core corporate processes. To support engagement with local stakeholders, 

Eni  as  adopted  a  ‘Stakeholder  Management  System’9  (SMS),  operating  at  both  central  and  subsidiary  level.  The  system  maps  over 

7,400 stakeholders and supports the definition of engagement strategies, as well as the management of requests and issues raised by 

stakeholders. The table below provides, for each stakeholder category, information on objectives, activities carried out during the year, and 

the outcomes achieved through this dialogue. Stakeholder feedback is taken into account in defining the Company’s strategy and 

 Other 

ESG commitments and targets, as well as of the 

 Business Model. Eni adopts differentiated engagement tools and levels of stakeholder 

engagement — including workshops, institutional meetings, consultations, training programmes, information events, collaborations with 

other  entities,  dedicated  channels,  and  partnerships  for  sustainable  development  and  innovation  —  managed  by  dedicated  corporate 

functions  with  clearly  defined  responsibilities  for  each  stakeholder  category.  This  approach  varies  according  to  stakeholder  type  and 

ensures structured, coherent dialogue in line with the principles of the Code of Ethics. In order to share Eni’s approach to Sustainability 

and its Energy Transition pathway with the various reference stakeholders, in 2025 numerous initiatives were implemented, both at local 

level (including Ravenna, Côte d’Ivoire, Mexico and Mozambique) and at central level (Gazometro in Rome and Brussels), also leveraging 

voluntary  sustainability  reports,  published  at  local  level.  To  further  facilitate  interaction,  Eni  has  established  a  single  Whistleblowing 

Reporting  Platform,  shared  with  its  subsidiaries  and  accessible  both  online  and  via  the  intranet.  The  platform  allows  all  internal  and 

external stakeholders to report, including anonymously, any suspected violations of the Code of Ethics. Further details are provided in the 

 Business Conduct section. 

(9) Management tool used to track relationships with stakeholders and to monitor the progress status of projects and the results achieved.

PROCESS AND RESULTS OF THE DOUBLE MATERIALITY ASSESSMENT 

[ESRS 2 IRO-1; GOV-2] 

The 2025 materiality assessment, aimed at identifying the sustainability topics that are most relevant to Eni and its stakeholders, builds on the 

results of the 2024 assessment, which was already carried out in accordance with the ESRS and the double materiality principle. Under this 

principle, Eni identifies its most significant impacts — both positive and negative, actual and potential — on the environment and people, including 

human rights (the ‘impact materiality’ or ‘inside-out’ perspective), as well as the sustainability-related risks and opportunities that may significantly 

affect the Company’s development, performance and financial position over the short, medium or long-term (the ‘financial materiality’ or ‘outside-in’ 

perspective). During 2025, changes in both the Group’s business activities and the external context were assessed; these analyses did not identify 

the emergence of new material topics compared to the previous reporting cycle, confirming the continued relevance of the materiality outcomes 

identified in the prior year. To further validate the materiality results related to the Company’s activities, the topics and concerns of the different 

stakeholder categories were reviewed through the involvement of the corporate functions responsible for managing the relevant relationships, 

in order to assess whether any new aspects not identified in the previous cycle had emerged (see 

 Stakeholder Engagement). With regard to 

impacts classified as non-material in 2024, additional in-depth analyses were carried out, also involving corporate functions and companies 

representing the different business segments: these impacts were confirmed as non-material also for 2025. From a disclosure perspective, in 

order to enhance the usability of information, the presentation of social impacts was updated through a greater level of aggregation compared to 

the 2024 Eni Sustainability Statement, without any changes to the underlying content. The financial materiality assessment was updated based 

on the results of the Integrated Risk Management process (hereinafter, IRM), which did not identify changes in material risks compared to 2024. 

In addition, a consistency check was performed on the opportunities identified as material in the previous year against the current Strategic Plan, 

in order to assess their continued validity for 2025. Finally, building on the value chain materiality analysis carried out in the previous reporting 

cycle, targeted in‑depth analyses of potential environmental impacts were conducted during 2025, as described below. 

Phases of the materiality process 

Eni’s materiality process, defined in the previous reporting year and confirmed as valid for the current year, is structured around the following 

phases: 

• IdentificationofthelistofpotentiallymaterialtopicsrelatedtoEni’sactivitiesanditsvaluechain,bothupstreamanddownstream, based 

on a top‑down approach. This approach considered the Company’s objectives, insights from benchmarking and contextual analyses, the 

requirements set out in the ESRS and the relevant GRI sector standards, as well as the outcomes of the human rights due diligence process, 

including the update of Eni’s mapping of the so called Salient Human Rights Issues10 (see 

 Human Rights for Eni). The analysis also took 

into account Eni’s materiality assessments carried out in the previous year, at Group level, and by subsidiaries that prepared a sustainability 

report. In addition, to reflect stakeholders’ interests, priority topics identified during the year by the corporate functions engaging with the 

different stakeholder categories were considered.

•  Identificationofimpacts,risksandopportunities(IROs)associatedwithpotentiallymaterialtopics. For the impact assessment, publicly 

available  sources  were  reviewed,  and  relevant  internal  functions  were  involved.  Based  on  their  experience  in  the  respective  areas  of 

responsibility, they identified impacts related to the Company’s activities, considering also relevant aspects across the value chain. During 

the analysis, impacts were also disaggregated, where necessary, in order to identify specific geographical areas or business segments in 

which such impacts are particularly significant. The process for identifying risks associated with potentially material topics is based on the 

broader IRM process (see 

 Integrated Risk Management), under which risks are identified and assessed in relation to the achievement of 

Eni’s main objectives. The outcomes of the assessment also include risks related to ESG topics, including those arising from dependencies 

on natural, human and social resources, as well as risks linked to impacts on the environment and people. Opportunities were identified with 

reference to the Strategic Plan, with a focus on initiatives that are effectively being pursued by the Company.

•  Definitionofthemodelfortheassessmentofimpacts,risksandopportunities(IROs), under which, in accordance with the ESRS and the 

EFRAG Guidance on materiality, assessment scales were defined, based on specific drivers depending on the topic, as follows: (i) for impact 

materiality, impacts are assessed in terms of significance, determined through a combination of scale, scope and irremediable character 

(the latter applicable exclusively to negative impacts), and the likelihood of occurrence11. The negative impact related to climate change 

is considered material based on recognised scientific consensus; (ii) for financial materiality, in line with the Integrated Risk Management 

framework, risks are assessed by combining the likelihood of occurrence with the magnitude of effects, measured using both quantitative 

and qualitative metrics. These include, for example, economic and financial metrics, based on reductions in operating cash flow or net profit, 

(10) Limited to social topics, the analysis considered the mapping of so-called Salient Human Rights Issues and therefore includes potential negative impacts on the most significant 
human rights matters, in line with the provisions of the relevant international instruments. The reporting of impacts actually verified during the year is provided in the ‘Actions’ sections 
within the various social thematic chapters.
(11) The probability of current impacts was not assessed, as the impact has already occurred.

ENI ANNUAL REPORT 2025

--- PAGE 149 ---
MANAGEMENT 
REPORT

CONSOLIDATED 
FINANCIAL STATEMENTS

ANNEX

147

and reputational metrics, assessed in terms of the duration of reputational impacts and the stakeholders affected. For both perspectives, 

the model provides likelihood assessments as well as evaluations of significance (for impact materiality) and magnitude of effects (for 

financial materiality), using rating scales ranging from 1 to 5. For opportunities, relevance is assessed by combining likelihood and relevance; 

relevance is evaluated through both a qualitative scale, defined by two levels of significance, and a quantitative scale based on capital 

expenditure (CapEx) and operating expenditure (OpEx). With regard to likelihood, a two‑dimensional probability scale is applied, with the 

highest level attributed to opportunities included in the Strategic Plan.

•  AssessmentofthesignificanceoftheIROs: (i) for impact materiality, the assessment was carried out by the relevant corporate functions 

through an IT platform12 that enables the traceability of the evaluation process. Subsequently, the companies representing the different 

business segments were involved to identify and assess any additional impacts specific to their activities/sectors13. Based on the overall 

assessments, impacts were classified as material where they exceeded the internally defined materiality threshold, as determined through 

a two-dimensional matrix combining likelihood and significance. These correspond to Tier 1 and Tier 2 impacts, out of a total of three tiers; 

(ii) for financial materiality, risks are assessed in terms of likelihood and magnitude of effects and represented within a matrix structured into 

three tiers (Tier 1, Tier 2 and Tier 3, in descending order of relevance); risks classified within Tier 1 and Tier 2 represent Eni’s main risks (Top 

Risks - see the  

  Integrated Risk Management section) and are therefore considered material for the purposes of financial materiality. The 

assessments are based on data and assumptions that vary depending on the nature of the risk and, where available and relevant, leverage 

both historical data on past events and forward‑looking estimates, including those developed with the support of relevant functions (e.g. 

market scenario forecasts). The scope of risk assessment activities is defined by selecting the subsidiaries included in the assessment 

process, based on qualitative and quantitative drivers, in order to ensure adequate coverage of the Company’s strategic objectives. The 

assessment of opportunities is conducted through an integrated process, involving the Sustainability, Integrated Risk Management and 

Strategic Planning functions, with a focus on consistency with corporate plans and with initiatives that are already implemented or planned. 

Based on the assessment of strategic and economic relevance, only opportunities classified as Tier 1 were considered.

•  Discussion of the impact materiality results emerging from the assessment, through direct engagement with experts, via dedicated 

meetings, on the topics assessed and/or on CSRD matters, including international sustainability organisations, audit and consulting firms 

and financial institutions.

•  Definition of the list of material IROs and calibration of the results, based  on the assessments carried out and, where applicable, on 

predefined thresholds. This process involved the prioritisation of the impacts, risks and opportunities relevant to the Company and the 

exclusion of all IROs below the materiality threshold. The outcomes of the analysis were then reviewed from an overall perspective, also 

taking into account inputs gathered from subject-matter experts, in order to refine the final list of impacts and opportunities, where necessary. 

The results of the analysis, with specific reference to material IROs, were shared14 with the Control and Risk Committee, the Sustainability 

and Scenarios Committee and the Board of Statutory Auditors, and with the BoD in occasion of the approval of the Sustainability Statement.

MATERIAL TOPICS FOR ENI AND ITS STAKEHOLDERS [ESRS 2 SBM-3]

The table below presents the results of the materiality assessment, in terms of impacts, risks and opportunities, associated with ESRS topics 

and/or with aspects relevant to the business and the sector. Compared to the previous year, the results reflect a more in-depth analysis of impacts 

related to the value chain, as further described below. Based on the identification of material IROs, the relevant ESRS topics and sub-topics, as 

well as the corresponding material datapoints, were identified and are disclosed in the thematic chapters. These chapters provide further detail 

on the specific impacts and on the effects of IROs on the business model and strategy (for example, by addressing the link between the climate 

strategy, the business model and the supplier engagement strategy aimed at building a sustainable supply chain). For further details on business 

activities associated with material impacts and on the actions undertaken to address the identified IROs, reference should be made in particular 

to the sections dedicated to IROs and related actions within the individual thematic chapters. With regard to the main risks to which the Group 

is exposed, reference is made to the sections 

 Integrated Risk Management and 

 Risk factors and uncertainties, while details on the Group’s 

performance in 2025, are provided in the 

 Commentary on Economic and Financial Results. As regards current financial effects arising from 

risks, no significant impacts were identified during the year. For information on impairment testing and provisions recognised in the financial 

statements, particularly those relating to the abandonment and restoration of depleted fields and the dismantling or removal of industrial facilities, 

(12) This  platform  enables  the  assessment  of  impacts  by  internal  functions,  ensuring  traceability  of  the  assessments  and  related  changes;  within  the  platform,  for  completeness 
purposes, the materiality assessment of the KPIs associated with the different topics has also been tracked.
(13) For further details on any potential link between material impacts and business activities or relationships, please refer to the sections describing the IROs in the individual thematic 
chapters.
(14) The involvement of the administrative and control bodies took place during the sharing of the final list of material IROs. Management was involved, within its areas of responsibility, 
in the IROs‘ assessment processes.

VALUE CHAIN AND MAIN IMPACTS 

Eni is present across the entire energy value chain, from the exploration, development and extraction of resources15 to the marketing 

of energy, products and services to end customers (for further details see 

 Activities). Through its 

 Business model, Eni combines 

the  use  of  largely  proprietary  technologies,  with  the  development  of  an  innovative  satellite  model,  based  on  the  establishment  of 

dedicated  companies  capable  of  independently  accessing  markets  to  finance  their  growth.  In  Eni’s  operating  context,  the  ability  to 

build a network of partnerships and alliances with stakeholders and host Countries is a key factor in ensuring their involvement in the 

Company’s activities and in the complex process of energy system transformation. From this perspective, Eni develops and manages 

its operations in close interconnection with upstream and downstream actors along the value chain, while monitoring the potentially 

significant impacts that may arise from these interconnections. In fact, to identify the most significant impacts across its value chain, 

the materiality assessment did not focus exclusively on Eni’s direct activities — which already cover a substantial portion of the energy 

value chain — but also included, starting from the previous reporting cycle, dedicated in‑depth analyses of the value chain. For 2025, 

the  results  of  the  analysis  carried  out  in  the  previous  reporting  cycle  were  confirmed. This  analysis  focused  on  impacts  that  occur 

broadly across the entire value chain, regardless of business segment, specific activities or geographical areas. In particular, among 

the impacts identified, the relevance of climate change  both upstream and downstream is confirmed for the environment topic, with 

the main emission‑related impacts concentrated in the use phase of the products and services sold (Scope 3 emissions; for further 

details  see 

  Climate  Change/Metrics).  With  regard  to  social  matters,  material  impacts  were  identified  in  relation  to  human  rights 

and workforce health and safety, particularly within so‑called labour‑intensive supply‑chain activities, such as plant construction and 

maintenance and general services16. Along the downstream value chain, potential impacts emerged in the interactions with customers 

and  consumers,  mainly  linked  to  unclear  commercial  or  communication  practices.  Finally,  in  the  area  of  governance,  impacts  were 

identified in relation to cybersecurity, loss of information confidentiality and corruption episodes, particularly in upstream activities. 

Conversely, potential positive impacts include support for the sustainable growth of the business ecosystem through the dissemination 

of  sustainability  principles,  the  promotion  of  sustainable  energy  use  among  customers  and  consumers,  and  the  development  of 

initiatives aimed at protecting the health of value chain workers and promoting community health. These initiatives are implemented 

in partnership with actors along the value chain and other relevant stakeholders. Moreover, during 2025, the value chain analysis was 

expanded  and  deepened  to  identify  additional  potential environmental impacts  specific  to  the  Oil  &  Gas  value  chain.  This  in-depth 

analysis, focused on strategic suppliers within the value chain, was developed through multiple streams: (i) analysis of sustainability 

information  published  by  companies;  (ii)  direct  engagement  with  selected  suppliers  through  interviews;  (iii)  identification  of  the 

outcomes of suppliers’ materiality analyses available via Open‑es17; and (iv) analysis of selected data providers, such as Encore, which, 

depending on the sector, support the identification of environmental impacts, risks and dependencies. Based on the outcomes of these 

analyses,  in  addition  to  the  topics  already  identified  in  the  previous  year,  further  potential  environmental  impacts  were  considered 

material  for  Eni’s  value  chain  where  they  consistently  emerged  across  all  analytical  streams  (as  reported  in  the  materiality  results 

table). In particular, these include aspects related to potential water and soil pollution from accidental spills and potential impacts 

on biodiversity, both associated with drilling activities, plant construction, the manufacture of components and equipment, chemical 

production and maritime transport carried out by suppliers on behalf of Eni.

151

The resilience of the strategy to material IROs [ESRS 2 SBM-3]

The assessment of the resilience of the strategy with respect to material impacts, risks and opportunities is integrated into the process 

for defining the Strategic Plan, starting from the preparation of the proposal, taking into account the underlying risk profile, through to 

its  review  by  the  BoD.  The  BoD  is  required  to  assess  the  degree  of  compatibility  of  the  identified  risks  with  a  management  approach 

that is consistent with the strategic objectives set. The assessment of the risks compatibility with strategic objectives is supported by 

the  Integrated  Risk  Management  process,  which  provides  a  comprehensive  view  of  the  Company’s  main  risks,  including  those  related 

to  sustainability  matters,  and  their  evaluation,  taking  into  account  the  mitigation  actions  implemented.  The  risk  profile  underlying  the 

Strategic Plan is further analysed through an integrated assessment of the effects of risks on financial objectives, as well as through the 

analysis of the Plan’s de-risking actions related to the Company’s strategic risks. In addition, the process for defining the Strategic Plan, 

incorporates considerations regarding measures to mitigate negative impacts, as well as aspects concerning the achievability of positive 

impacts and material opportunities identified, to ensure the achievement of the defined objectives. With regard to mitigation actions, the 

Company adopts a set of measures designed to reduce material impacts and its exposure to the main risks to which it is subject. These 

measures  are  summarised  in  the 

  Integrated  Risk  Management  chapter  and  in  the  individual  thematic  chapters  of  the  Sustainability 

Statement. Eni also carries out dedicated scenario analyses to assess the resilience of its strategy with respect to climate‑related impacts 

and risks (as detailed in the 

 Climate Change chapter), as well as similar analyses concerning nature‑related and 

 Biodiversity risks. 

For social matters, Eni has implemented corporate 

 Due Diligence processes and systems aligned with relevant frameworks and best 

practices. These enable the Company to identify potential negative impacts related to its operations, value chain, products or services and 

business relationships (see   Human Rights for Eni).

Climate Change [ESRS E1] 

POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; E1-2] 

As  stated  in  its  Mission,  Eni  ‘concretely  supports  a  just  energy  transition  […]  and  promotes  efficient  a  sustainable  access  to  energy’;  this 

commitment is reflected in the company’s policies, as well as in the Code of Ethics, and is substantively implemented through the definition 
and execution of a   Decarbonisation Plan.

POLICY

KEY CONTENTS:

 Code of Ethics

•  Commitment to contribute positively to the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals and to support a lower 
carbon and socially just energy transition, also through innovative solutions aimed at reducing the impact of its operations 
by promoting the efficient use of natural resources, the protection of biodiversity and water resources, and by supporting 
mitigation and adaptation actions in the local contexts in which it operates.

•  Commitment, as a signatory to the Paris Pledge, to support the objectives set out in the Paris Agreement.

 Supplier Code of Conduct

•  Requirement for its suppliers to commit to environmental protection, optimisation of resource use and contribution to 
the achievement of plant efficiency and emission reduction targets, thereby supporting Eni along its pathway towards 
Carbon Neutrality to 2050.

Internal Regulatory System

•  Specific procedures defining, in line with the main international standards, the methodologies for emissions reporting (for 

further information, please refer to the 

 Metrics section).

CLIMATE GOVERNANCE [ESRS 2 GOV-3; E1 GOV-3]

The decarbonisation plan forms an integral part of Eni’s business strategy, in line with the Company’s Mission. Under the adopted governance model, 

the management of the Company is entrusted by the Board of Directors (BoD) to the Chief Executive Officer (CEO), with the exception of the most 

significant strategic, operational and organisational powers, which remain reserved to the BoD. Within this framework, both the CEO and the BoD 

play a central role in managing key aspects related to climate change. The CEO is responsible for defining the climate strategy and, following its 

approval by the BoD, for implementing it and its related decarbonisation targets, ensuring their integration into the Strategic Plan and corporate 

decision‑making processes. The BoD reviews and approves, upon proposal of the CEO, the Strategic Plan, which includes industrial business targets 

as well as financial results and sustainability objectives, including decarbonisation targets. In carrying out these responsibilities, the BoD is supported 

by the Sustainability and Scenarios Committee (SSC), an internal Board committee dedicated to assisting the Board in its assessments and decisions 

regarding processes, initiatives and activities aimed at overseeing the Company’s commitment to sustainable development across the entire value 

chain. For the topics examined by the SSC during the year and additional details on the role of the corporate bodies, please refer to the 

 Governance 

section. The effective implementation of the corporate strategy is further supported by Eni’s Remuneration Policy through incentive systems designed 

for Directors, General Managers, Executives with strategic responsibilities and other Executive Managers. In particular: (i) the Long‑Term Incentive 

Plan, equity‑based, includes specific environmental‑sustainability and energy‑transition objectives (overall weighting of 35%), structured around targets 

linked to decarbonisation and transition processes (20% Net GHG emissions Upstream Scope 1 and 2, and 15% Biojet fuel production capacity); 

(ii) the Short‑Term Incentive Plan is likewise closely linked to Eni’s strategic‑transformation objectives and includes an environmental‑sustainability 

target focused on reducing net GHG emissions Upstream Scope 1 and 2, consistent with the Long‑Term Incentive Plan. This target has a weighting 

of 20% for the CEO, while for management the weighting varies according to assigned responsibilities. For further information, please refer to the  

 Report on the 2026 Remuneration Policy and Remuneration paid 202518. Eni’s organisational structure is also evolving in line with its energy‑transition 

path.  Starting  from  2024,  business  activities  have  been  grouped  into  three  structures:  (i)  Transition  &  Financial,  responsible  for  developing  and 

implementing Eni’s economic and financial strategy and including Plenitude and Enilive; (ii) Global Natural Resources, which manages the upstream 

Oil & Gas portfolio, the development of Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) businesses and agri‑hub activities, and oversees the Power Generation 

& Marketing business and Trading activities; (iii) Industrial Transformation, focused on accelerating the industrial‑transformation activities of the 

Chemicals business (Versalis), the conversion of the traditional downstream (Refining) and the evolution of environmental‑remediation activities (Eni 

Rewind). Lastly, an equally important aspect of the transition process is Eni’s engagement with policymakers — both directly and through industry 

associations — to contribute, in line with respective roles and responsibilities, and drawing on its experience as an international energy company, to the 

development of strategies and regulations aimed at promoting the path towards Carbon Neutrality by 2050 (see   Business Conduct).

(18) The remuneration linked to the Short‑Term and Long‑Term Incentive Plans for 2025 was paid on the basis of the performance of the Net Carbon Footprint Upstream indicator, 
defined on an equity boundary (see 

 Metrics).

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E1-4]

A detailed and comprehensive framework of Eni’s GHG emission reduction targets and commitments is provided in the 

 Decarbonisation 

Plan section.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3; E1 IRO-1]

Material impacts 

The process carried out by Eni to identify its climate‑related impacts considered scientific19, regulatory20 and guidance references21, all of which 

indicate  that  GHG‑emission  estimation  is  the  parameter  used  to  assess  the  company’s  negative  outward  impact.  Aware  of  the  need  for  a 

collective response to the global decarbonisation challenge, Eni has long been committed to a pathway for reducing GHG emissions towards 

the goal of Carbon Neutrality by 2050 (see 

 Decarbonisation Plan). In line with major international standards and industry best practices22, 

Eni has implemented internal procedures for identifying emission sources and defining methodologies for calculating direct and indirect GHG 

emissions. These calculations are based on assessments carried out by operators at individual industrial sites and subsequently consolidated 

centrally (bottom‑up approach). Through the mapping of emission sources, Eni has established an inventory of actual direct and indirect GHG 

emissions. To ensure that the data‑collection and control process is robust and structured, specific procedures and adequate controls have 

been implemented within the Sustainability Statement control system. The Group’s emission profile is also assessed on a forward‑looking basis 

by estimating potential GHG emissions in line with the Strategic Plan projected to 2050. For further details, please refer to the   Metrics section.

PROCESS FOR PRIORITISING EMISSIONS IMPACTS

Since  the  early  2000s,  Eni  has  developed  a  structured  GHG  emission  inventory  that  includes  detailed  information,  among  other  aspects,  by 

business type and emission source. This inventory enables the identification of priority areas for intervention, while also considering the evolution 

of the international climate debate. For example, decisions taken in international forums — including those adopted at the Conferences of the 

Parties (COP) — as well as energy and climate‑scenario analyses and United Nations studies ‑ such as the scientific reports published by the 

IPCC23 ‑ provide indications on the main levers available for decarbonisation (see 

 Main reference scenarios). Analysis of the external context 

highlights  a  clear  orientation  towards  prioritising  actions  that  reduce  Scope  1  and  2  emissions,  for  which  technologically  feasible  mitigation 

options exist. This also includes the reduction of methane emissions — a greenhouse gas with a higher global‑warming potential24 than carbon 

dioxide — for which economically and technically viable solutions are already available. Consistent with this approach and with the assessment 

of its own emissions inventory, Eni has, since 2016, defined a series of targets aimed at improving the GHG emission performance of its assets, 

with a specific focus on methane and flaring. Subsequently, starting in 2020, Eni established a decarbonisation pathway that also includes targets 

for indirect emissions, with the aim of reaching Carbon Neutrality by 2050. This extension reflects an incremental approach: after first addressing 

emissions directly under its control (Scope 1 and 2), Eni progressively expanded its focus to indirect value chain emissions, which by their nature 

can only be managed indirectly. The process for identifying, assessing and prioritising emissions impacts is updated annually. Over the years, this 

process has been progressively refined, in line with the maturation of scientific evidence and growing global awareness of climate issues.

Material risks

Climate‑related risk refers to the possibility that changes in climate scenarios or conditions may generate transition risks (market, regulatory 

and legal, technological and reputational) and physical risks (acute and chronic) affecting Eni’s businesses in the short, medium and long‑

term. Risks associated with the implementation of the initiatives included in the Strategic Plan are also considered, as these may influence the 

achievement of decarbonisation targets in terms of timing, performance or economic viability ‑ for example, in cases of delays in delivering 

industrial projects, technological under‑performance or higher‑than‑expected development costs. The process for identifying and assessing 

climate‑related risks is part of Eni’s 

 Integrated Risk Management model, as described in the section 

 Process and results of the double 

materiality assessment. This process ensures the identification, consolidation and analysis of Eni’s risks and supports the Board of Directors 

in  verifying  the  compatibility  of  the  company’s  risk  profile  with  strategic  objectives  –  including  from  a  medium  to  long‑term  perspective 

–  while  monitoring  the  evolution  of  key  risks  and  de‑risking  actions.  Risks,  including  climate  change,  are  assessed  considering  both  the 

likelihood of occurrence and the potential effects on Eni’s quantitative and qualitative objectives that would materialise if the risk were to 

occur. Based on the metrics defined for risk assessment, climate change is considered by Eni to be a top risk.

(19) The prevailing position of the scientific community identifies GHG emissions as the main cause of climate change, while recognising that there is no linear relationship between GHG 
emissions and the resulting climate change impacts (see IPCC AR6 and, for example, Rial et al., 2004; Trudinger and Enting, 2005; Millar et al., 2017).
(20) As an example, the climate standard ESRS E1 identifies a company’s GHG emissions as an impact on climate change (Disclosure Requirement ESRS 2 IRO‑1, paragraph 20a).
(21) OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises on Responsible Business Conduct (2023).
(22) WBCSD/WRI GHG Protocol Initiative and the IPIECA/API/IOGP Petroleum Industry Guidelines for Reporting Greenhouse Gas Emissions (2011).
(23) The IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change), established in 1988 by the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) and the United Nations Environment Programme 
(UNEP), is the leading international body for assessing climate change.
(24) It refers to the ability of a gas to persist in the atmosphere for a given period of time.

ANNEX

SCENARIO ANALYSIS

The identification of Eni’s physical and transition risks is also supported by an in-depth analysis of climate scenarios. At the international 

level, a wide range of scenarios currently exists, developed by numerous analysts, consultants, organisations and energy-sector entities. 

These scenarios rely on different construction methodologies and outline a broad spectrum of possible evolutions of the energy system. 

They  combine  decarbonisation  levers,  technologies,  assumptions  on  the  evolution  of  consumption  patterns  and  policy  frameworks  in 

different ways, in order to illustrate potential future trajectories and provide a useful reference framework for guiding strategic choices 

and  policy  directions.  In  this  context,  given  the  absence  of  universally  recognised  decarbonisation  pathways  for  the  Oil  &  Gas  sector, 

Eni  analyses  multiple  prospective  pathways  characterised  by  heterogeneous  mixes  of  solutions  and  milestones,  among  which  those 

developed by the International Energy Agency (IEA) and the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) are particularly relevant. 

Specifically, climate scenarios belonging to the IPCC C1-C2 categories were used as a reference for assessing the compatibility of Eni 

decarbonisation plan25 with trajectories aligned with the 1.5°C limit by 2100, while acknowledging the intrinsic limitations of comparing 

a corporate context with the global one (see 

 Decarbonisation levers). The IEA Net Zero Emissions (NZE) scenario was considered for 

assessing the resilience of Eni’s strategy to transition risks (see 

 Resilience of the strategy to transition scenarios), whereas the IPCC 

SSP5-RCP8.5 (C8) scenario was used for the analysis of physical risks (see 

 Physical risks).

Main reference scenarios

IEA - Net Zero Emissions (NZE) Scenario: in the World Energy Outlook 2025, the IEA revised the assumptions underpinning the NZE 

scenario (the scenario targeting Net Zero emissions by 2050), shifting from a limited-overshoot pathway to a high-overshoot26 one. In 

this scenario — for which the IEA provides numerical detail only at the global level — decarbonisation of the energy system is achieved 

primarily through increasing electrification and a major scale-up of variable renewables (whose share in the power-generation mix rises 

from today’s 15% to around 75% in 2050), improved energy efficiency, and rapid deployment of CO2 capture and storage technologies for 

industrial processes (CCUS), bioenergy with CCS (BECCS) and direct air capture (DACS). In terms of energy mix, the NZE-IEA scenario 

features  a  substantial  decline  in  the  role  of  fossil  fuels,  whose  share  in  the  global  mix  drops  from  the  current  79%  to  16%  by  2050, 

alongside a falling energy demand (0.5% per year on average between 2024 and 2050). By 2050, the most affected energy source is coal, 

with emissions falling by 99% compared with 2024 (consumption down 92%), followed by natural gas (95% emissions; consumption 

down 80%) and oil (90% emissions; consumption down 77%). 

Unlike the IEA, the IPCC develops climate scenarios27 by considering all GHG emissions, not only CO2 from the energy sector. These 

scenarios are grouped into eight categories (C1-C8)28 based on the projected increase in global temperature by 2100 associated with 

each pathway. Categories C1 and C2 include over 200 scenarios capable of limiting global warming to 1.5°C: (i) C1 includes scenarios 

with  no  or  limited  overshoot  (with  Net  Zero  GHG  emissions  reached  between  2030  and  2100,  depending  on  the  scenario);  (ii)  C2 

includes high-overshoot scenarios. The decarbonisation levers and technologies deployed in these pathways, while broadly similar to 

those of the NZE-IEA scenario, are combined in different ways. For example, electrification in these trajectories does not necessarily 

rely almost exclusively on variable renewables – it may also derive from an expanded role of nuclear power. Some scenarios feature 

a greater use of CCUS and biofuels, which are particularly relevant for decarbonising hard-to-abate sectors, and incorporate land-use 

measures (e.g., reforestation and afforestation) as mitigation levers. Overall CO2 emissions in the C1 category decline by 50% between 

2019 and 2030 (range: −60% to −40%), whereas in the C2 category they fall by 5% (range: −5% to 0%). For comparison, over the same 

period, the NZE-IEA 2025 scenario projects a decrease in global CO2 emissions of 19%29. In the figure below30, which shows changes 

in emissions and fossil-fuel consumption between 2019 and 2030, IPCC scenarios display a wide variability in the evolution of Oil & 

Gas demand, which in C2 pathways is projected to increase by 2030. By contrast, coal consumption is expected to decline in both 

categories by 2030.

(25) Defined on Entity-specific targets.
(26) An overshoot refers to the temporary exceeding of a given global-warming level, such as 1.5°C. Overshoot implies a temperature peak followed by a subsequent decline in global 
warming, achieved through anthropogenic CO2 removals exceeding the remaining global CO2 emissions (source: IPCC Glossary, Special Report: Global Warming of 1.5°C). Two types of 
overshoot are recognised: (i) ‘Limited’ overshoot, indicating an exceedance of up to about +0.1°C above 1.5°C (corresponding to warming of around 1.6°C); ‘High’ overshoot, indicating 
an exceedance of +0.1°C to +0.3°C (corresponding to warming between ~1.6°C and ~1.8°C). In both cases, the exceedance persists for several decades (source: IPCC Climate Change 
Synthesis Report, 2023).
(27) Assessment Report 6, WGIII (2021).
(28) The categories range from very low emissions (C1) to very high emissions (C8), with intermediate categories limiting global warming at different thresholds: C2 at 1.5°C after high 
overshoot; C3-C4 at 2°C; C5 at 2.5°C; C6 at 3°C; C7 at 4°C (source: IPCC Climate Change Synthesis Report, 2023).
(29) Change in CO2 emissions: 2019 data from IEA, Global CO2 emissions from energy combustion and industrial processes and their annual change, 1900-2023 (link: https://www.
iea.org/data-and-statistics/charts/global-co2-emissions-from-energy-combustion-and-industrial-processes-and-their-annual-change-1900-2023);  2030  data  from  IEA,  World  Energy 
Outlook 2025, Table 7.1, p. 354.
(30) Data from the IPCC Assessment Report 6, WGIII (2021), Chapter 3, Table 3.6 (p. 353).

155

Finally, category C8 includes 29 scenarios that project a doubling of global GHG emissions relative to 2015 levels and a rise in average 

global temperature of more than 4°C. These scenarios describe the potential increase in the frequency and intensity of certain acute 

and chronic meteorological and climatic phenomena (e.g., heatwaves, heavy precipitation, glacier retreat). They assume an energy‑

intensive economic growth pattern (SSP5) and a high concentration of future GHG emissions (RCP8.531).

TRANSITION RISKS

The context in which Eni operates is significantly influenced by global commitments to achieving carbon neutrality and by potential shifts in 

consumer preferences, which could lead to a structural decline in hydrocarbon demand over the medium‑to‑long‑term and to an increase in 

operating costs for the Oil & Gas sector. Uncertainties regarding future demand trends and the feasibility or profitability of decarbonisation 

technologies make long‑term investment decisions inherently risky. Furthermore, growing public scrutiny of climate issues and increasingly 

stringent stakeholder expectations may result in more limited access to capital markets and could challenge the licence to operate of Oil & 

Gas companies. For a detailed contextual analysis of individual drivers or transition events (market, regulatory and legal, technological and 

reputational), please refer to the 

 Risk factors and uncertainties section. In response to these emerging trends, Eni identifies mitigation 

actions (see   Actions taken on material IROs) aimed at minimising risks. Specifically, Eni evaluates variables that may affect operating costs 

– such as carbon price signals – and monitors the resilience of its strategy against transition scenarios.

INTERNAL CARBON PRICING [E1-8]

In 2025, Eni applied an internal carbon pricing mechanism (shadow price) to assess the economic and financial exposure of its investments 

to the potential introduction of carbon pricing systems in the Countries where it operates. The analysis conducted in 2025 used the IEA’s Net 

Zero scenario (World Energy Outlook), adopting a 2050 price range differentiated by level of economic development (advanced economies, 

emerging  economies  with  and  without  Net  Zero  commitments32).  For  major  investment  projects,  the  expected  return  is  subjected  to  a 

sensitivity analysis aimed at estimating the potential impacts of CO2 costs on project profitability. This analysis is applied only where the 

Country’s current legislation does not already provide for an explicit carbon pricing system. The results are reviewed by the Board of Directors 

both at the preliminary authorisation stage of each investment falling within its remit (Final Investment Decision ‑ FID) and subsequently as 

part of the annual project‑monitoring process.

(31) RCP (Representative Concentration Pathways): scenarios that include time series of emissions and concentrations of all greenhouse gases, aerosols and chemically active gases, 
as well as land‑use/land‑cover changes. The term ‘representative’ indicates that each RCP provides only one of many possible scenarios that could lead to specific radiative‑forcing 
characteristics (W m‑2). The term ‘pathway’ highlights that not only the long‑term concentration levels are relevant, but also the trajectory followed over time to reach these outcomes. 
In particular, RCP 8.5 represents a high‑emission scenario that leads to an increase in radiative forcing exceeding 8.5 W/m² by 2100 (source: IPCC Glossary). 
(32) The range of values is between 180‑250 USD/tCO2 (real 2024 terms). For emerging and developing economies (other than Brazil, China, India and South Africa), lower carbon price 
values are assumed, ranging between 55‑180 USD/tCO2. Ref. Table B.6, IEA ‑ World Energy Outlook 2025.

ANNEX

RESILIENCE OF THE STRATEGY TO TRANSITION SCENARIOS [ESRS 2 IRO-1] 

The Group’s strategic‑planning and investment‑selection/monitoring processes are designed to identify the actions needed to maximise 

asset value while considering the risks and opportunities associated with the energy transition. Within this framework, action and spending 

plans are regularly defined to achieve short, medium and long‑term decarbonisation targets, based on a set of base‑case assumptions 

concerning  the  speed  of  transformation  of  the  energy  system  and  the  resulting  impacts  on  prices.  Progress  towards  the  targets  is 

systematically monitored and reported. One of the tools used ex post to support management in understanding Eni’s exposure to transition 

risk is the sensitivity analysis of Oil & Gas asset values to alternative price scenarios compared with the base case. The analysis assesses 

the variability of asset values and the potential risk of distributing unrealised earnings under stress conditions, namely: (i) a linear 10% 

reduction in hydrocarbon prices over all years of projected cash flows; (ii) a one‑percentage‑point increase in the discount rate (adjusted 

WACC) used to determine the net present value of assets in each Country of operation; (iii) application of the IEA Net Zero Emissions 2050 

(NZE‑IEA 205033) scenario for hydrocarbon‑price and CO2 cost projections. The overall value‑in‑use of Oil & Gas properties calculated under 

the various stress‑test scenarios shows a margin (‘headroom’) above the carrying amounts. Under the NZE‑IEA 2050 scenario in particular, 

the headroom stands at 18%, with potential impairments of €3.4 billion. For further details on the analysis and related results, see 

 Note 

15 of the consolidated financial statements.

PHYSICAL RISKS

Physical  risks  arising  from  climate  change  may  result  from  single  (acute)  events  or  from  long‑term  (chronic)  changes  in  climatic 

phenomena. These risks may generate financial implications for companies, including direct damage to assets and indirect impacts due to 

disruptions to their own operations or those across the value chain, leading to loss of output and cash flow as well as increased restoration 

and  maintenance  costs,  including  effects  on  the  supply  chain.  To  this  end,  Eni  has  developed  a  methodology  to  assess  physical‑risk 

exposure for both its owned assets34 and the main third‑party assets within the value chain whose unavailability could affect the operability 

of Eni’s assets. For the identification and assessment of adverse climatic events and their evolution over time, Eni uses the IPCC SSP5‑8.5 

scenario ‑ the extreme scenario characterised by a temperature increase of more than 4°C by 2100 compared with preindustrial levels 

(see IPCC C8 category scenarios in   Main reference scenarios). Main physical climate related hazards35 considered by Eni include: water‑

related phenomena, such as sea‑level rise, water stress, drought, heavy precipitation (rain and hail), and coastal, river and pluvial flooding 

extreme wind related events, such as cyclones, hurricanes and typhoons; temperature‑related events, particularly wildfires; solid mass‑

related phenomena, such as landslides. With respect to the physical risks associated with the climatic events listed above, Eni conducts 

a  stress‑testing  exercise  on  its  current  asset  portfolio  over  a  long‑term  time  horizon.  This  assessment  is  carried  out  annually  and  is 

continuously  improved,  also  in  response  to  the  future  evolution  and  increasing  accuracy  of  climate‑projection  models36. The  analyses 

carried out in 2025 covered the entire Eni asset portfolio (around 900 sites). The following charts present current risk exposure (horizontal 

axis) and projected risk exposure in 2050 (vertical axis), broken down by geographical area and business line. Bubble size is proportional 

to the net carrying amount of assets in the portfolio; positive values on the vertical axis indicate an increase in long‑term risk exposure, 

whereas negative values indicate a decrease.

(33) Scenario reported in the IEA‑OECD World Energy Outlook 2025.
(34) The geographical coordinates of each Eni asset are used to assess, with site‑specific granularity, the quantitative metrics of projections for the different natural‑hazards events at 
Eni locations.
(35) Physical climate‑related hazards are identified on the basis of the classification table set out in Annex A of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/2139 and assessed in 
relation to their applicability to the various types of Eni assets.
(36) Eni uses a forward‑looking climate‑risk modelling framework provided by a leading climate‑intelligence company.

RESILIENCE OF THE STRATEGY TO TRANSITION SCENARIOS [ESRS 2 IRO-1; SBM-3]

Once the physical risks associated with Eni’s assets (inherent risk) have been identified, an assessment of existing mitigation measures or 

barriers is carried out, including both physical protections and organisational systems or procedures in place. Subsequently, the residual risk 

is evaluated for each asset. Where a high level of residual risk is identified, Eni implements different types of actions: (i) for chronic risks (e.g., 

water stress), monitoring activities are planned and carried out, with potential subsequent definition and follow‑up of an intervention plan; 

(ii)  for  acute  risks,  the  asset‑integrity  process37  is  activated,  which  may  lead  to  the  definition  and  implementation  of  an  adaptation  plan. 

The physical‑risk analyses conducted in 2025 on Eni’s operating assets and on the main assets within the value chain showed that Eni’s 

portfolio  is  overall  resilient  to  climate‑related  physical  risks.  The  main  reasons  behind  the  overall  resilience  of  Eni’s  asset  portfolio  are: 

(i) the intrinsic resilience of the assets themselves, which are already designed according to strict engineering and safety criteria to withstand 

extreme natural events; (ii) the geographical diversification of the asset portfolio.

Material opportunities 

While transition events may entail risks, they can also create opportunities whose realisation requires disciplined capital allocation and a 

rigorous strategic‑planning process. To capture such opportunities, Eni is integrating its business model with solutions linked to the energy 

transition  ‑  including  the  growth  of  renewable‑energy  production,  the  expansion  of  the  electric‑vehicle  charging‑point  network  through 

Plenitude, the development of biorefining activities through Enilive (see 

 Operating review/Enilive and Plenitude), and the development of 

renewable‑based chemicals. At the same time, Eni is implementing solutions aimed at reducing its own emissions and those of third parties, 

including the development of Carbon Capture, Utilisation and Storage (CCUS) projects (see 

 Operating review/CCS and Agri), and continues 

to invest in research and development through innovative projects focused on advanced technologies such as magnetic-confinement fusion. 

To enable the development of these opportunities, Eni has adapted its organisational structure (see   Climate governance and   Policies) in 

line with the evolution of its strategy, and has implemented a ‘satellite model’38 to reduce the financial burden associated with the growth of 

new businesses while making their market value more visible.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs 

Decarbonisation plan [ESRS 2 MDR-A; E1-1; E1-3]

DECARBONISATION TARGETS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E1-4]

Five years have passed since Eni launched its pathway towards Carbon Neutrality by 2050. During this period — in the absence of consolidated 

regulatory  guidance  on  the  approach  to  be  adopted  —  the  company  structured  its  decarbonisation  plan  through  net‑emission  reduction 

targets defined using its own methodology, consistent with major international standards and industry best practices. The actions undertaken 

in  recent  years  have  enabled  progress  in  reducing  emissions  in  line  with  the  defined  intermediate  targets  (for  performance  details,  see  

 Metrics/Entity-specific indicators - Equity). In 2024, the first year of CSRD implementation, Eni chose to present its decarbonisation pathway 

by maintaining the methodological approach adopted in previous years, within an evolving external and regulatory context. From the 2025 

reporting cycle onwards, following the consolidation of the European regulatory framework39, the decarbonisation plan and related targets 

have been recalibrated for consistencies with the new requirements and enhance comparability within the sector. Specifically, for Scope 1 

and 2 emissions, a financial control boundary is adopted (previously an equity‑share boundary), while maintaining a primary focus on the 

Upstream  sector. The  Net  Zero  targets  for  Upstream  Scope  1+2  GHG  emissions  by  2030  and  for  Eni  as  a  whole  by  2035  are  confirmed 

and are redefined as ‘Net Scope 1+2 Upstream’ and ‘Net Scope 1+2 Eni’, respectively. Scope 1+2+3 emissions include contributions from 

Scope 1 and 2 as well as Scope 3, the latter reported in accordance with the GHG Protocol classification (previously based on a Lifecycle 

methodology). The Scope 1+2+3 target is expressed exclusively in terms of emissions intensity, with the aim of reflecting the diversification 

of the energy portfolio and highlighting both emission‑mitigation actions and the development of lower carbon businesses. The Net Zero 

intensity target for 2050 is confirmed and redefined as ‘Net Intensity Scope 1+2+3’. For all targets, any residual emissions are compensated 

through the use of high‑quality carbon credits40. Additionally, baseline‑year (2018) indicator values have been recalculated to align with the 

(37) The asset integrity process is applied to Eni’s assets to ensure correct design and appropriate construction with the most suitable materials, to enforce maximum rigour in plant 
operations, and to ensure proper decommissioning, while managing residual risks in a way that safeguards people’s safety, protects the environment and preserves the company’s 
reputation.

Reporting Directive (CSRD).

boundary adopted for target setting. For details on the methodological criteria applied in reporting Eni’s Entity‑specific Net Zero measurement

•  NET  SCOPE  1+2  UPSTREAM41:  represents  the  Scope  1+2  GHG  emissions  associated  with  Eni’s  upstream  activities  —  whether  operated 

directly or by third parties – accounted for under the financial control boundary and net of carbon credits generated mainly through Natural 

Climate  Solutions  and  technological  solutions.  In  2025,  the  indicator  decreased  by  around  31%  compared  with  2024,  driven  primarily  by 

operational‑efficiency initiatives and project activities aimed at generating carbon credits. In addition, in 2025 the target of –65% versus 2018 

was exceeded, with a reduction of around –68%. The trajectory remains consistent with achieving the Net Zero Upstream target by 2030.

•  NET SCOPE 1+2 ENI42: represents the Scope 1+2 GHG emissions associated with activities operated by Eni or by third parties, accounted 

for under the financial control boundary and net of carbon credits generated mainly through Natural Climate Solutions and technological 

solutions. In 2025, the indicator decreased by around 10% compared with 2024, mainly due to operational‑management optimisation and 

project activities aimed at generating carbon credits. Compared with 2018, the indicator has decreased by around 40%, in line with the 

trajectory towards the Net Zero Eni target for 2035.

•  NET INTENSITY SCOPE 1+2+343: represents the ratio44 between Eni’s Scope 1+2+3 GHG emissions (net of carbon credits generated mainly 

through Natural Climate Solutions and technological solutions) and the energy associated with energy products. In 2050, achieving the Net 

Zero Intensity Scope 1+2+3 target will require the use of carbon credits within a limit of 10% of the baseline value of Scope 1+2+3 emissions. 

In 2025, the indicator decreased (around –0.4%) compared with 2024, driven by the lower emission intensity of the portfolio mix. Compared 

with 2018, the indicator has decreased by around 6%.

METHANE AND FLARING EMISSION REDUCTION TARGETS IN THE UPSTREAM BUSINESS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E1-4]

In the Upstream business, where natural gas plays a significant role, methane emission abatement and gas‑flaring reduction are central 

components of the decarbonisation pathway, having contributed substantially to the reduction of direct GHG emissions (Scope 1). In this 

context, as early as 2016, Eni set a target to reduce fugitive methane emissions by 80% by 2025 (vs. 2014), a target achieved well ahead of 

schedule in 2019 thanks to the systematic implementation of LDAR (Leak Detection And Repair45) campaigns on operated assets. Since 

2022, Eni has also adhered to the ‘Aiming for Zero Methane Emissions’ initiative promoted by the Oil and Gas Climate Initiative (OGCI), 

which  aims  to  minimise  methane  emissions  from  operated  assets  to  the  lowest  technically  achievable  levels  by  2030.  The  initiative 

identifies an emission‑intensity threshold of 0.2% as the current sectoral benchmark for near‑zero methane emissions. In 2025, methane‑

emission  intensity  stood  at  0.09%  (reducing  to  0.08%  when  including  operating  companies46),  confirming  a  trend  already  consolidated

achievement of the target depends on the completion of projects in Libya, expected during 2026. Eni confirms its intention to maintain 

these performance levels and to consolidate its commitment to zero routine flaring across operated assets, ensuring continuity of the 

actions undertaken and in line with the World Bank’s ‘Zero Routine Flaring by 2030’ initiative.

DECARBONISATION LEVERS [E1-1; E1-3]

The decarbonisation levers and technologies identified by Eni in its decarbonisation plan apply across the various businesses and are adopted 

and calibrated in a targeted manner, with time horizons that reflect the technological and commercial maturity of each solution. Between 

2018 and 2025, Eni implemented actions that contributed, on the one hand, to the reduction of Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions from its own 

operations‑acting primarily on flaring, methane and energy‑efficiency initiatives aimed at reducing fossil‑fuel consumption (see 

 Methane 

and  flaring  emission  reduction  targets  in  the  Upstream  business  and 

 Energy  consumption  and  energy  mix).  On  the  other  hand,  these 

actions also contributed to reducing emissions along the value chain (Scope 3), particularly through synergies between traditional activities 

and transition‑related businesses, as well as portfolio measures. Looking ahead, the initiatives planned by Eni to reduce Net Intensity Scope 

1+2+3 will be structured along multiple levers that act both on operational emissions and on the evolution of the energy portfolio. By 2030, 

a significant contribution will derive from improved operational performance, Oil & Gas portfolio actions and the evolution of the production 

mix. In particular, efficiency measures and the rationalisation of industrial assets will help reduce Scope 1 and 2 emissions. In addition, the 

progressive increase in the share of gas (including condensates) over total production (above 60% by 2030) will reduce overall emission 

intensity  compared  with  a  scenario  characterised  by  a  greater  share  of  oil,  due  to  the  lower  emission  factor  of  natural  gas.  Another  key 

contribution will stem from the progressive diversification of the portfolio towards lower carbon solutions, reducing the carbon intensity of the 

products and services offered. The development of biofuels will represent a central element of this strategy, offering an opportunity to convert 

and scale down existing traditional refining capacity. Following the conversions of the Porto Marghera (2014) and Gela (2019) biorefineries, 

the conversion project of the Livorno site was launched in 2024, with start‑up expected by the end of 2026. In 2025, Eni reached a biorefining 

capacity of 1.65 million tonnes and expects to reach 5 million tonnes by 2030, with optionality for more than 2 million tonnes of Sustainable 

Aviation  Fuel  (SAF),  thereby  contributing  to  the  decarbonisation  of  hard‑to‑abate  sectors  such  as  aviation,  maritime  transport  and  heavy 

road transport. In parallel, renewable‑energy generation developed through Plenitude will support the progressive reduction of the emission 

intensity of the energy portfolio. In 2025, Plenitude reached an installed renewable capacity of 5.8 GW, achieving an annual growth rate above 

40%, in line with expansion targets to reach 15 GW by 2030. Complementing the industrial and portfolio levers – and in line with evolving 

consumption patterns – a potential reduction in emissions is also considered through the increasing share of Oil & Gas volumes destined for

lower carbon sectors (e.g., non-energy uses47) and/or the compensation of residual emissions through high-quality carbon credits (offsets), 

primarily from Natural Climate Solutions (up to a maximum of 15 MtCO2eq.48 by 2030).

In the long-term, Eni’s pathway towards Carbon Neutrality by 2050 will continue along the same directions, with a further strengthening of 

the gas component in the production mix (around 90% by 2050), the consolidation of lower carbon solutions, and an increase in Plenitude’s 

renewable capacity to 60 GW by 2050. Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) is an additional decarbonisation lever. From the period after 

2030 onwards, as technologies advance and projects mature, CCS will help reduce operational emissions linked to energy use in Eni’s 

activities. For residual emissions, compensation and removals are envisaged through Natural Climate Solutions (NCS) and technological 

solutions. In 2050, achieving the Net Zero Intensity Scope 1+2+3 target will require the use of carbon credits within a limit of 10% of the 

baseline  value  of  Scope  1+2+3  emissions  (see 

 GHG  emissions  offsets  and  removals).  Eni  also  contributes  to  the  energy  transition 

through activities that, while not directly affecting its own emissions, support decarbonisation of third parties and hard-to-abate sectors. 

These include research and development (R&D), Plenitude’s offering of solutions and services aimed at the electrification of consumption 

and  efficiency  improvement,  and  the  development  of  CCS  projects  for  industrial  clients  (see 

  Operating  review/CCS  and  Agri).  The 

pace of this transformation and the relative contribution of each lever will depend on several variables, including market dynamics, the 

scientific and technological outlook, and the regulatory framework. The milestones of Eni decarbonisation pathway have been identified 

through  a  prioritisation  exercise  for  intervention  levers,  based  on  internal  analyses  and  on  the  main  international  scenarios  that  aim 

for carbon neutrality by 2050 in order to limit the increase in global temperature to 1.5°C by 2100. While acknowledging the limitations 

of comparing a corporate context with the global context, Eni decarbonisation plan49 – both in terms of levers and emission reduction 

targets – is substantially compatible with such scenarios (see IPCC category C1-C2 scenarios in 

 Main reference scenarios, the section 

 Decarbonisation targets).

ASSESSMENT OF LOCKED‑IN EMISSIONS [E1-1]

Locked‑in emissions represent the estimate of future GHG emissions associated with existing or planned assets and operations in the Upstream 

and Downstream, based on their technological configuration and remaining useful life. Under certain energy‑transition scenarios aligned with low 

emission decarbonisation pathways, these emissions may not be fully compatible with expected emission levels, potentially leading to economic‑

financial implications. Eni monitors potential locked‑in emissions arising from its main assets and projects, maintaining a medium‑to‑long‑term 

perspective through the Strategic Plan and assessing progress towards the pathway to Carbon Neutrality by 2050. Portfolio‑resilience analyses 

carried out as part of impairment testing have not identified any factors that would compromise the recoverability of asset carrying amounts under 

the scenarios considered. In the Upstream sector, Eni continues to adopt an exploration model based on organic growth and selective enhancement 

of discoveries. Investment selection considers both economic value and emission intensity; to help reduce the emission footprint, priority is given 

to investments in resources already in production and to exploration activities adjacent to existing assets and infrastructures. In Downstream, Eni 

aims to improve the efficiency of its operations and integrate lower carbon products into its offering, including through the conversion of traditional 

refining capacity. Eni also assesses portfolio resilience to mitigate risks associated with stranded assets and applies an internal carbon‑pricing 

mechanism to verify the consistency of new investments with decarbonisation targets (see   Transition risks).

PATENTS AND INNOVATION [ENTITY-SPECIFIC] 

Eni’s  Code  of  Ethics  assigns  a  central  role  to  innovation  (see 

  Policies)  and,  consistently,  Eni  is  committed  to  acquiring  cutting‑edge 

technological competences to support this principle. In particular, the topic of innovation is strongly linked to climate aspects and, for the 

next strategic plan, the company has set the target of maintaining 70% of R&D expenditure on areas relating to decarbonisation, circular 

economy and the energy transition.

expenditure on innovation, which includes, in addition to Research & Development expenditure, also other innovative activities, including those relating 

to High Performance Computing, Artificial Intelligence and Open Innovation, is equal to about € 460 million.

CAPITAL ALLOCATION [E1-1; E1-3] 

The shift towards a lower carbon product portfolio is supported by the steady growth of initiatives to develop new energy solutions and services,

intensity activities. In particular, over 50% of this spending was allocated to the development of biorefining and biofeedstock initiatives and renewable 

power  generation,  and  over  20%  to  Scope  1+2  emission  reduction  initiatives  (including  energy‑efficiency  measures  and  flaring‑down  projects).

GHG EMISSIONS OFFSETS AND REMOVALS [E1-7]

Eni  supports,  through  medium  and  long‑term  agreements  with  partners  and  local  authorities,  the  development  of  projects  aimed  at 

generating carbon credits in the voluntary market to compensate for residual GHG emissions that cannot otherwise be reduced, monitoring 

the quality and integrity of such credits. To achieve the 2050 Net Zero target for Net Intensity Scope 1+2+3, Eni intends to use carbon credits 

only after reducing 90‑95% of its GHG emissions. Currently, the carbon credits used by Eni to meet its Net Zero targets are generated by 

activities that reduce CO2 emissions that would otherwise be released into the atmosphere. These include Natural Climate Solutions (NCS), 

which aim to protect and sustainably manage land. Examples include REDD+53 projects, the first initiatives promoted by Eni, which support 

forest conservation, biodiversity protection and local sustainable development54. Alongside NCS initiatives, technological solutions are also 

deployed.  Among  these  there  is  the  ‘Eni  for  Clean  Cooking’  programme,  launched  in  2018,  which  promotes  the  introduction  of  improved 

cookstoves that reduce the consumption of non‑renewable biomass55, with the dual objective of improving people’s health conditions and 

contributing to forest conservation. The programme has been implemented in Congo, Mozambique, Angola, Rwanda, Tanzania, Madagascar 

and  Côte  d’Ivoire,  reaching  so  far  around  3.7  million  people56  (2.2  million  in  2025  alone),  with  the  goal  of  ensuring  access  to  safer,  more 

efficient  and  more  sustainable  cooking  systems  for  20  million  people  by  2030.  The  large‑scale  adoption  of  clean‑cooking  systems  also 

fosters the development of local entrepreneurship and economic activity (e.g., production and distribution of cookstoves). Starting in 2025, 

the programme began transitioning from improved cookstoves to more advanced technologies ‑ such as induction cookstoves in urban areas 

and pyrolytic cookstoves powered by agricultural and agroforestry residues in rural areas. This evolution will make it possible to progressively 

eliminate the use of non‑renewable biomass, significantly reducing deforestation. Eni’s strategy foresees a progressive increase in the share 

of  credits  derived  from  so‑called  Carbon  Dioxide  Removal  (CDR)  projects  ‑  activities  (NCS  or  technological  solutions)  that  remove  CO2 

directly  from  the  atmosphere,  such  as  ecosystem  restoration  or  increased  soil‑carbon  stocks  through  appropriate  agricultural  practices. 

In this context, Eni has launched the Makueni Agroforestry Carbon Project (MACP) in Kenya, promoting Sustainable Agriculture and Land 

Management (SALM)57, and the Conservation & Restoration of Classified Forests (CRCF) project in Côte d’Ivoire. The carbon credits used by 

the Group are certified under internationally recognised voluntary‑market standards, such as the Verified Carbon Standard (VCS) by Verra 

or the Gold Standard (GS). In addition, the credits are accompanied by supplementary certifications — such as the Climate, Community & 

Biodiversity Standards (CCBS) or the Sustainable Development Verified Impact Standard (SD VISta) — attesting to their socio‑environmental 

benefits (e.g., biodiversity conservation, economic development and improved living conditions for local communities). Details of the carbon 

credits retired58 in 2025 are provided below.

(52)  Over  40%  of  the  planned  expenditure  for  2026‑2030  is  aligned  with  the  EU  Taxonomy.  Compared  with  the  regulation,  the  2030  spending  forecast  differs  as  it  also  includes 
interventions carried out in joint ventures, all expenditure contributing to emission reduction (such as energy efficiency measures and routine flaring reduction), and spending supporting 
the development of Plenitude’s customer base.
(53) Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and Forest Degradation’ (REDD+), a framework defined and promoted by the United Nations.
(54) These initiatives are located in Zambia, Tanzania, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Mozambique, Mexico and Côte d’Ivoire.
(55) Non‑renewable biomass (often indicated as fNRB ‑ fraction of Non‑Renewable Biomass) refers to wood, charcoal or other biomass used as fuel and harvested unsustainably from 
forests or ecosystems, exceeding the natural regeneration capacity of vegetation.
(56) Expansion into additional Countries in Sub‑Saharan Africa and Asia is also under evaluation.
(57) Among the actions falling under the SALM category are agricultural practices that increase soil organic‑carbon content and the integration of tree species into cropping systems.
(58)  Refers  to  the  cancellation  or  retirement  of  carbon  credits  within  the  electronic  registry  where  they  are  held,  thereby  preventing  further  transfer  or  use  for  emission‑offsetting 
purposes (e.g., avoiding double counting).

METRICS [ESRS 2 MDR-M; E1-5; E1-6]

GHG Metrics (Scope 1, 2 and 3)

Eni reports its GHG emissions in line with major international standards and industry best practices. Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions are reported 

by including within the consolidation boundary: (i) subsidiaries (fully consolidated) and (ii) the proportional share of activities in joint operations 

(incorporated and unincorporated) that are consolidated, as well as activities related to mining initiatives managed by operating companies. In 

addition, for operated assets, emissions are reported at 100%. Scope 3 emissions related to the value chain are reported in accordance with the 

categories defined by the GHG Protocol and based on available sector methodologies, following a materiality assessment consistent with Eni’s 

activities. In addition to the metrics described above, Eni also reports emissions through a set of additional entity‑specific indicators used to track 

operational performance and progress along the pathway to achieving Carbon Neutrality by 2050.

 Operating review.

In 2025, Scope 1 emissions reported under the ESRS boundary amounted to 28.4 MtCO2eq., a reduction of around 9% compared with 2024. The 

decrease was mainly driven by the Exploration & Production sector (due to asset divestments in Nigeria and Congo, the implementation of gas 

valorisation projects in Congo, and actions to curb non‑routine flaring) and by the chemicals sector (associated with the industrial transformation 

plan). It should be noted that, of the consolidated total (26.2 MtCO2eq.), 3.8 MtCO2eq. (14.5%) relate to assets not operated by Eni. Scope 2 

emissions declined slightly in both the location‑based and market‑based views, due to lower electricity imports in the chemicals sector. Scope 

3 Category 11 emissions (use of sold products) amounted to approximately 183 MtCO2eq. in 2025, a slight increase (+1%) consistent with the 

rise in Upstream sold production. Considering the contribution in 2025 from LNG, renewable electricity and biofuel sales in terms of potentially 

avoided emissions60, the overall saving would be around 17.3 MtCO2eq.61. Specifically, 13.2 MtCO2eq. are associated with LNG sales, assuming 

that natural gas replaces more carbon‑intensive fossil fuels — such as oil and coal — in power generation; 2.1 MtCO2eq. derive from the sale 

of electricity generated from renewable sources, assuming displacement of the average Country grid‑mix emissions as well as from e‑mobility 

services and energy efficiency retrofitting initiatives; and 2.0 MtCO2eq. result from biofuel sales, assuming an emission saving factor of around 

80% compared with the reference fossil fuel.

(60) Avoided emissions are emissions that would have occurred if a particular action or intervention had not taken place. Certain emissions can be avoided by using a more efficient and/
or lower emission product or service (e.g., using renewable energy instead of fossil fuels ‑ see WBCSD, 2023), thereby reducing third‑party emissions.
(61) (i) LNG: ~13.2 MtCO2eq. ‑ the avoided‑emissions calculation considers the share of gas destined for the power sector in the Countries of sale. For all fossil fuels analysed (coal, oil, 
LNG), only emissions from the electricity‑generation phase are included, based on IEA (Data & Statistics, Emission Factors) and Enerdata data. (ii) Renewables: 2.1 MtCO2eq. ‑ emission 
factors were derived from IEA Emission Factors 2025. (iii) Biofuels: 2.0 MtCO2eq. ‑ the average avoided‑emission factor was calculated as the ratio between emissions associated 
with HVO biofuel volumes sold in 2025 (from sustainability certificates) and the reference fossil‑fuel value defined in the RED III Directive (94 gCO2eq./MJ). The contribution from the 
Chalmette biorefinery in Louisiana is not included.
