ANNEX

Methodological Note

Scope 1-2

In line with applicable regulatory requirements and consistent with major international standards (WBCSD/WRI GHG Protocol Initiative 

Standard and sector best practices), GHG emissions are reported for all relevant emission sources, considering the following gases: 

CO2, CH4 and N2O. Other greenhouse gases (HFCs, PFCs, SF6, NF3), based on analysis of available data and in line with Oil & Gas sector 

practice, are not considered significant as they account for approximately 0.02% of total GHG emissions. Global Warming Potentials 

(GWPs) from the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report (AR6) over a 100‑year horizon are used for conversion into CO2eq.: 29.8 for CH4 and 

273 for N2O. Scope 1 GHG emissions are classified into the following categories: (i) Combustion and process (stationary combustion, 

mobile sources and industrial‑process operations); (ii) Flaring (controlled combustion of hydrocarbons via flare); (iii) Venting (controlled 

release to the atmosphere of unburned gas, mainly CH4, during operational activities such as Oil & Gas exploration and production, 

power  generation  and  gas  transportation);  (iv)  Fugitive  emissions  (unintentional  methane  leaks  from  equipment  and  components 

such as pumps, valves, compressor seals, etc.). Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions are calculated based on measurement or, where not 

available, estimation (e.g., fuel consumed, electricity purchased, distance travelled). Depending on their physical origin, data are sourced 

from: (i) fuel‑meter readings; (ii) utility bills (e.g., electricity consumption); (iii) direct measurement (e.g., LDAR campaigns for fugitive 

emissions);  (iv)  other  methods  applied  at  specific  Eni  sites  and  facilities.  Emission  factors  are  determined  based  on  gas  chemical 

composition or derived from literature sources. Specifically: (i) for installations subject to the EU ETS, national standard parameters 

under Regulation (EU) 2018/2066 (updated for 2025) issued by the Ministry for Ecological Transition are applied for natural gas, LPG, 

refinery fuel gas, petroleum‑derived gas and flare gas; (ii) for other installations, the main references are the IPCC guidelines and the API 

Compendium of Greenhouse Gas Emissions Methodologies for the Oil and Natural Gas Industry (2009/2021) for CO2, CH4 and N2O. At 

Eni sites where LDAR programmes are in place, fugitive CH4 emissions are determined using periodic leak‑measurement campaigns. 

Measurement results are used to quantify annual emissions by applying emission factors from API, EPA (e.g., EPA Protocol No. 453) 

and  OGMP  standards.  At  sites  where  LDAR  is  not  implemented,  fugitive  emissions  are  estimated  based  on  equipment‑component 

inventories (valves, flanges, etc.) or on oil and gas production volumes, using standard emission factors (API Compendium). Biogenic 

CO2 Scope 1 emissions are reported separately; they amounted to 0.27 and 0.21 MtCO2eq. in 2024 and 2025 respectively, and derive 

from  biomass  combustion  and  biomethane  production  processes.  Scope  2  emissions  are  reported  according  to  two  approaches: 

a)  Location‑Based  ‑  based  on  the  average  electricity‑generation  mix  of  the  Country  from  which  third‑party  electricity  is  purchased 

(IEA  Emission  Factors  database);  b)  Market‑Based  ‑  based  on  supplier‑specific  electricity‑data,  considering  the  share  of  renewable 

electricity, the Country residual mix, and contractual instruments linked or unlinked to the supply contracts (in 2025, these instruments 

covered around 55% of total electricity purchases). Emissions associated with non‑renewable or non‑certified supply are calculated 

using supplier‑specific factors or the residual mix where available; otherwise, the Country electricity mix is used (AIB 2024 factors). 

Emission factors for steam purchases are derived from the API Compendium. Biogenic CO2 Scope 2 emissions are not estimated as 

they are deemed not significant. Non‑consolidated operated Scope 2 emissions amounted to 0.04 MtCO2eq. (location‑based) and 0.05 

MtCO2eq. (market‑based), as required by ESRS E1‑6 50b. 

Scope 3 

GHG emissions associated with Eni’s value chain — not accounted for as Scope 1 or Scope 2 — are reported according to the WBCSD/WRI 

GHG Protocol, the Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Standard and IPIECA methodologies. Scope 3 emissions are classified by category 

and reported following a materiality assessment consistent with Eni’s activities. For the Oil & Gas sector, the only category considered 

significant  (~92.8%  of  total)  is  the  use  of  sold  energy  products  (Category  11). These  emissions  are  estimated  using  the  Net  Volume 

Accounting criterion defined by IPIECA. The calculation applies ISPRA emission factors, includes assumptions on end‑use sold products 

applications, and is entirely based on primary activity data (specifically, sold hydrocarbon production). Other categories are not reported as 

they are not considered significant (together 7.2% of total Scope 3, equal to 196.5 MtCO2eq., of which ~0.9% Category 1, ~1.0% Category 

10 and ~3.2% Category 15). For Joint Ventures, Associates, within the value chain and outside it, only Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions are 

considered. Biogenic CO2 Scope 3 emissions amounted to approximately 3.1 and 2.9 MtCO2eq. in 2024 and 2025 respectively, linked to 

the combustion of biofuels sold and biomethane injected into the grid, calculated using DEFRA factors.

Energy consumption and energy mix

The energy efficiency interventions carried out in the year allow an effective saving of primary energy compared to baseline consumption 

of over 308 ktoe/year, deriving mainly from projects in the Upstream area (77%), with a benefit in terms of emission reduction equal 

to  over  765  kt  of  CO2eq.  Considering  also  Scope  2  emissions,  i.e.  deriving  from  purchased  electricity  and  heat,  the  net  CO2  saving 

resulting  from  energy‑saving  projects  rises  to  about  800  kt  of  CO2eq.  In  2025  Eni’s  total  energy  consumption  (equal  to  84.4  Million 

MWh) records a reduction compared to 2024, mainly due to the contraction of fossil energy consumption, in particular in Upstream, to 

boundaries changes (portfolio operations in Nigeria with a consequent fall in fuel gas consumption and in Congo, with a reduction in 

diesel consumption and a further contraction in fuel gas consumption) and to the shutdowns of Versalis plants (Brindisi and Priolo, in 

Italy). Renewable energy consumption (equal to 548,060 MWh) is overall down, since increases in purchases of electricity covered by 

guarantees of origin were offset by lower consumption of fuels from renewable sources following the shutdown of biomass boilers at 

the Versalis plant in Crescentino (Italy).

(a) The data relating to consolidated entities non operated by Eni (but by third parties) are not always reported, as they are collected with a different level of granularity compared to operated entities.
(b) Eni’s energy consumption balance is calculated as follows: (i) each of the energy carriers is converted into tonnes of oil equivalent according to the appropriate conversion factors indicated at site/company level; (ii) for each 
energy carrier Eni’s consumption is calculated as the sum of the values of production and import from companies outside Eni’s consolidation boundary, from which the values of export to companies outside Eni’s consolidation 
boundary are then subtracted (for the purposes of calculating Eni’s energy balance, data consolidation takes place excluding internal exchanges between group sites/companies); (iii) the consumption of all the individual energy 
carriers is converted into MWh and their sum represents Eni’s energy balance. The parameters considered are: (i) Total energy consumption (sum of Fossil energy consumption and Renewable energy consumption); (ii) Fossil energy 
consumption (sum of Consumption of fuel from crude oil and petroleum products, Consumption of fuel from natural gas, Consumption of fuel from other fossil resources and Consumption of electricity, heat, steam and cooling 
acquired or purchased from fossil sources); (iii) Renewable energy consumption (sum of Consumption of fuel from renewable sources, including biomass, Consumption of electricity, heat, steam and cooling acquired or purchased 
from renewable resources and Consumption of non‑combustible renewable energy, self‑produced).

ANNEX

TRANSPARENCY AND PARTNERSHIP

Transparency in the disclosure 

Eni communicates climate‑related information in compliance with regulatory sustainability‑disclosure requirements, while also referring to the 

main voluntary guidelines and best practices for climate reporting, including the OECD Guidelines for the inside‑out perspective and the TCFD 

framework for the outside‑in view. Eni contributes to the development of standards and best practices for comprehensive and effective climate 

disclosure, including through participation in international industry initiatives. In particular, adherence to the OGMP 2.0 programme strengthens 

the reliability and transparency of methane emissions reporting in the Oil & Gas sector. The quality and transparency of Eni’s methane reporting 

have  been  recognised  by  UNEP  through  the  awarding  of  the  OGMP  2.0  Gold  Standard  Reporting  level,  as  referenced  in  the  IMEO  methane 

emissions  reports  published  in  2024  and  2025.  Eni’s  climate‑disclosure  quality  and  transparency  have  also  been  positively  assessed  in  the 

Pledges to Progress 2025 report, produced by UNEP, the IEA and the Environmental Defense Fund (EDF), which evaluates actions undertaken 

by Oil & Gas companies in support of the decarbonisation commitments set out in the Oil & Gas Decarbonisation Charter (OGDC). As part of 

its continuous improvement approach, in 2024 Eni published a dedicated report62 outlining its actions to reduce methane emissions across 

all  operations. Transparency  in  climate  disclosure,  combined  with  the  company’s  decarbonisation  strategy,  has  contributed  to  Eni’s  positive 

performance in major ESG ratings and climate benchmarks (see 

 ESG Ratings and 

 Capital Market Update on Eni’s website). As part of its 

advocacy activities, Eni also shares its position on climate change and related climate strategy topics (see   Business Conduct).

Partnerships for decarbonisation 

Eni  collaborates  and  engages  with  institutions,  academia,  companies  and  international  organisations  to  foster  the  energy  transition  by 

generating and sharing knowledge, developing best practices and supporting initiatives that create value both for the company and for its 

stakeholders. Eni is a founding member of the UN Environment Programme’s Oil & Gas Methane Partnership (OGMP), the Oil and Gas Climate 

Initiative (OGCI), and actively participates in expert groups such as IPIECA and IOGP. Eni is also a signatory of the Oil & Gas Decarbonisation 

Charter63  (OGDC),  a  key  initiative  launched  at  COP28  to  guide  the  sector  towards  transparent  and  concrete  actions  to  reduce  emissions, 

including methane and flaring. To support these commitments, Eni has joined the Global Flaring and Methane Reduction (GFMR) trust fund, 

launched by the World Bank, which aims to help governments and national operators eliminate methane emissions and routine flaring by 

2030. These collaborations have contributed to the development of industry best practices for methane emission monitoring, reporting and 

verification, as well as the promotion of innovative technological solutions at sectoral level, including through the Climate Investment fund 

established by OGCI. Eni has also signed cooperation agreements with NOCs and joint venture partners, including EGAS and Sonatrach, with 

the aim of sharing its expertise in managing and reducing methane emissions. In parallel, Eni develops partnerships with energy‑intensive 

companies to promote lower carbon solutions. In this context, Eni has joined the Patto per la Decarbonizzazione del Trasporto Aereo (PACTA), 

an initiative promoted with Aeroporti di Roma that brings together institutions, sector stakeholders and third‑sector organisations to define a 

roadmap for the decarbonisation of the aviation sector by 2050. Finally, Eni collaborates with universities and start‑ups to develop innovative 

solutions in the energy field, including emerging technologies with long‑term emission reduction potential, such as magnetic‑confinement 

fusion.

Climate due diligence reconciliation framework

PHASE

DESCRIPTION

Eni states its commitment to tackling climate change in its corporate Mission and 
through various policies ‑ particularly the Code of Ethics. The Company has defined a 
decarbonisation plan to reach Carbon Neutrality by 2050, as an integral part of its corporate 
strategy and implemented via a structured Corporate Governance system. The commitment 
to tackle climate change is also embedded in management performance through the 
Remuneration Policy. Responsible conduct is promoted along the value chain and in 
advocacy activities, whose cornerstones are set out in the Supplier Code of Conduct and in 
the Assessment of Industry Associations’ Climate Policy Positions.

Eni has internal tools and processes to identify GHG emission sources.

 Material impacts

Based on this mapping, Eni develops an emissions inventory, reports emissions and 
establishes a prioritisation of mitigation actions, also taking into account the external 
climate debate.

 Policies

 Climate Governance 

To contribute to reducing the Company’s and the value chain’s GHG emissions, Eni has 
defined a pathway towards Carbon Neutrality by 2050.

 Decarbonisation Plan

 Partnership for decarbonisation

TAKING ACTIONS TO 
ADDRESS NEGATIVE 
IMPACTS

Recognising the value of collective action to tackle climate change, Eni promotes combined, 
multi-sector and global initiatives by collaborating with academia, civil society, institutions 
and businesses to identify and advance actions supporting the energy transition.

Monitoring progress against GHG net-reduction targets follows a structured cycle that 
includes strategic planning, the allocation of targets linked to management remuneration, 
and semi‑annual KPI monitoring to analyse gaps and reprioritise actions for the next cycle.

 Metrics

COMMUNICATING

Eni communicates climate-related information fully and transparently, in line with legal 
requirements on sustainability reporting, and follows the main voluntary guidelines and best 
practices on climate disclosure. Eni also conducts ongoing monitoring of developments in 
both soft-law and hard-law climate regulations to assess the robustness of its tools and any 
need for adjustment.

171

Environment and Eni’s 
management system 

IN   THIS CHAPTER:

Pollution (E2)

Water resources management (E3)

Biodiversity (E4)

Resource use and circular economy (E5)

Eni  pays  particular  attention  to  the  promotion  of  the  efficient  use  of  natural  resources,  such  as  water,  to  the  prevention  and  control  of 

pollutant emissions, to waste management, and to the protection of biodiversity and ecosystem services. Environmental matters, together 

with 

 Health and Safety, are managed through a single integrated HSE management system, which defines the roles, responsibilities and 

management procedures for all business sectors. Furthermore, to raise awareness among employees and across the supply chain on these 

aspects,  Eni  continues  a  program,  launched  in  2019,  to  strengthen  environmental  culture,  involving  all  company  levels;  in  particular,  the 

program involved operational sites in Italy and is being extended to foreign subsidiaries, also through the signing of Environmental and Safety 

Pacts,  which  engage  suppliers  in  tangible  and  measurable  improvement  actions.  In  addition,  in  2025,  activities  continued  to  support  the 

adoption of virtuous behaviours by employees and suppliers, in line with Eni’s values, commitment and standards, through the promotion of 

the Environmental Golden Rules, which have been updated to enhance their effectiveness.

HSE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM [ESRS 2 MDR-A; E2; E3; E4; E5 IRO-1]

For  the  management  of  the  environment,  health  and  safety  in  the  workplace,  Eni  has  adopted  a  three‑level  responsibility  model 

(employers,  senior  executives  at  business  area  level  and  Eni  top  management),  each  supported  by  a  dedicated  HSE  function.  In 

order  to  ensure  oversight  over  activities,  Eni,  also  for  the  purpose  of  preventing  offences  pursuant  to  Italian  Legislative  Decree  No. 

231/2001,  has  implemented  an  adequate  HSE  control  model,  consistent  with  the  corporate  structure,  organisational  levels  and  the 

system of delegated authorities and responsibilities. In line with ISO 14001:2015 certification, within the Management System each site 

carries out, in relation to its activities, a process for the identification of environmental aspects, including the assessment of potential 

associated impacts and risks, as well as the identification and monitoring of possible opportunities. The assessment process considers 

the lifecycle of assets and activities under different operating conditions (normal, abnormal and emergency). The risk64 associated with 

each environmental aspect/impact is assessed based on the technical and managerial barriers in place at the site. This assessment 

process is periodic, monitored and updated in order to ensure and improve the quality and effectiveness of the identification, analysis 

and  risk  assessment  process,  as  well  as  to  periodically  verify  its  consistency,  adequacy  and  the  effectiveness  of  the  mitigation 

measures implemented. The Internal Regulatory System establishes the classification of all Eni‑controlled entities into three HSE risk 

clusters, based on the activities carried out: (i) significant (industrial activities), for which the adoption of an HSE management system, 

the certification in accordance with ISO 14001 and ISO 45001 standards, and annual internal HSE audits are required; (ii) limited (office 

activities or activities of limited relevance), for which the adoption (but not certification) of an HSE management system and periodic 

internal HSE audits are required; (iii) absent (absence of employees and operational activities), for which no specific obligations apply. 

All  entities  classified  as  significant  risk  are  ISO  14001  and  ISO  45001  certified  or  have  planned  to  obtain  such  certifications  (at  the 

end  of  2025,  94%  of  these  had  already  achieved  ISO  45001  certification  and  93%  ISO  14001);  entities  classified  as  limited  risk  and 

required to develop an HSE management system have also already implemented it (90% in 2025) or have planned its implementation. 

In  addition  to  third‑party  audits  required  to  maintain  certifications,  further  internal  audits  are  carried  out  on  a  semi‑annual  basis  to 

verify  the  adequacy  of  the  HSE  management  system  and  regulatory  compliance.  During  the  implementation  phase  of  operational 

activities, the objective is to prevent, reduce and manage identified direct and indirect environmental risks and impacts. These impacts 

may  arise  both  from  the  specific  activities  of  production  units/organisational  structures  and  from  the  various  processes  of  design, 

(64) In this context, the term risk is not intended in terms of financial materiality risk; rather, it refers to the combination of the likelihood that a given event may occur within a specific 
period or under specific circumstances and the consequences that may arise.

development,  use  and  end‑of‑life  of  products  and  services.  The  approach  adopted  takes  into  account  all  stages  of  the  lifecycle  in 

order  to  ensure  comprehensive  management  of  environmental  aspects.  In  this  phase,  appropriate  processes  for  the  selection  and 

management of suppliers and contractors are also ensured, in accordance with Eni’s HSE requirements, including specific criteria and 

controls throughout the entire process – from qualification to contract execution. Environmental impact assessment procedures are 

shared with local stakeholders through public consultations, where required by applicable regulations and, in some cases, also on a 

voluntary basis. Inclusiveness and stakeholder engagement represent reference principles for Eni, which promotes preventive, free and 

informed consultations, considering their concerns with respect to activities, projects and development initiatives. In general, the needs 

and expectations of interested parties are assessed by Eni sites within the context analysis in accordance with ISO 14001:2015, and the 

handling of grievances is ensured through the Grievance Mechanism and the whistleblowing process (for further details see 

 Human 

Rights for Eni). The monitoring, including through HSE reviews65 and reporting, play a strategic role in maintaining the efficiency of the 

organisational system, supporting the decision‑making process and identifying areas for improvement and actions to be implemented 

in order to achieve the defined objectives. Analysis of site level information enables the identification of the most critical situations 

and the planning of specific interventions and related priorities. For the implementation of environmental activities, financial resources 

required to meet identified commitments are defined within Eni’s Strategic Plan and detailed in the relevant thematic chapters. 

POLLUTION 

[ESRS E2]

POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; E2-1]

POLICY

KEY CONTENTS:

 Code of Ethics

•  Commitment to acting sustainably, by minimizing environmental impacts.

  Internal Regulatory System

•  Principles for the operational management of environmental impacts/risks: (i) optimization of water cycle management; 

(ii) control and monitoring of water discharges; (iii) prevention, monitoring and control of pollutant emissions; (iv) 
prevention and monitoring of spills; (v) management of soil, subsoil, surface water and groundwater contamination and 
the relevant emergency safety and reclamation actions; (vi) emergency management.

•  Prevention, protection and mitigation for the conservation and safeguarding of the environment from the release of 

pollutants, through effective monitoring and control mechanisms that are periodically verified, based on the results of 
assessments conducted to identify environmental impacts.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E2-3] 

Eni adopts an Internal Regulatory System and an 

 HSE Management System that ensures the definition of operational guidelines for 

all business areas, based on an understanding of the operating context, applicable legislative obligations, environmental compliance 

requirements and stakeholder expectations. Within this framework, Eni, while not setting quantitative targets, is consistently engaged in 

implementing actions aimed at safeguarding water resources, air quality and soils through an approach focused on the prevention and 

minimisation of risks and impacts related to emissions affecting these environmental matrices. The 

 Actions required to implement 

the management system are reviewed on a semi‑annual basis, together with the analysis of   Metrics useful for the detailed monitoring 

of  performance,  enabling  an  intervention  in  case  of  deviations.  Furthermore,  Eni  ensures  compliance  with  legislative  requirements 

also  through  HSE  management  systems  certified  in  accordance  with  international  standards  and,  as  further  detailed  in  the  chapter 

dedicated to water resources, the adoption of quantitative targets on pollution is under evaluation for future Strategic Plans. In fact, 

the commitment defined in terms of water positivity (  Water Resources), in line with the principles of Positive Water Impact that Eni 

follows, intrinsically also considers the water quality dimension and therefore, where necessary, also aims at objectives for reducing 

water pollution.

(65) The HSE Review is aimed at assessing the management of HSE risks and verifying the suitability, adequacy and effectiveness of the HSE management system adopted.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3]

The material impacts and risks related to the own activities66 on pollution topic are attributable to the release of substances into air, water or 

soil associated with industrial activities in the exploration, development and production of hydrocarbons, refining and transportation of fuels 

and other flammable products, petrochemical production, as well as potential malfunctions in water treatment systems within remediation 

activities. Although Eni operates in compliance with national and local regulations and is subject to oversight by the competent authorities, 

these activities are inherently exposed to operational risks that may result in impacts on the environment and on people within Eni, as well as 

on contractors, suppliers and business partners, and local communities.

Material impacts

Air emissions from the Oil & Gas sector are a source of potential environmental impacts, particularly at local level, in terms of air quality, 

odour disturbance, photochemical smog and the phenomenon of acid rain (acidification). The main emissions, mostly related to processes at 

Upstream industrial facilities and at power generation, chemicals and refining plants, concern nitrogen oxides (NOx) and sulphur oxides (SOx), 

particulate matter (PM) and non‑methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs). The same activities are also a source of potential impacts 

related  to  water  discharges,  for  which  Eni  closely  monitors  the  presence  of  hydrocarbons  in  Upstream  produced  water  and  in  industrial 

wastewater. With regard to releases to soil, Eni’s activities do not involve operational discharges; however, unintentional releases of oil and 

other chemical products may occur as a result of accidental loss of containment, mainly associated with Upstream and refining activities, or 

unlawful acts (theft and sabotage).

Material risks and opportunities

Although effective preventive systems and good management practices are adopted, the operational risks inherent in Eni’s business activities 

may lead to the occurrence of: (i) process incidents, such as fires, explosions, spills, releases of hazardous substances, and asset integrity 

incidents;  (ii)  other  non-process-related  incidents  (such  as  those  occurring  in  road,  rail,  and  maritime  transport,  service  stations,  and  gas 

distribution networks); (iii) blowouts resulting from uncontrolled hydrocarbon flow from within the well (see 

 Risk factors and uncertainty). No 

material opportunities related to the topic of pollution have been identified.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; E2-2] 

Eni,  across  the  various  geographical  contexts  in  which  it  operates,  is  committed  to  preventing  and  minimising  environmental  impacts 

by  promoting  the  adoption  of  international  good  practices  and  Best  Available  Technologies  (BAT)67,  both  technical  and  managerial.  The 

operational management of pollution‑related impacts and risks focuses on managing pollutant emissions to air and water, preventing spills, 

and monitoring the effectiveness of the actions undertaken. Industrial facilities operate in compliance with environmental regulations and 

permit conditions and with the principles of prevention, protection and mitigation of environmental impacts, implementing actions aimed at 

the continuous improvement of environmental performance. Eni has adopted a multi‑level responsibility model and an Internal Regulatory 

System (see   Health & Safety) which provides, among other things, the adoption of management system, in higher‑HSE risk sites, certified in 

accordance with ISO 14001 and ISO 50001 standards (as well as ISO 45001 for health and safety matters). The technical audit plan and the 

internal control system adopted to prevent and minimise operational risks enable continuous verification of activities against Eni’s regulatory 

principles. Below are the actions implemented by Eni on the basis of the material impacts, risks and opportunities identified, grouped into: 

(i)  Air  emissions;  (ii)  Water  emissions;  (iii)  Soil  emissions  in  relation  to  accidental  releases  (Spills).  It  should  be  noted  that  the  actions 

implemented by Eni and described below refer to accidental releases into the environment, both onshore and offshore.

Air emissions

The  operational  model  adopted  by  Eni,  in  addition  to  ensuring  regulatory  compliance,  provides  an  approach  aimed  at  preventing  and 

reducing the risks associated with air pollution that emissions may cause, as well as their potential effects on local air quality. To this end, Eni 

systematically and continuously defines and implements actions related to:

•  monitoring and control emissions, taking into account the territorial, environmental and regulatory context in order to ensure best performance 

in  terms  of  limiting  releases  to  the  atmosphere.  Particular  attention  is  paid  to  potential  atmospheric  effects  and  odour  impacts,  through 

continuous oversight and direct control of individual emission sources. For activities subject to the European Industrial Emissions Directive 

(IED), Eni operates to ensure compliance with the requirements expressly set out in the Monitoring and Control Plan, as required by applicable 

regulations  and  in  line  with  the  application  of  specific  BAT  according  to  the  different  types  of  emissions  (channelled,  diffuse,  fugitive  and 

(66) For any other impacts on the value chain, see the chapter   Value Chain and main impacts.
(67) By way of reference, consideration is given to the documents issued by the European Commission (BREF ‑ BAT reference documents).

ENI ANNUAL REPORT 2025

--- PAGE 176 ---
174

MANAGEMENT 
REPORT

CONSOLIDATED 
FINANCIAL STATEMENTS

ANNEX

odorous); with regard to fugitive emissions, for example, monitoring and control campaigns are periodically carried out, where envisaged, at 

Eni’s operational sites, including the repair of any detected leaks (Leak Detection and Repair ‑ LDAR), ensuring continuous control of emissions; 

•  promotion  and  implementation  of  Best  Available Technologies  (BATs),  technical,  operational  and  managerial,  throughout  the  lifecycle 

of  plants,  both  during  the  design  and  the  operational  phases,  following  compliance  checks  with  the  applicable  BATs,  particularly  at  the 

European level.

It should be noted that the ongoing transformation projects related to decarbonisation and energy efficiency, further detailed in the   Climate 

Change chapter, generally also have positive effects on air pollutant emissions.

Water emissions

Eni implements continuous actions during both project development and operational phases, including:

•  systematic prevention, monitoring and control in the management of water discharges emissions, aimed at safeguarding not only the 

use of the resource but also the quality of the aquatic environment. With regard to the monitoring of discharge quality, Eni adopts internal 

pre‑alert thresholds for specific pollutants, in order to promptly initiate any corrective actions, where necessary;

•  development of water-treatment technologies and application of the BATs;

•  monitoring of total oil in produced water discharged, related to Upstream operations. 

It should be noted that projects aimed at optimising water cycles and minimising water withdrawals, described in the   Water Resources chapter, 

also entail benefits in terms of reducing pollutant releases into the aquatic environment.

Spills

With  regard  to  potential  accidental  and  unintentional  releases,  such  as  operational  spills  or  breaches  involving  oil  or  other  chemical  products, 

Eni is continuously committed to managing the risks and emergencies associated with these events, which may affect both soil and the aquatic 

environment, through actions aimed at:

•  monitoring and prevention, including actions aimed at improving the capability to detect and locate spill events through the remote pipeline 

monitoring system (technology designed and developed by Eni, known as e‑vpms®68 Eni Vibroacoustic Pipeline Monitoring System). In 2025 

the monitoring system was installed in Côte d’Ivoire on a section of an offshore field. In Italy, a programme for the implementation of the 

remote monitoring system (TPI system, Third Party Interference technology) is being carried out on all pipeline sections most vulnerable to 

breaches, damage from third parties or ground movements. For example, it is currently being implemented on the Gaeta‑Pomezia line, in 

Italy. This system is subject to technological updates of both hardware and software to continuously improve the detection of third‑party 

interference and prevent breaches. To prevent spills, in particular from storage tanks, Eni invests in a proprietary technology (e‑siam® ‑ Eni 

Structural  Integrity  Acoustic  Monitoring)  to  detect  and  locate  corrosion  phenomena  and  leaks  from  tanks  and  pipelines,  supported  by 

ongoing testing and trials. In addition, verification, monitoring and replacement activities of onshore and offshore pipelines are carried out at 

frequencies specific for each asset. For storage tanks, dedicated cleaning and inspection programmes enable repairs and, where necessary, 

tank replacement or installation of double bottoms;

•  preparedness and response, following the analysis of possible accidental scenarios and the development of the most appropriate response 

procedures.  Marine  pollution  response  capability  is  also  strengthened  through  participation  in  sector  programmes,  such  as  regional 

initiatives carried out in collaboration with IPIECA and the International Maritime Organization, including, for example, the Global Initiative 

for West, Central and Southern Africa or the Oil Spill Preparedness Regional Initiatives in the Black Sea, Caspian Sea and Central Eurasia, 

aimed at enhancing response capacity to marine pollution following potential oil spills;

•  restoration, through remediation actions, where necessary, in compliance with applicable regulations as well as with the requirements set 

by supervisory authorities. In 2025, for example, remediation with certification was completed for several service areas. In 2025, remediation 

activities were completed and certified for several service stations, including the one in Vercelli (Italy).

With  regard  to  financial  resources,  the  following  table  reports  the  expenditure  incurred  for  air  protection  and  for  spill  prevention  activities. 

Expenditure for air protection includes: (i) monitoring and control activities; (ii) initiatives69 aimed at promoting and implementing BATs. Expenditure 

for spill prevention includes costs for the purchase, installation and maintenance of advanced monitoring systems and for the improvement of 

containment, storage and transport systems. For the next five‑year period, Eni has allocated €0.2 billion for air protection measures and €0.2 billion 

for spill prevention activities.

(68) This technology enables real‑time analysis and monitoring activities on new or existing pipelines, for the transportation of both hydrocarbons and water, through an innovative 
vibro‑acoustic  wave  system  that  detects  external  actions,  such  as  attempted  breaches  or  accidental  impacts  on  pipelines,  as  well  as  flow  variations,  maximising  the  efficiency  of 
transport systems.
(69) Examples of initiatives may include: design, purchase and construction of new plants; modifications to existing plants and/or equipment for the introduction of new technologies 
aimed at reducing pollutant emissions and improving combustion processes or enabling the use of improved fuels; the maintenance and management activities of existing plants.

(a) Spill volumes are estimated by Eni’s various operating entities using specific calculation models and based on the monitored operational parameters. The volumes include the total quantity of the spill that reached the environment, 
including amounts subsequently recovered, evaporated or otherwise dispersed.
(b) Unintended spill into the environment from primary or secondary containment of crude oil, refined petroleum products or petroleum waste occurring during operational activities.

During 2025, emissions of air pollutants showed a downward trend. The decrease in SOX emissions (‑7% compared to 2024) is linked to the 

reduction of contributions from the Italian refineries of Sannazzaro, Taranto (due to planned plant shutdowns during the period) and Livorno (due 

to plant reconfiguration related to the ongoing conversion to a biorefinery) and to the lower consumption of fuel oil from Steam Cracking at the 

Dunkirk petrochemical plant. The reduction in NOX emissions (‑16% compared to 2024) and NMVOC (‑26% compared to 2024) was influenced 

by the exit from the Upstream portfolio, at the end of 2024, of assets in Nigeria, by the already mentioned conversion activities of the Livorno 

refinery and by the shutdowns of some petrochemical plants (Brindisi, Priolo, Ragusa and Grangemounth). As regards PM emissions (‑33% 

compared to 2024), in addition to the portfolio operations in Nigeria, further reductions in activities recorded in the Upstream sector contributed 

to the downward trend (in particular the decrease of emissions from non‑routine flaring in Ghana and the divestment of the M’Boundi field in 

Congo), and by those of the Sannazzaro refinery due to the introduction of new calibration procedures for the measurement of emissions. The 

content of total hydrocarbons in discharged waters was equal to about 53 tonnes, showing a significant decrease (‑50%) compared to 2024. 

The trend is mainly attributable to the lower contribution of the Venice biorefinery, where it was recorded a reduction in the average hydrocarbons 

concentration in the effluent due to targeted cleaning interventions of the sewer network and a decrease in discharged water flows, as a result 

of water optimization and saving measures implemented during the period. In 2025 the volumes spilled following operational oil spill recorded a 

further decrease (217 barrels vs. 675 in 2024, ‑68%), with a reduction also in the number of cases (9 vs. 18 in 2024) and improving performance 

both in the Upstream business and at the refineries. The events recorded in 2025 involved three Countries: Germany, for 88% of the spilled 

volumes, Italy for 11% and Tunisia for the remaining 1%; among the recorded events, the most significant event (157 barrels) occurred at a 

service station in Germany. As regards the recovery activities of the spilled volumes following the occurred events, it is specified that in some 

cases they are still in the phase of design and sharing with the competent authorities. In 2025 no oil spill events due to acts of sabotage were 

recorded, also following the divestment of assets in Nigeria where historically such events were concentrated. As regards chemical spills, they 

also recorded a decreasing trend: overall 4 events are counted in Upstream, for a total of 0.3 barrels spilled (compared to the 70 barrels of 2024).

Other pollutants listed in EU Regulation 166/2006 (E-PRTR) 

In  line  with  the  requirements  set  out  by  ESRS  E2‑4  standard,  the  annual  quantities  of  additional  air  pollutants71  emitted,  consolidated 

according to the E‑PRTR methodology, are reported below for all sites of Eni’s business sectors in Europe (upstream, refining, petrochemical, 

thermoelectric, etc.), which fall within the scope of Regulation (EC) No. 166/2006 on the European Pollutant Release and Transfer Register 

(E‑PRTR) and that have exceeded the applicable emission threshold specified in Annex II72 of the Regulation. It should be noted that Eni’s 

non‑European sites which fall outside the scope of Regulation (EC) No. 166/2006 essentially pertain to the Upstream business and carry 

out processes and operations that mainly generate pollutants from combustion processes or hydrocarbon evaporation; such pollutants are 

already included in the reporting presented in the previous table (NOX, SOX, NMVOC and PM).

(71) As set out in Annex II to Regulation (EC) No 166/2006 (E‑PRTR).
(72) The 2025 figures reported in the table refer to the best available estimates, largely based on measurements, of pollutant emissions in accordance with the E‑PRTR methodology, 
used by sites for E‑PRTR reporting pursuant to Regulation (EC) No. 166/2006. In fact, it should be noted that the E‑PRTR registers for the year 2025 will be published in line with the 
timelines set out by the applicable mandatory regulations. The figures reported in the table may therefore be subject to changes resulting from refinements of the estimated data 
following the availability of additional information. The 2024 figures have been updated compared with the previous edition of the Sustainability Statement, using the data actually 
published in the 2024 E‑PRTR Declarations, which became available after the publication of the 2024 Sustainability Statement, providing an improved estimate. For the updated 2024 
parameters, the absence of the data in the 2025 Sustainability Statement reflects that the relevant materiality threshold was not met; conversely, the disclosure of data that was not 
included in the 2024 Statement indicates that the threshold was exceeded for the data collected.

WATER RESOURCES

[ESRS E3]

POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; E3-1]

POLICY

KEY CONTENTS:

 Code of Ethics

 Eni’s position on water

•  Commitment to the fight against climate change, including innovative solutions aimed at reducing the impact of Eni’s 

operations, also through the protection of water resources.

•  Commitment to setting targets to minimise freshwater withdrawals in water‑stressed areas(a), by seeking improved 

solutions or by reducing consumption through efficiency activities and by looking for solutions outside the Company 
and its operational boundary. 

•  Commitment to periodically carry out a water risk assessment, analysing the specific areas where its operational assets 

are located, adopting an integrated and cross‑cutting approach that involves specialist functions and business lines.

•  Commitment to identifying partnerships for optimal water management in line with local needs, to promote social 
development and safeguard ecosystems, providing for the active involvement of stakeholders in its procedures.

Internal Regulatory System

•  Water resource management model defining the procedures for: (i) identifying water‑stressed areas; (ii) managing 

water withdrawals, use and discharges; (iii) identifying priority sites and related interventions; (iv) reporting and 
communication activities. These tools aim to identify withdrawals and consumption across all business sectors, to 
assess and minimise potential impacts on ecosystems and communities. The treatment, disposal or reinjection of 
water is governed by sector‑specific best practices.

•  Actions to be implemented through the application of water risk mitigation criteria: avoid, replace, reduce, recycle, 

replenish.

•  Procedures to inform and engage stakeholders, promoting preventive, free and informed consultation, in order to take 

their views into account regarding activities, new projects and development initiatives.

(a) Water stressed areas are identified using Aqueduct, a tool developed by the World Resources Institute, and are monitored annually through an internal analysis carried out down to the 
level of the individual operational site. Areas are classified as water‑stressed when their Baseline water stress value exceeds 40% (that is, when withdrawals of renewable water are greater 
than 40% of the basin’s recharge capacity). 

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E3-3]

Eni continues its journey to protect water resources. Over the years, this journey has included its endorsement of the CEO Water Mandate 

and the publication of 

 Eni’s position on water. In 2024, Eni announced its ambition to achieve water positivity74 by 2050 in its operated 

sites. This ambition is framed within an approach that also considers measures at river basin level and is inspired by the Positive Water 

Impact (PWI) principles set out by the CEO Water Mandate. Actions to safeguard water will focus on the aspects identified as most critical 

for each territory in terms of water ‘availability’, ‘quality’ and ‘accessibility’, which represent the three dimensions to be considered under the 

approach proposed by the CEO Water Mandate. Interventions will therefore be tailored to the identified needs and to the relevance of each 

operational site, giving priority to those located in highly water‑stressed basins. As an intermediate milestone along the pathway towards the 

2050 ambition, Eni is committed to achieving water positivity by 2035 in at least 30% of its sites that withdraw more than 0.5 Mm³/year of 

freshwater in areas identified as water‑stressed, based on 2023 data (priority sites)75. This commitment therefore entails identifying actions 

to safeguard water resources, whose implementation and benefits must be monitored over time. During 2024, Eni conducted a pilot study 

at one of its operated sites to assess the applicability of the PWI approach to the Oil & Gas sector. In 2025, three priority sites were analysed, 

identifying impacts and opportunities in line with PWI principles, for which feasibility assessments will now be carried out. Eni applies an 

Internal  Regulatory  System  and  an  HSE  management  system  that,  based  on  contextual  knowledge,  legal  requirements  and  stakeholder 

expectations, ensures the definition of operational guidelines and the related actions required for their implementation across all business 

areas. Progress is monitored every six months through the HSE review process and by using a set of metrics (both those reported in the 

Sustainability Statement and additional indicators monitored internally), ensuring timely corrective measures in the event of deviations from 

expected performance.

(74) Ensuring that, at river basin level, water stewardship initiatives generate greater benefits than the impacts associated with the presence of an operational site, such as those related 
to the withdrawals required for industrial processes or to the quality of water returned to the environment, in line with the Guidance issued by the 
(75) Priority sites (with withdrawals above 0.5 Mm³ in 2023), which account for over 90% of Eni’s high‑quality freshwater operated withdrawals in waterstressed areas in 2023 (baseline).

 CEO Water Mandate.

ANNEX

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3]

Material impacts

The consumption of water, specifically freshwater, associated with business activities represents a material impact for Eni. Across industrial 

operations, around 86% of the water used is seawater, while the remaining 14% is freshwater, a resource that is difficult to replace in many 

applications and whose withdrawal may affect local availability for people, fauna and flora. Freshwater withdrawals, mainly used for cooling, 

for producing demineralised water and for generating steam as an energy carrier, are attributable for approximately 80% to petrochemical 

and refining activities. Seawater, on the other hand, is primarily used for cooling and, in Upstream operations, to increase the amount of crude 

oil that can be extracted from reservoirs (Enhanced Oil Recovery ‑ EOR). It is also used for drilling activities, for which surface or underground  

brackish water can likewise be used. 

Material risks and opportunities 

The 

  Process  and  results  of  the  double  materiality  assessment  did  not  identify  any  material  risks  or  opportunities  at  Group  level. The 

assessment of exposure to water risks at asset level, as well as of related opportunities, is carried out on an annual basis as part of the 

responsible water management system (see 

 Actions taken on material IROs), taking into account the dependence of certain assets on 

water resources (see   Biodiversity).

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; E3-2]

Eni  recognises  the  importance  of  responsible  water  management  and  therefore  closely  monitors  water  withdrawals,  discharges 

and  consumption  across  all  its  operations,  also  considering  the  interest  that  all  stakeholder  categories76  have  in  this  resource. 

Eni’s  water  resource  management  model  is  based  on  identifying,  assessing  and  minimising  impacts  on  water  resources,  preventing 

adverse  and/or  unlawful  environmental  events  and  supporting  the  protection  and  enhancement  of  ecosystems.  This  process  is  an 

integral  part  of  Eni’s  broader  environmental  management  approach  across  its  operational  contexts,  in  which  all  relevant  projects  are 

subject to ESHIA77. Each year, Eni carries out – particularly with regard to freshwater – an analysis to assess the level exposure to water 

risk78 of its assets and to identify opportunities to improve water management through the planning of interventions, prioritised in line 

with each business activity. Eni also conducts regular supplier assessments through continuous monitoring of their ESG performance, 

including  aspects  related  to  water  management,  promoting  the  adoption  of  management  systems  aligned  with  leading  international 

standards. Finally, Eni’s commitment to increasing the share of produced water79 reinjected into reservoirs helps reduce withdrawals of 

saline or brackish water and enhances hydrocarbon recovery. Based on the results of water risk analyses, the main improvement actions 

are directed and planned at the most significant sites in terms of freshwater withdrawals in water‑stressed areas, namely downstream 

industrial sites located in central and southern Italy and Upstream assets in North Africa. Eni implements actions to safeguard water 

resources  through  two  main  strategies:  on  the  one  hand,  improving  efficiency  through  internal  recycling  of  freshwater;  on  the  other, 

replacing high‑quality80 freshwater sources with lower‑quality alternatives. The actions implemented by Eni on the basis of the material 

impacts identified for freshwater consumption are presented below, divided into: (i) water stress areas; and (ii) areas not at water stress.

Water stress areas

Eni implements actions to safeguard water resources in water‑stresse areas. Below are some examples of measures aimed at replacing 

high‑quality freshwater with the following types of water:

•  wastewater, consisting of a combination of civil and industrial discharges as well as rainfall collected and drained through sewer networks 

or drainage systems. Some examples of actions carried out, in Italian sites, during the year include: (i) Ravenna petrochemical hub, with 

a wastewater reuse plant, operational from 2026; (ii) Brindisi petrochemical site, with a plant for the reuse of approximately 0.4 Mm³ of 

wastewater per year, expected to be operational by 202681; (iii) Gela biorefinery, where the reuse of urban wastewater for industrial purposes 

has been increased since August 2024;

(76) For further information on community engagement, see 
(77) Environmental, Social and Health Impact Assessments.
(78)  Water  Risk:  the  potential  for  current  and  future  water‑related  challenges  to  impact  people,  the  environment,  and/or  business  viability,  arising  from  a  company’s  activities  and 
environmental and social externalities (Ipieca, ‘Water Stewardship’, 2024).
(79)  Water  associated  with  hydrocarbon  extraction,  naturally  present  in  the  reservoir,  which  may  contain  contaminants.  Once  appropriately  treated,  this  water  can  be  reused  for 
operational purposes, helping to reduce freshwater withdrawals.
(80) High‑quality freshwater refers to water sourced from groundwater, surface water, municipal supply or third‑party providers.
(81) The initiative may be subject to changes due to the ongoing industrial transformation.

 Environment and Eni’s Management System.

--- PAGE 183 ---

FINANCIAL STATEMENTS

ANNEX

181

•  remediation water, namely groundwater contaminated in sites under remediation, which requires treatment to remove pollutants before 

reuse  or  discharge.  Eni  Rewind  is  committed  to  making  treated  contaminated  groundwater  available  for  industrial  uses  through  its 

remediation facilities (Groundwater Treatment Plants), thereby contributing to the reduction of withdrawals of high‑quality freshwater. In 

several sites (e.g. Porto Torres, Priolo and Gela ‑ all sites located in Italy), the treatment of contaminated groundwater is specifically aimed 

at enabling its industrial reuse;

•  produced water, i.e. water associated with Oil & Gas production that is treated and reused within the industrial cycle. Some examples of 

actions aimed at reducing water withdrawals include: (i) Meleiha site (Agiba, Egypt), where an optimal produced water management project 

has  been  implemented,  a  reinjection  facility  was  upgraded  in  2023  and  a  new  facility  was  built  to  enable  full  reinjection  for  production 

purposes in 2025; (ii) Burun site (Turkmenistan), where an initiative to eliminate reinjection for disposal purposes was completed in October 

2024, with benefits starting to emerge in the following months;

•  desalinated water, obtained by removing salts and impurities from seawater or other high‑salinity sources. In Egypt, at the Zohr site and the 

Abu Rudeis site, desalinated water is used to minimise freshwater withdrawals.

Additional measures to safeguard water resources relate to water‑use efficiency, through projects aimed at optimising distribution networks 

in Egypt, Algeria, Cyprus, Italy, Iraq and Mozambique.

Areas not at water stress

Eni is committed to safeguarding water resources also beyond water‑stressed areas, in line with its principles. By way of example, some of 

the actions implemented at sites in Italy not located in particularly water stressed areas are reported as follows: (i) at the Enipower plant in 

Ferrera Erbognone, where an innovative water filtration system was tested, improving water use efficiency; (ii) Versalis petrochemical plant in 

Mantua, where the internal recycling of freshwater for cooling has increased; (iii) Ferrara, where Versalis and Enipower signed a Memorandum 

of Understanding with local stakeholders (May 2024), defining priority actions to reduce withdrawals from the Po River. 

The financial resources used for water resource management include: (i) monitoring and treatment of wastewater; (ii) injection and reinjection 

facilities  for  produced  water;  (iii)  supply,  desalination  and  cooling  facilities.  More  than  half  of  the  total  expenditure  for  water  resource 

management in 2025 is allocated to interventions at sites located in water‑stressed areas. For the next five‑year period, Eni has allocated €0.7 

billion for water resource management.

(a) Difference between water inflows – which include, in addition to water withdrawals, unused rainwater and any other incoming water flows such as wastewater received from third parties – and water outflows, related to 
evaporation, water incorporated into products and treatments, and uncontrolled losses. Wastewater sent to evaporation ponds or injected into deep geological formations also contributes to consumption.
(b) Water that has already been used once for industrial purposes and reused one or more times within the production cycle/site (including freshwater recirculated in closed-loop systems) before discharge, following any necessary 
treatment.
(c) Sum of seawater (water with total dissolved solids greater than or equal to 30,000 mg/l), brackish water (water with total dissolved solids between 2,000 mg/l and 30,000 mg/l) and freshwater (water with total dissolved solids 
up to 2,000 mg/l).
(d) Sum of seawater discharged and freshwater discharged or supplied to third parties, measured through flow meters. Internal procedures regulate compliance with minimum quality standards and authorised limits for each 
operational site, ensuring compliance and timely corrective action in case of exceedances.
(e) Percentage of freshwater recycled or reused compared to the sum of recycled or reused freshwater and freshwater withdrawn.
(f) Percentage of formation water associated with extracted oil and produced with it (onshore and offshore), reinjected (EOR) or injected for disposal purposes, out of the total formation water produced.

In 2025, water withdrawals decreased compared to 2024 (‑341 Mm³), driven by reductions in both seawater and freshwater withdrawals. 

Seawater withdrawals, amounting to 707 Mm³ (86% of total water withdrawals), fell by 326 Mm³ year on year, mainly due to shutdowns at 

the Italian petrochemical plants in Brindisi and Priolo (which together account for more than 60% of the reduction) and to lower production 

at the Enipower Brindisi power plant (where withdrawals decreased by more than 111 Mm³). The downward trend was further supported by 

the end of Upstream activities in Eni UK during the first months of 2025 (‑18 Mm³) and the shutdown of the Taranto refinery (‑10 Mm³) in the 

South of Italy. These effects were partly offset by higher withdrawals in Côte d’Ivoire, linked to the growth of offshore operations. Freshwater 

withdrawals  in  2025,  representing  14%  of  total  water  withdrawals  and  attributable  for  approximately  80%  to  petrochemical  and  refining 

activities, fell by more than 10% compared with 2024. This reduction is mainly due to Versalis, totalling ‑11 Mm³, of which 8 Mm³ relate to the 

Mantua site, in Italy (thanks to optimised water consumption enabled by operation at reduced loads) and 1.3 Mm³ to Porto Marghera (Italy), 

following changes in the production setup. Further contributions to the reduction in freshwater withdrawals were provided by the Sannazzaro 

refinery (more than ‑0.6 Mm³), in Italy, as a result of the stable operation of the Water Reuse plant, the divestment of onshore sites in Nigeria at 

the end of 2024 and the increased internal reuse of treated groundwater (at Eni Rewind sites). The volumes of recycled freshwater, more than 

72% of which are attributable to Versalis, decreased by 5% compared to 2024, mainly due to the general shutdown of the Ragusa plant (Italy). 

The percentage of Eni’s freshwater reuse in 2025 remained stable at 90%. In 2025, total water consumption in water stress areas accounted 

for  40% of Eni’s  overall consumption. It  is  specified that high‑quality freshwater withdrawals –  sourced from surface water,  groundwater 

and municipal water supply – carried out in these areas represented 2% of Eni’s total water withdrawals. The percentage of produced water 

reinjection increased to 56% in 2025 (51% in 2024), reflecting both higher reinjected volumes in Turkmenistan, Mexico and Italy, and lower the 

overall volumes of produced water generated during the period. This decrease is linked to the divestment of assets in Nigeria and Alaska in 

the second half of 2024 and to the end of Upstream activities in Eni UK in the early months of 2025.

BIODIVERSITY

[ESRS E4]

POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; E4-2]

POLICY

KEY CONTENTS:

 Code of Ethics

•  Implementation of innovative solutions aimed at reducing the impacts of Eni’s operations, including the protection of 

biodiversity, also in consideration of climate change.

  Eni Biodiversity and 

Ecosystem Services (BES) 

Policy

•  Technical guidelines for biodiversity risk assessment and management across global activities and decision‑making 

processes throughout the project lifecycle(a), considering potential impacts on local communities, where relevant.

•  Adoption of the mitigation hierarchy(b) to manage identified impacts, prioritising preventive over corrective measures to 

avoid net biodiversity loss or, where feasible, achieving a net gain.

•  Commitments adopted over time for the protection of areas of ecological significance.

  Eni Position on Water 

•  Close link between water resources, biodiversity and ecosystem services protection, and forest conservation.

  Eni No-Go Commitment

  Eni Position on Biomass 

•  Formal commitment not to perform Oil & Gas exploration and development activities within the boundaries of Natural 

Sites included in the UNESCO World Heritage List(c).

•  General principles to ensure the sustainability of agricultural practices, sourcing and consumption of raw materials, 
including: traceability and transparency along the supply chain; selection of suppliers meeting ESG criteria; and the 
inclusion of contractual clauses to ensure the sourcing of certified biomass(d) only, not originated from land converted 
from areas of high biodiversity value or from ecosystems providing essential ecological services, such as carbon 
capture and storage.

Internal Regulatory System

•  Processes for identifying, prioritising, managing, and monitoring biodiversity impacts and actions, in accordance with 

the HSE Integrated Management System.

(a) In joint ventures in which Eni is not the operator, it is committed to promoting with its partners the development and adoption of management best practices in line with the BES Policy.
(b) The mitigation hierarchy is an internationally recognised best practice for managing environmental risks and potential impacts, based on a sequence of actions: (i) anticipate and avoid 
impacts; (ii) minimize impacts where avoidance is not possible; (iii) restore; and (iv) offset.
(c) As of May 31th, 2019.
(d) Sustainability certification schemes recognised at the European or international level.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E4-4]

While  Eni  has  not  established  consolidated  quantitative  targets,  it  remains  committed  to  implementing  actions  aimed  at  safeguarding 

biodiversity and ecosystem services by focusing on the prevention and minimisation of risks and impacts. Biodiversity is site‑specific and 

characterised by unique features that vary significantly across geographical areas, environmental conditions of ecosystems, and ecological 

interactions. The lack of a universally recognised metric for measuring global biodiversity makes it complex to define aggregated targets 

at  the  Group  level.  For  this  reason,  Eni  applies  a  ‘site‑specific’  management  approach,  implementing,  where  necessary,  environmental 

management plans that identify targeted measures and local indicators, as described below. This approach allows the specific features of 

each environmental context to be addressed more effectively, ensuring that actions are concrete, measurable, and tailored to impacts on the 

territory.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS and OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3]

Impacts identification process

The process of identification and assessment of dependencies and impacts on biodiversity and ecosystem services (BES) related to Eni’s 

activities82 is integrated into the HSE Management System. Each site, in line with ISO 14001:2015 certification, conducts studies to identify and 

evaluate environmental impacts (EIA ‑ Environmental Impact Assessment). In addition, Eni annually maps its sites and concessions to verify 

proximity to legally protected areas and Key Biodiversity Areas (KBAs), and to assess the potential presence of priority species from the IUCN 

Red List83, in order to ensure more effective management of impacts in priority conservation areas. The screening identifies the sites with the 

(82) For any other impacts on the value chain, see 
(83) The IUCN Red List is the global database developed by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) that classifies the conservation status of species according to 
internationally recognized scientific criteria. The geographic distribution (range) of species is available through IBAT (Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool), a platform that integrates 
global data on species, protected areas and Key Biodiversity Areas.

 Value Chain and main impacts.

ANNEX

highest risk of biodiversity loss, especially those overlapping with at least one important biodiversity area, where further in‑depth studies (BES 

Assessments) are carried out, following a risk‑based prioritization, to verify the possible presence of significant unmanaged residual impacts. 

During the impact assessment process, related to EIA studies or BES assessments, Eni evaluates the possible interactions between company 

activities and the environment, considering the main global drivers of biodiversity loss, such as land and sea‑use change, overexploitation 

of natural resources, the global phenomenon of climate change, pollution, and the introduction of invasive species. This analysis defines the 

operational and environmental context, identifies sensitive BES receptors, and assesses main dependencies and both direct84 and indirect85 

impacts. The significance of biodiversity impacts is determined by combining the magnitude of the impact (e.g., pressures generated by 

the project on environmental matrices) with the sensitivity of the BES receptor involved (e.g., presence of species at risk of extinction), and 

assigning  a  significance  category.  If  significant  residual  impacts  are  identified,  they  are  managed  through  specific  measures  included  in 

the  site  environmental  management  plans  or  through  dedicated  Biodiversity  Action  Plans  (BAPs).  Local  stakeholders  are  engaged  from 

the earliest stages of a new project and throughout the project lifecycle, through required authorization processes and, where appropriate, 

voluntary initiatives, such as consultations or dedicated workshops. Consultation and collaboration with communities, indigenous peoples, 

and other local stakeholders help to understand expectations and concerns, determine how ecosystem services and biodiversity are used, 

and develop management options that address local needs.

Material impacts

In the absence of actions targeted at mitigation, Eni’s activities can cause significant negative impacts in terms of degradation or loss of 

biodiversity (habitats, ecosystems, and species) and of ecosystem services. The extent of these impacts depends on project complexity, 

the value of the natural environment, and the social context of reference. Among the most common impacts are those associated with 

changes in land (or sea) use and with the physical presence of plants and infrastructure, which can result in habitat removal, degradation, 

or  fragmentation,  with  consequences  for  the  distribution  and  abundance  of  species.  In  Oil  &  Gas  Upstream  activities  and  in  the  large‑

scale development of plants for renewable energy generation, such impacts are particularly relevant when they involve natural or semi‑

natural areas86. Moreover, wind plants can have negative impacts on vulnerable species, such as birds of prey, due to the risk of collision 

with turbines, wind blades, and distribution lines. Downstream activities instead develop predominantly in contexts already industrialized; 

therefore, the contribution to changes in land use is generally limited and does not represent to date a material impact in terms of loss of 

biodiversity.

Material risks and opportunities [E4-1]

The   Process and results of the double materiality assessment did not identify material risks (including systemic risks)87 or currently realised 

opportunities related to biodiversity. Nevertheless, Eni continues to monitor potential risks and opportunities by consulting publicly available 

scenarios, used to understand how changes in the state of nature may translate into physical or transition risks, as well as into opportunities, 

including those associated with nature‑based solutions. Physical risks are associated with ecosystem degradation and potential reduction 

of natural resources, which may affect the operability and profitability of assets. Transition risks, instead, derive from the evolution of the 

regulatory framework, environmental protection policies, and stakeholder expectations, which may result in limitations on access to natural 

resources or land use in certain geographical areas. Within this scope are also included reputational risks, connected to the perception of 

the energy sector, which could affect litigation, reputation and access to financing. In 2024, Eni conducted an internal analysis based on the 

qualitative approach outlined in the TNFD88 ‘Guidance on scenario analysis’ to assess the resilience of Eni’s strategy with respect to such 

risks. The analysis was aimed in particular at exploring transition risks linked to key dependencies on nature. Regarding physical risks, water 

represents the main dependency for some assets, as it is essential in production processes; the metrics on water withdrawals and use are 

reported in the   Water resources chapter, while resilience to physical risks associated with reduced water availability (water stress, drought) 

is analysed as part of the assessment of asset resilience to climate‑related physical risks, in 

 Climate Change chapter. The relevance of 

(84) For the identification of direct impacts, any change (potential or actual) in the state of nature caused by an Eni activity with a direct causal link (due to the physical presence of plants 
and infrastructure and related activities such as emissions, discharges, and waste) is considered.
(85) For indirect impacts, an indirect causal link is considered (for example, greater colonization of the areas surrounding operational sites may lead to increased access to natural areas 
previously inaccessible through the opening of service roads).
(86) It is an ecosystem with most processes and biodiversity intact, albeit altered by human activity (IPBES glossary).
(87) Risks arising from the collapse of the system as a whole rather than from the malfunctioning of individual components; characterised by the indirect combination of minor tipping 
points that generate severe disruptions through cascading interactions between physical and transition risks.
(88) The TNFD (Taskforce on Nature‑related Financial Disclosures) is an international initiative born to help companies and financial institutions manage risks and opportunities linked 
to nature and biodiversity.

other dependencies on natural resources, analysed in terms of transition risks, varies by business type (for example, biomass sourcing is 

particularly significant for biofuel production). In this context, the resilience analysis, conducted in 2024, focused on Eni’s direct operations 

and made reference to a publicly available scenario developed by ‘Inevitable Policy Response’ (Forecast Policy Scenario ‑ FPS89), to explore 

possible future trajectories. The scenario, developed over the 2020‑2050 time horizon, highlights a global context characterized by a growing 

awareness of the importance of safeguarding biodiversity and ecosystem services, which could lead, through new regulations, to constraints 

on  land  availability  for  bioenergy  crops  in  certain  areas  or  for  renewable  energy  installations.  Eni’s  resilience  to  such  risks  is  based  on  a 

strategy that integrates global portfolio diversification, the development of new technologies, and the adoption of circular business models. 

In the Agribusiness context, for example, the company has implemented an approach that diversifies the type of agri‑feedstock used and 

sourcing regions, and it valorises waste and residues to reduce reliance on virgin raw materials.

all mapped sites.

or production activities (wells, sealines, pipelines and onshore and offshore facilities) are present, as documented in the company GIS (Geographic Information System) geodatabase.

development or production operations (wells, sealines, pipelines and onshore and offshore facilities) are located outside the overlap area, as documented in the company GIS geodatabase. Such concessions were classified as

(j) The area (thousand hectares) represents the total extent of the operational site or concession, and not the actual area of overlap with protected areas or KBAs. The area is calculated in hectares based on the geographical 
boundaries of sites, using GIS tools and geometric analysis functions. For Plenitude wind farms, starting from 2025 reporting year, a methodological update has been introduced to improve the spatial representation of assets and 
the consistency of overlap and adjacency analyses. Under the new approach, wind turbines are modelled as curvilinear surfaces based on rotor diameter. The wind turbines have been grouped for adjacency analysis with protected 
areas. This update enables a more accurate representation of the area actually occupied by the turbines, replacing the previous modelling based on broader wind farm area boundaries; this resulted in a downward revision of surface 
values compared to the previous reporting period.

For the sites overlapping protected areas or KBAs, and for Upstream concessions overlapping with operational activities within the overlap 

area, Eni initiates BES Assessment studies, according to a risk‑based prioritisation approach and, where necessary, defines a Biodiversity 

Action Plan (BAP) to manage significant residual impacts on such areas. BAPs can also be developed for sites not overlapping with protected 

areas  or  KBAs,  if  required  by  EIA  studies,  financing  or  permitting  conditions,  or  other  management  needs.  The  BAP,  together  with  site 

Environmental Management Plans (EMP91), represents the main tool for implementing and monitoring the mitigation actions for identified

Non-operated assets

Beyond the assets operated by Eni, the company holds an interest in third‑party concessions that overlap with protected areas. These include: 

(i) in Kazakhstan, the Kashagan concession (NCOC) overlapping with the State Reserved Zone in Northern part of Caspian Sea (national 

protected area); (ii) in Egypt, four concessions92, operated through Petrobel Company, overlapping with the El Qa plain (KBA); (iii) in Iraq, the 

Zubair concession, partially overlapping with the East Hammar (national protected area and KBA), the Khor Az‑Zubayr (KBA), the Hammar 

Marsh (Ramsar site), the Khor Al‑Zubair Marshes (national protected areas) and the ‘The Ahwar of Southern Iraq: Refuge of Biodiversity and 

the Relict Landscape of the Mesopotamian Cities’ (UNESCO World Heritage Site ‑ WHS). No operational activities are conducted within the 

overlapping portions of these sites, including the WHS; (iv) in the Netherlands, the NOGAT onshore gas terminal is located in proximity to 

the ‘Waddenzee’93 (Natura 2000  site); moreover, the offshore pipeline crosses  the Friese Front  (Natura 2000  site). No significant  impacts 

on biodiversity result from activities in the Netherlands and in Iraq. In Kazakhstan, main potential impacts include changes to seabed use 

and possible disturbances to marine fauna from offshore operations. In this context, environmental monitoring and specialist studies are 

carried out, consistently with the EIA framework, to verify the effects on marine ecosystems and define corrective measures or operational 

recommendations to reduce impacts. In Egypt, Eni coordinates the implementation of a BAP within the JV, focusing on mitigating impacts 

on modified desert habitats affected by the presence of waste awaiting treatment and disposal. The main intervention involves cleaning, 

characterization, and remediation of degraded land. Ongoing assessments are also evaluating potential impacts on migratory bird species, 

which are key to the KBA status of the El Qa plain, as well as on marine habitats.

RESOURCE USE AND CIRCULAR ECONOMY 

[ESRS E5]

POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; E5-1]

POLICY

KEY CONTENTS:

 Code of Ethics

•  Commitment to the promotion of development models based on the regenerative principles of the circular economy that 
minimize the use of virgin resources and reduce waste, maximizing the recovery and valorisation of waste and residues.

  Internal Regulatory System

•  Implementing actions across its operations aimed to improve efficiency, reduce waste, maximise the recovery and 
valorisation of waste and by‑products, use secondary raw materials or renewable sources, extend the useful life of 
assets and innovate processes and products, with the broader goal of creating long‑term value for both the environment 
and society.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E5-3]

Although Eni has not adopted quantitative targets, aside from the business target relating to biorefining capacity outlined in the 

 Strategy 

section, the Company promotes the prevention of waste generation in line with the priority criteria established under EU legislation and 

ensures proper waste management in accordance with its Internal Regulatory System. Eni is also consistently engaged in implementing 

projects with a strong circular footprint (see   Actions taken on material IROs). To guide these processes, the Company adopts an Internal 

Regulatory System and an   HSE Management System that set out operational guidelines for all business areas, informed by the operating 

context, environmental compliance requirements, stakeholder expectations, and the assessment of impacts, risks and opportunities. The 

system also provides for semi‑annual monitoring of the actions required to implement these guidelines, supported by the collection of 

specific KPIs enabling effective performance monitoring and prompt intervention in the event of deviations from expected trends.
