•  occupational medicine and industrial hygiene, including: (i) research activities for the assessment, identification and control of risk factors 

that may have impacts on workers’ well-being in relation to new businesses; (ii) definition of operational procedures for the comfort and 

healthiness of indoor working environments; (iii) measurement of the healthiness of indoor working environments through sensors (140 

sensors tested at onshore operating sites in Italy and abroad);

•  healthcare assistance and medical emergency services for Eni workers and their families, in line with the findings of needs assessments 

and epidemiological, operational and legislative contexts. Programmes that were strengthened or extended include: (i) medical services and 

care for the prevention, diagnosis, treatment and management of acute and chronic conditions, for workers and, where applicable, family 

members; (ii) online psychological support service for employees in Italy and abroad (available to 80% of Eni people); (iii) Psychological 

First Aid (PFA) service available to all employees in Italy and abroad in cases of catastrophic and unexpected events; (iv) awareness-raising 

activities and specific services relating to gender-related assistance; in Italy, for example, a dedicated helpline is available for victims of 

harassment and gender-based violence; (v) a new programme to safeguard the cognitive well-being of workers over 50; (vi) a package of 

free H24 healthcare assistance services for Eni people and their family members in Italy (telemedicine, home medical services, bookings 

and medical history consultations);

•  health promotion activities, such as: (i) awareness-raising on endemic diseases, such as tuberculosis and malaria, sexually transmitted 

diseases and non-communicable diseases, such as diabetes and cardiovascular diseases, worldwide; (ii) extension to new Italian cities 

of the free biennial check-up service for oncological and cardiovascular prevention, which involved 97% of the Eni population in Italy; (iii) 

relaunch of the social assistance service in Italy.

With regard to future resources, investments planned for health-related activities for the 2026-2030 five-year period amount to approximately 

€357 million.

Health and Safety in the value chain [S2-4]

Eni is committed to improving the safety of contracted works and to the provision of specialist training and coaching services, as well as to 

providing operational HSE support to the business. In this context, the main actions carried out in 2025 concerned:

•  proactive oversight and support in the supplier improvement process, promoting management models characterised by an increasingly 

preventive culture of safety and environmental protection, promptly addressing situations identified as below standard and enhancing the 

innovative good practices identified, ensuring their sharing among contractors. The Safety and Environment Pacts (voluntary agreements 

with companies) active in 2025 covered 65 sites in Italy and 22 abroad and in 2026 will be extended not only to major energy transition 

projects (Sannazzaro de Burgondi and Priolo biorefineries, as well as the Brindisi Giga Factory), but also to additional entities abroad of 

Versalis, Enilive and to certain companies/JVs in the Global Natural Resources sector;

•  training  and  awareness-raising  activities  on  occupational  health  and  safety  with  strategic  suppliers  to  promote  the  key  principles  of 

safety culture. The programme focused on the active involvement of management, on the strengthening and monitoring of competencies, 

on the application of the ‘Stop Work Authority’ principle and on the adoption of all measures necessary to ensure safety. A webinar was 

also  delivered  to  contractors,  with  the  aim  of  promoting  proper  management  of  the  skills  of  personnel  employed  in  contracted  works 

and the correct organisation of operating teams and involved subcontractors, while at the same time fostering the sharing of sector best 

practices. Finally, on occasion of the Eni Supply Chain Awards, some best practices in the field of safety were recognised: several virtuous 

suppliers were awarded for distinguishing themselves both for operational performance at sites and for the adoption of innovative solutions 

developed also for the benefit of the entire value chain;

• risk  management  of  health  impacts  along  the  value  chain  of  the  agro-industrial  supply  chain.  Within  the  framework  of  a  global 

partnership  with  the  International  Labour  Organization  (ILO),  Eni  implements  a  structured  programme  aimed  at  strengthening 

occupational health and safety and expanding access to social protection along agribusiness value chains. In Kenya, initiatives have 

reached an advanced stage of implementation, translating analytical assessments into concrete actions at operational level. In Côte 

d’Ivoire, specific assessments made it possible to identify priority areas for improving health and safety practices and social protection 

coverage. Building on these experiences, the partnership was extended to the Republic of the Congo, where activities were launched at 

the end of 2025 and are currently focused on programme set-up and stakeholder engagement. The programme involves small and large 

farmers, aggregators, cooperatives and producers along the value chain, contributing to improved working conditions and alignment 

with international labour standards.

METRICS [ESRS 2 MDR-M; S1-14]

People safety

Eni’s internal HSE regulatory system requires all entities with employees to adopt an HSE management system. For all entities with more 

than 250 employees or carrying out industrial activities, in addition to developing a management system, certification in accordance with ISO 

45001 and ISO 14001 standards is required. Regarding Eni’s health management system, all employees and contractors are covered, also 

through specific internal procedures applied in compliance with the regulations in force in the Countries in which the Company operates. 

In 2025, the Total Recordable Injury Rate (TRIR) improved compared to 2024, both for contractors and employees; for both categories, a 

significant reduction in the number of events was recorded (44 injuries in 2025 compared to 69 for contractors in 2024 and 34 compared to 

42 in 2024 for employees). In addition, during the year there were neither fatal events nor events resulting in permanent disability. The Fatality 

Index was equal to zero for both employees and contractors.

personnel, processes, equipment, materials and tools for the execution of service/work contracts under Eni’s supervision, instructions and HSE management system. The contractor/subcontractor has its own HSE management 
system to ensure that the personnel for whom it is responsible are qualified and fit for the job, and that the processes, equipment, materials and tools provided are suitable, properly maintained and fit for the purpose of the contract.

reporting of HSE performance. Eni is responsible for verifying the overall effectiveness of the contractor’s HSE management controls, including interfaces with subcontractors, and for ensuring compatibility between Eni’s and the

Process Safety [ENTITY-SPECIFIC]

In 2025, a further decrease was recorded in the total number of Tier 1 and Tier 2111 process safety incidents, continuing the downward trend 

observed since 2018. In particular, 4 Tier 1 Process Safety Events (PSEs) and 6 Tier 2 PSEs were recorded. Half of the PSEs (50%) resulted in 

spills, 40% in gas leaks and 10% in fires/explosions.

POLICY

KEY CONTENTS:

 Code of Ethics

•  Expectation that business partners adopt socially responsible behaviour, and develop adequate ethical programmes and 
safeguards, consistent with the principles and behaviours presented in Eni’s Code of Ethics; where these expectations 
are not met, Eni reserves the right to take appropriate measures.

  ECG Policy on Respect for 

Human Rights at Eni

  Supplier Code of Conduct(a)

  Agri-feedstock Code of 
Conduct

  Eni’s Position on Conflict 
Minerals

•  Commitment to not infringing Human Rights and to address any adverse Human Rights impacts which might result 

from activities in which Eni is involved.

•  Engagement of its third parties in the prevention and mitigation of any adverse human rights impacts that their 

operations, products, or services might cause, or contribute to causing.

•  Respect for human rights across the supply chain through: (i) a dedicated supplier assessment model with a focus on 
human rights; (ii) contractual obligation to comply with the principles contained in applicable laws, guidelines and best 
practices, as well as Eni’s Code of Ethics and Supplier Code of Conduct.

•  Commitment to identify and fight human rights violations across its supply chain, including forced labour, modern 

slavery and labour exploitation.

•  Commitment by the supplier to operate with integrity, safeguarding its people and promoting the adoption of these 

principles throughout its own supply chain.

•  Commitment by the supplier to promoting the health and safety of people and to respecting workers’ rights, establishing 
fair working conditions; prohibition of child labour, forced, undeclared or compulsory  labour, human trafficking and all 
forms of modern slavery.

•  Commitment by Eni to making reporting tools available to stakeholders, including suppliers and their employees, and 
the corresponding expectation that suppliers provide their workers and the communities (with whom they interact on 
behalf of Eni) with their own remedial mechanisms.

•  Supplier commitment to respect human rights, promoting the adoption of these principles within their organisations and 

along their supply chains.

•  Objective to reduce the risk of human rights violations, including indirect risks, related to the extraction, production and 

supply of certain minerals in conflict areas of Central Africa.

Internal Regulatory System

•  Risk-based model and operational procedures for managing human rights within the procurement process.
•  Definition of priorities, targets and action plans for sustainability due diligence activities across the supply chain.

(a) The Supplier Code of Conduct is aligned with the ILO Declaration on Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work and with the principles expressed in the Code of Ethics, the ECG Policy 
Anti-Corruption, and the ECG Policy on Human Rights at Eni. 

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDRT; S2-5]

The targets set by Eni regarding respect for workers’ human rights across its value chain are part of the broader objectives related to suppliers’ 

ESG assessment and their engagement in achieving a fair and sustainable transition, as further detailed in the relevant section   Sustainable 

Supply Chain Management, while for health and safety topics please refer to the   Health & Safety section.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3; S2 SBM-3]

Workers in the value chain

Within Eni’s classification of value chain workers most closely monitored, the following groups are primarily considered: (i) workers employed 

by  Eni’s  suppliers,  particularly  those  working  at  Eni’s  operational  sites;  and  (ii)  workers  employed  by  Eni’s  business  partners112,  including 

those involved in joint ventures. In certain higher-risk geographical contexts, some workers may also be considered more vulnerable, such 

as migrant workers, workers operating in remote areas, or individuals belonging to minority groups, and therefore more exposed to potential 

risks of forced labour, modern slavery, or child labour113. 

(112) Including, for example, those involved in logistics, distribution, and sales activities.
(113) The geographical areas identified as higher risk, based on the specialized data provider (Maplecroft), are: Angola, China, Congo, Ghana, Indonesia, Iraq, Côte d’Ivoire, Kenya, Libya, 
Mozambique, Nigeria, Pakistan, Turkmenistan, Venezuela, Vietnam (source: Maplecroft).

FINANCIAL STATEMENTS

ANNEX

Material impacts

Although Eni’s activities contribute to increasing employment rate along its entire value chain, they may also be associated with negative 

impacts affecting stakeholders such as contractors, suppliers’ employees, and business partners. The presence of joint ventures or other 

business relationships in certain Countries and contexts, together with the complexity of the Group’s activities, entails the involvement114 of 

a large number of suppliers and business partners. These actors vary in nature and size and operate in Countries characterised by different 

socio economic and cultural contexts, as well as in activities and sectors that may be identified as having a higher risk of labour related 

human  rights  violations,  including  potential  impacts  in  terms  of  forced  labour  and  modern  slavery.  Furthermore,  the  outsourcing  of 

production related activities may generate negative impacts in relation to workers’ health, workplace discrimination and lack of fairness 

in treatment and opportunities, employment guarantees, adequacy of wages, non application of collective agreements, and obstacles 

to freedom of association and trade union membership affecting workers in the value chain. Impacts related to sexual harassment in 

the workplace are more likely to occur in sectors with a significant male presence and in remote work locations. According to the risk-

based model adopted by Eni (see   Human Rights for Eni), both industrial activities — such as maintenance, construction, assembly — and 

general services — such as cleaning, catering and security — have been classified as activities at high-risk of human rights violations. The 

adoption of structured due diligence in managing supplier relationships is therefore essential to prevent and mitigate potential negative 

impacts  (   Actions  taken  on  material  IROs  –  Safeguards  in  the  procurement  process).  No  widespread  or  systemic  negative  impacts 

related to procurement activities or to business relationships maintained by Eni have been identified; therefore, where they occur, impacts 

are generally linked to specific individual events. With regard to positive impacts related to the supply chain, please refer to the chapter  

 Sustainable supply chain management.

Material risks and opportunities 

At Group level, no material risks or opportunities have been identified in relation to workers in the value chain (see   Process and results of the 

double materiality assessment). The only material aspect concerns the potential risk of loss of confidentiality of sensitive information linked 

to Cybersecurity, which is addressed in the dedicated   Cybersecurity chapter.

ENGAGEMENT OF THE WORKERS IN THE VALUE CHAIN [S2 SBM-2; S2-2; S2-3]

Engagement activities involving workers in the value chain take place primarily through the supplier, as the legal entity, and are embedded 

throughout all phases of interaction with Eni — from supplier qualification to sourcing and contract execution. These activities, coordinated 

by the central procurement function, with the support of business procurement units and requesting units, include: (i) workshops and training 

on human rights and other social matters; (ii) workshops on health and safety-related topics (see   Health & Safety); (iii) training activities on 

sustainability and the energy transition (see   Sustainable supply chain management); (iv) training on anti-corruption matters (see   Business 

Conduct); (v) training on responsible supply chain management. The effectiveness of engagement activities on these topics is assessed 

through periodic evaluations of suppliers’ performance, conducted through audits and reviews, followed by monitoring the implementation 

of jointly agreed action plans to address any identified gaps. For companies considered to be more exposed to potential negative impacts 

according to the risk-based model (see   Actions taken on material IROs), onsite audits are carried out, including interviews with the workforce 

on human rights-related aspects and business conduct.

Whistleblowing mechanisms and remediation processes [S2-3] 

In line with its commitments and in compliance with international standards, Eni has established mechanisms for receiving complaints and 

reports, which are also accessible to suppliers’ workers. These mechanisms may be used in cases of alleged human rights violations115 and 

can trigger a remediation process, including through collaboration with third parties. These mechanisms are further described in the   Human 

Rights for Eni chapter. Eni also expects its suppliers to provide workers and communities with their own reporting and remedy mechanism, 

which should also be accessible on an anonymous basis.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; S2-4]

Eni defines a set of control and mitigation measures aimed at managing the risk of potential human rights violations. In order to ensure 

that actions are targeted in line with the material impacts identified, these measures are structured around several key areas: (i) safeguards 

in the procurement process; (ii) training and awareness-raising activities; (iii) health and safety of workers in the supply chain detailed in 

(114)  For  further  details  on  how  impacts  related  to  workers  in  the  value  chain  are  taken  into  account  in  defining  the  Company’s  strategy  and  business  model,  see  the  chapter  

 Stakeholder Engagement.

(115) Any confirmed cases of human rights violations are addressed in the 
remediation actions undertaken are described.

 Actions taken on material IROs chapters, where, in the event that a violation is ascertained, the related 

ENI ANNUAL REPORT 2025

--- PAGE 217 ---
MANAGEMENT 
REPORT

CONSOLIDATED 
FINANCIAL STATEMENTS

ANNEX

215

the section Action taken on Material IROs in the 

 Health & Safety chapter; (iv) the definition of a governance oversight model of the other 

business partners. All actions undertaken are part of the broader support provided to suppliers in meeting ESG requirements, by offering 

tools to support their sustainable development journey and, more generally, the competitiveness of their businesses (for actions and related 

metrics, see the   Sustainable supply chain management chapter). The main actions under each area are outlined below.

Safeguards in the procurement process 

Eni’s commitment to engaging the entire production system in a sustainable development pathway translates into concrete solutions and a 

systemic strategy characterised by openness to the market, a collaborative approach, and a strong focus on people and innovation. The focus 

on people means that particular attention to human rights and safety is not limited to direct contractual relationships, but also extends to 

the workforce of subcontractors and potential suppliers. This approach is reflected in a procurement process that includes: (i) the adoption 

by the Procurement function of transparent, impartial, consistent and non-discriminatory conduct in supplier selection, bid evaluation and 

the verification of contractually required activities; and (ii) the assessment of aspects related to individuals working for suppliers through 

the application of a dedicated model. This model is applied throughout all stages of the procurement process, from qualification to contract 

execution, and involves different safeguards and actions by all units engaged in relationships with third parties (central procurement function, 

procuring units and contract management units). It enables suppliers to be subject to a process of continuous monitoring, aimed at periodically 

assessing the effectiveness of the control measures adopted and updating the evaluations relating to each supplier. The model adopts a risk-

based approach that allows suppliers to be analysed and classified according to their potential level of risk of generating negative impacts. 

This assessment is based on: (i) the supplier’s Country risk, derived from data provider information (Maplecroft), in order to consider the 

likelihood of human rights violations; and (ii) the risk of the activities performed, assessed by considering vulnerabilities related to specific 

conditions, such as labour intensity, the level of training and skills required for tasks, and health, safety, and environmental risks. Based on 

this risk mapping, the model provides for differentiated control measures inspired by international standards such as SA8000: the higher the 

potential risk of negative impacts associated with a supplier, the more detailed the assessment and the actions undertaken. The model also 

includes the adoption of specific prevention and mitigation measures, as well as monitoring plans aimed at supporting suppliers in adopting 

and developing a culture of respect for human rights. As part of human rights due diligence, annual expenditure is incurred (relating to the 

functions and personnel involved, as well as the costs of on-site audits carried out by third parties). These costs are fully integrated into 

ordinary management and operational activities and form part of the broader structural and cross-functional costs incurred by Eni. In order 

to obtain or maintain supplier status, all companies are required to sign the Supplier Code of Conduct. When a contract is awarded, specific 

clauses are included to guarantee respect for human rights and, in higher-risk cases, a clause allowing Eni to carry out verification activities 

at the supplier’s premises. Contractual mechanisms are also included to safeguard Eni’s interests, enabling the Company to suspend and/or 

terminate the contract in the event that the contractor fails to comply with the declarations, guarantees and contractual obligations relating 

to human rights116. Regardless of the risk level associated with a supplier, due diligence checks are carried out to verify whether the supplier 

has been involved in cases of human rights violations, through the use of open sources. Periodic qualification checks are also conducted, 

based  on  performance  indicators,  document  reviews,  on-site  audits  and  dedicated  questionnaires,  in  order  to  minimise  the  likelihood  of 

violations. During the tender phase, suppliers are required to meet and demonstrate minimum human rights requirements, particularly where 

the  activities  involved  may  present  significant  risks.  During  contract  execution,  Eni  evaluates  and  monitors  suppliers  and  subcontractors 

through feedback mechanisms and documentary checks, with the aim of preventing impacts related to modern slavery and forced labour, 

child labour, wage discrimination, irregularities in social security contributions, and other issues that could negatively affect workers. Where 

critical issues are identified, improvement plans focused on human rights are established, requiring the implementation of specific corrective 

actions. Where minimum acceptability requirements are not met, suppliers may be prevented from participating in Eni tenders; in the most 

serious cases of non-compliance, the relationship with the supplier is terminated and the supplier is removed from Eni’s vendor list. As part of 

a continuous improvement approach, the Company aims to make its model increasingly accessible and replicable by the suppliers, promoting 

stronger accountability among its direct business partners and encouraging them to systematically carry out due diligence on their own third 

parties, in order to safeguard human rights throughout the supply chain. In line with this approach, during 2025 more than 1,000 human rights-

related checks were carried out on contractors and subcontractors, including both document-based reviews and on-site inspections. These 

activities also took into account reports received through the whistleblowing channel. Where shortcomings were identified, suppliers had their 

participation in Eni tenders restricted and corrective action plans were agreed to ensure alignment with human rights standards. A notable 

example of monitoring activities concerns a case identified at Eni Algeria, where in 2025 a delay in the payment of wages by a contractor 

(116) Eni has developed a set of standard human rights clauses to be included, on a risk-based approach, in the main types of contracts. It also provides support to the business in their 
negotiation.

ENI ANNUAL REPORT 2025

--- PAGE 218 ---
216

MANAGEMENT 
REPORT

CONSOLIDATED 
FINANCIAL STATEMENTS

ANNEX

was  detected.  Eni  intervened  promptly  to  ensure  that  contractual  obligations  were  fulfilled,  introducing  a  number  of  measures  including 

closer and more frequent monitoring of the contractor’s performance in coordination with the contract manager. These actions also aimed 

at strengthening the supplier’s awareness and capabilities in this area. The supplier was subsequently included in a watch list and subject to 

conditional engagement. These measures enabled the outstanding wages to be paid. As a precautionary step, Eni also launched a new tender 

process for the service, from which the contractor will be excluded due to the shortcomings identified. The model for overseeing potential 

impacts within the procurement process enables such impacts to be identified from the qualification phase through to contract award and 

throughout contract execution and provides for improvement or remediation actions where actual impacts are identified. Eni is committed to 

strengthening internal checks on subcontractors and on all entities with which it maintains business relationships, with particular attention to 

critical or high-risk contexts, adopting an increasingly rigorous approach. This effort aims to enhance the Company’s ability to identify, prevent 

and mitigate risks, while reinforcing transparency and shared responsibility across the supply chain in the short and medium-term.

Training and awareness-raising activities

Eni organises workshops, training and awareness-raising initiatives that give suppliers the opportunity to engage with experts on ESG topics, 

including issues related to respect for human rights across the supply chain. The Company also promotes awareness of its human rights 

safeguards through internal training programmes and workshops for professionals involved in supplier management, including those working 

in foreign subsidiaries. In this context, in 2025 the actions carried out included: (i) the dissemination of the ‘IPIECA: Online Labour Rights 

Training’ course for colleagues responsible for procurement in foreign subsidiaries and for their suppliers’ employees; (ii) the involvement of 

suppliers in workshops dedicated to training and awareness-raising on responsible supply chain management and due diligence activities 

on Open-es; (iii) the availability, within the Open-es platform, of a dedicated area for measuring aspects related to respect for human rights 

for Eni’s suppliers and all companies in the community (Open-es Human Rights Card). Within the platform, through an assessment process, 

companies  receive  also  feedback  on  their  positioning,  together  with  insights  and  practical  suggestions  on  actions  to  be  undertaken  for 

improvement.

Health and safety of workers in the value chain

Eni  requires  its  suppliers  to  identify  and  assess  risks  related  to  the  health  and  safety  of  their  workers,  by  implementing  appropriate 

prevention and protection measures to avoid behaviours that could cause harm to people, assets or the environment. Suppliers are expected 

to regularly update their operating procedures and use the best available technologies, in a perspective of continuous improvement. Top 

management  commitment  to  managing  workers’  health  and  safety  is  also  required,  including  ensuring  adequate  training  and  raising 

awareness on the adoption of safe working practices and behaviours. These requirements are not limited to the qualification stage but are 

also requested and verified periodically throughout the commercial relationship. When activities are carried out on Eni sites, suppliers are 

required to ensure cooperation with Eni and other suppliers, including through the proactive implementation of good operational practices, 

reporting  of  hazardous  conditions  or  actions,  and  investigation  and  sharing  of  lessons  learned  from  all  incidents.  In  addition,  personnel 

working  for  supplier  companies  on  Eni  operational  sites  are  involved  in  specific  training  and  awareness-raising  activities  related  to  HSE 

topics. For activities, metrics and measures aimed at protecting the health and safety of workers in the supply chain, please refer to the  

 Health & Safety section.

Safeguard model for other business partners

Eni also considers potential human rights impacts arising from the activities of companies in which it holds a minority interest or is not primarily 

involved  in  operational  management  (so-called  ‘non-operated’  entities),  as  well  as,  more  generally,  from  the  activities  of  third  parties.  This 

includes promoting measures to prevent, mitigate, and address such impacts. Eni expects these entities to commit to and comply with the 

principles set out in Eni’s Code of Ethics and in the ECG Policy on Respect for Human Rights in Eni, including through the inclusion of specific 

compliance obligations in relevant contracts and the exercise of leverage activities (for example, through the role of Eni representatives in joint 

ventures). Such contractual compliance provisions are defined according to a risk-based approach and typically require potential counterparties 

or partners to fulfil their obligations in accordance with key international human rights standards and guidelines, including the United Nations 

Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises, and the ILO Declaration on Fundamental 

Principles and Rights at Work. Where necessary, these clauses may also require the counterparty to include commitments on human rights in 

contracts with subcontractors and/or sub-suppliers engaged in performing the activities under the contract with Eni. These commitments must 

be equivalent to those required of the counterparty itself (‘contractual cascading’). Human rights are also integrated into due diligence processes 

for specific types of transactions, such as M&A deals and joint ventures, always following a risk-based approach. If these reviews identify human 

rights-related concerns, Eni considers the adoption of mitigation measures and the monitoring of their implementation.

LOCAL COMMUNITIES

 [ESRS S3]

POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; S3-1]

POLICY

KEY CONTENTS:

 Code of Ethics

•  Support for the development of host Countries, including through strategic alliances with internationally recognized 

partners.

•  Promotion of continuous and transparent consultation to ensure that local communities are duly informed and that their 

expectations are taken into consideration in Eni’s activities.

•  Respect for the rights of communities by recognizing and enhancing their culture, lifestyles, institutions, ties with the 

land of origin and development models.

  ECG Policy on Respect for 

Human Rights in Eni

•  Respect for the rights of local communities, with particular reference to biodiversity, environmental protection (including 
so-called ‘culturally sensitive’ areas), the rights to ownership and use of land and natural resources (‘Land Grabbing’ is 
not tolerated), the right to water and the right to the enjoyment of the highest attainable standard of physical and mental 
health.

•  Special attention to the rights of vulnerable groups, with a particular focus on national or ethnic minorities, religious and 

language minorities, people with disabilities, migrant workers and their families, children, women and girls.

•  Respect for the rights of indigenous peoples. 
•  Involvement of communities through free, prior, and informed consultations, with particular attention to the presence of 

vulnerable groups. 

•  Commitment to avoiding the resettlement of communities and, where this cannot be avoided, to carrying out 

consultations to reach joint agreements, fair compensation and improvements to living conditions.

•  Commitment to respecting human rights within security activities aimed at protecting its people and assets from any 

threat arising from third parties’ criminal conduct that could cause direct or indirect damage.

Internal Regulatory System

•  Model for supporting local development structured into various sub-processes: understanding the context, integrating 
sustainability and health into the business, identifying needs and expectations and developing initiatives, monitoring, 
evaluation, and reporting.

•  Commitment and operational procedures for health impact assessments and for community health projects.
•  Department responsible for local development aspects, engagement with local communities and stakeholders, and the 

assessment and management of social impacts.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; S3-5]

The  targets  and  commitments  relating  to  local  communities  are  grounded  in  the  principles  set  out  in  the  relevant  Policies.  They  are 

defined  through  a  bottom  up  approach,  bringing  together  individual  initiatives  and  grouping  them  by  specific  indicators  for  each  area 

of intervention, in line with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals. Monitoring is carried out internally on a quarterly basis, 

tracking the progress of initiatives through the Stakeholder Management System117. Annual results are published in Eni’s sustainability 

reporting, including at the local level where available. Both external and internal evaluations are carried out halfway through and at the end 

of each cycle. Their purpose is to assess the efficiency and effectiveness of the initiatives, identify best practices and lessons learned, 

involving the key stakeholders in the process. The targets listed below, grouped by Eni’s priority areas of intervention (access to energy, 

economic diversification, education and vocational training, access to water and sanitation services, health, and life on land) were defined 

through direct engagement with stakeholders. For initiatives related to healthcare services, local health authorities were also involved in 

the process.

(117) A management tool designed to track relationships with stakeholders and to monitor project progress and results.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3; S3 SBM-3]

Local communities of reference 

The communities potentially affected by material impacts are identified before any business activity begins in which Eni acts as operator. 

The  same  approach  applies  to  certain  joint  ventures  where  Eni  plays  a  significant  role  in  managing  local  stakeholders. This  assessment 

considers both communities located within Eni’s operational areas and those designated by national governments, for example in offshore 

development  zones. These  communities  may  also  be  identified  outside  the  defined  area  of  influence118,  taking  into  account  agreements 

with the host Country and the priorities set out in National Development Plans. Particular attention is given to vulnerable groups — including 

children, women, national and ethnic minorities, migrant workers and indigenous peoples — for whom specific assessments are carried out 

through inclusive consultation processes.

Material impacts 

Eni promotes the development of local communities, with particular attention to the local entrepreneurship and to community health, and is 

committed to creating long-term value by transferring its know-how and expertise to local partners (an approach known as ‘Dual Flag’ model). 

This is also achieved by developing local supply chains to enhance the competitiveness of local enterprises, engaging local workers and 

transferring skills, knowledge and development programmes that support economic growth and diversification. At the same time, hydrocarbon 

exploration and production activities can, by their nature, generate potential negative impacts on the human rights of local communities. 

These may relate to: (i) the impairment of land or water rights, due to the need for land for business activities (such as exploration, extraction 

and  the  development  of  transport  and  distribution  infrastructure)  which  may  at  times  require  the  temporary  or  permanent  relocation  of 

communities or restrict access to certain natural resources or means of livelihood, including for indigenous peoples; (ii) the health of local 

communities, which could be affected by increased pressure on local healthcare services during the construction phases of facilities, also 

due to the temporary rise in the number of people present in the area, as well as by a heightened risk of infectious diseases, such as malaria, 

or  of  sexually  transmitted  diseases;  (iii)  gender-based  violence  and  harassment,  arising  from  the  predominance  of  male  workers  in  the 

sector and particularly in projects that involve a substantial influx of workers from different communities; (iv) violations of personal freedoms 

resulting from measures adopted by security forces stationed to safeguard operational sites, affecting communities, individual citizens or 

Human Rights Defenders, including in fragile and conflict affected contexts.

(118) Scope of analysis for impact assessment studies carried out during the preliminary phases of business activities.

219

Material risks and opportunities 

Eni’s  activities  entail  a  number  of  operational  risks  that  may  generate  potential  impacts  not  only  on  its  workforce  and  infrastructure,  but 

also on local communities, both directly and indirectly. Material risks119 include major process and asset integrity incidents, such as fires or 

explosions, including blowouts, spills, releases of hazardous substances and other industrial events, as well as incidents not strictly related 

to  process  activities  (for  example  transportation  services,  fuel  stations  or  gas  distribution  networks),  all  of  which  may  negatively  affect 

communities  and  ultimately  have  reputational  and  economic  repercussions  for  Eni.  Also  relevant  for  business  operations  is  the  risk  of  a 

potential negative perception of Eni among local stakeholders. Continuous dialogue and engagement with local stakeholders, together with 

collaboration with civil society organizations and public institutions, enable Eni to appropriately manage these risks and to seize opportunities 

to access new business activities in synergy with the territory.

COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT [S3 SBM-2; S3-2; S3-3]

When operating in diverse socio economic contexts, it is essential to understand stakeholders’ expectations and share key decisions in 

order to build relationships based on mutual trust, identify actual, potential or perceived impacts, and determine the most effective forms 

of engagement. Understanding the broader context, including cultural aspects, makes it possible to design appropriate access channels 

and adopt the most suitable approaches for dialogue, information sharing and the management of any conflicts. Eni and its subsidiaries 

carry out engagement with local communities120, including indigenous peoples and vulnerable groups, through free, prior and informed 

consultations. Responsibility for these consultations lies with the local Managing Director, supported by the Sustainability unit at both 

local and central level. In some contexts, dedicated liaison figures are also appointed to maintain ongoing relationships, including through 

periodic consultations throughout the various phases of business activities. In the event of economic or physical resettlement, specific 

meetings  are  held  to  provide  transparent  and  comprehensive  information  to  the  communities  concerned,  with  particular  attention  to 

people in vulnerable conditions. For every new business development initiative, engagement takes place through public hearings open 

to local communities (where not prohibited by national regulations), ensuring the active participation of authorities (including indigenous 

authorities) and local representatives. This process ensures that communities are properly informed about business developments and able 

to provide feedback. Engagement does not end with the permitting phase but continues throughout the entire project lifecycle, including 

construction  and  operations.  Depending  on  the  operating  context,  consultations  are  carried  out  through  information  sessions,  focus 

groups, sharing of reports and updates, with periodic communication on project progress and awareness campaigns on health-related 

issues. Where relevant, Eni also identifies women’s associations active in the areas where it operates, involving them in consultations or 

proposing cooperation within project activities.

Whistleblowing mechanisms and remediation processes – Grievance Mechanism

In line with its commitments and in accordance with international standards, Eni has established mechanisms for receiving complaints and 

whistleblowing reports that can be used in cases of alleged human rights violations121, thereby activating an appropriate remedy process. 

These mechanisms are further detailed in the chapter 

 Human Rights for Eni. Among the various channels available, Eni has defined and 

applies guiding principles for the management of Grievance Mechanisms, for which operational responsibility lies with all subsidiaries. These 

entities analyse the grievances and agree the resolution with the complainants, whether individuals or communities. Any request or complaint 

received is handled and monitored until closure through agreements with the parties concerned and a response is provided even when the 

issue is not directly related to Eni’s activities. Grievances may be submitted through online channels (including a dedicated e mail address 

and  the  institutional  websites  of  subsidiaries),  or  physically  at  administrative/operational  offices  or  via  collection  boxes  located  in  areas 

affected by the project. Eni prohibits and acts to prevent any form of retaliation against workers and other stakeholders who raise concerns. 

As set out in the 

 ECG Policy on Respect for Human Rights in Eni, Eni does not tolerate or encourage threats, intimidation, retaliations or 

attacks (whether physical or legal) against human rights defenders and other Stakeholders and actively seeks dialogue and engagement 

with  them.  All  grievances  received,  assessed  and  managed by  subsidiaries  are  tracked in  the  Stakeholder Management  System,  the  tool 

used to map relationships with stakeholders. Grievances are classified by topic and relevance, and the rate of resolution is monitored. The 

system also tracks timeliness of management, trends in recurring issues and potential escalation towards disputes, as well as any critical 

stakeholder concerns that may require adjustments in the engagement strategy. Confidentiality regarding the content of each grievance is

safeguarded through measures designed to protect the complainant and the identity of the reported individuals, without prejudice to any 

party. To ensure the mechanism is effective and robust, the access modalities by complainants are assessed in each context, including any 

language-related implications and the possible need for support when submitting a grievance. The review also considers how the mechanism 

is communicated and whether stakeholders receive clear, adequate information on how it works. Furthermore, once the proposed resolution 

has been approved, Eni ensures that it is communicated to and discussed with the complainant, inviting observations or alternative solutions 

and ensuring that all steps are duly recorded and archived. If the complainant is not satisfied, Eni evaluates the reasons and, where necessary, 

activates  the  examination  and  response  process,  including  the  potential  involvement  of  third  parties.  In  relevant  Countries,  Eni  conducts 

quarterly reviews on the status of grievances, monitoring specific indicators. To strengthen confidence in the mechanism, the Eni assesses: 

(i) the most effective forms of communication; (ii) how to enhance stakeholders’ understanding of how to access and use the mechanism; 

(iii) the need for support when submitting a grievance, including through regular dialogue with local communities; (iv) whether and how the 

results of these indicators should be made accessible to communities.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; S3-4]

Starting from the analysis of the local socio-economic context, including the global Multidimensional Poverty Index122, Eni adopts tools and 

methodologies to identify and manage potential impacts, both negative and positive, direct and indirect, including those related to human 

rights. This assessment begins in the earliest stages of project design, with the objective of preventing, mitigating or remedying negative 

impacts, and of promoting the development of local communities in the case of positive impacts. Below are the actions implemented by Eni 

based on the material impacts, risks and opportunities identified, grouped as follows: (i) environmental, social and health impact assessment 

(ESHIA) studies; (ii) Eni’s management system for human rights; (iii) management of any resettlement and related compensation measures; 

(iv) local development projects; (v) security activities.

Environmental Social and Health Impact Assessment

In  addition  to  meeting  the  mandatory  requirements  for  environmental  authorization  in  the  Countries  where  it  operates,  Eni  conducts 

Environmental,  Social  and  Health  Impact  Assessments  (ESHIA)123  and  Health  Impact  Assessments  (HIA)124.  These  assessments  ensure 

compliance with recognized international standards125 and secure stakeholder engagement in order to protect their interests, identify critical 

issues, assess potential impacts and implement appropriate mitigation measures. In 2025, with the objective of evaluating potential health 

impacts on the communities involved, Eni completed 11 studies: 7 integrated into ESHIA processes in Oman, Indonesia and Cyprus, and 4 

specific health studies, of which 3 in Italy and 1 in Côte d’Ivoire. For each ESHIA, an Environmental and Social Management Plan is prepared. 

The Plan incorporates elements related to the respect for human rights and sets out the measures to mitigate such impacts throughout the 

project lifecycle. It is shared with the relevant authorities to support progress monitoring. At the end of the construction phase, compliance 

with project documentation, including environmental and social aspects, is verified and any deviations lead to the identification of corrective 

actions. Health impact assessments are either integrated into the ESHIA or conducted separately through HIA/VIS. If potential health impacts 

resulting from operational activities are identified, the outcome is communicated to the relevant stakeholders in accordance with applicable 

local legislation. A Mitigation and Monitoring Plan is then developed to ensure the proper management of any significant impacts and to 

provide for periodic monitoring of progress. In some Countries, such as Australia and Kenya, Eni operates in areas inhabited by indigenous 

peoples or tribal groups. In these contexts, Eni has adopted specific policies and procedures to protect their rights, tangible and intangible 

cultural heritage, traditions, lifestyles, institutions, ties to ancestral lands and development models, in line with international standards. These 

measures also promote their free, prior and informed consultation.

(122) The Global Multidimensional Poverty Index, developed in 2010 by the Human Development Report Office of UNDP, is an international measure of acute poverty, covering over 100 
developing Countries and integrating traditional monetary poverty measures with three other fundamental dimensions: health, education, and living standards.
(123) Through ESHIA studies, conducted before the start of business activities, the following are defined: the Area of Influence, i.e., the area within which project activities may potentially 
affect resources/receptors and where potential impacts (both direct and indirect) must be assessed, and the Study Area, which must be analysed to properly understand and characterize 
the reference scenario. These studies take into account the specific characteristics of each business activity. As part of the process, communities living or working near the operations, 
as well as those located within the Areas of Influence, are mapped and identified.
(124) The Health Impact Assessment (HIA) is a decision-support tool used to evaluate the potential health effects of industrial projects on the affected populations. It is applied, where 
relevant, to national initiatives, in line with the reference methodological framework and in integration with environmental studies. The analysis is based on available epidemiological 
evidence and is conducted with reference to the area affected by the project activities and the potentially involved communities, using available institutional and local data and information.
(125) Examples of references include UNGPs, OECD Guidelines, IFC Performance Standards, and methodologies defined by IPIECA.

Eni’s management system for human rights

Eni’s commitment to preventing potential negative impact on human rights arising from industrial projects is implemented through a structured 

due-diligence approach (see   Human Rights for Eni) and the application of a risk-based model. This model draws on contextual elements, 

such as risk indices provided by Verisk Maplecroft, and on project-specific characteristics, in order to classify business activities according 

to their potential human rights risk and to identify the appropriate management measures. Projects with higher risk levels undergo dedicated 

Human  Rights  Impact  Assessments  (HRIA)  to  identify  and  evaluate  potential  impacts,  including  through  engagement  with  right  holders, 

and to develop recommendations that are then translated into prevention and management measures within Action Plans. During the year, 

implementation and monitoring activities continued for the Action Plans (available on 

 Eni’s website) associated with HRIA or Human Rights 

Risk Analyses (HRRA) conducted in previous years. Moreover, in 2025 Eni advanced two projects launched in 2024 following the update of the 

Salient Issues: one focused on biomass trading activities and one on agri-feedstock initiatives for the production of vegetable oils intended 

for biofuel manufacturing. Although both business areas offer significant opportunities for agricultural development, given their reliance on 

the  cultivation  of  biomass,  they  are  also  exposed  to  potential  negative  impacts,  particularly  regarding  the  working  conditions  of  farmers 

in the supply chain and the implications for communities related to land use. With regard to biomass trading, in 2025 Eni implemented an 

Action Plan aimed at strengthening certain responsible-sourcing safeguards126. For agri-feedstock initiatives, a dedicated framework was 

established to govern this new area of activity through a set of cross-cutting measures — such as conducting specific impact assessments 

covering the entire supply chain, providing training and awareness-raising initiatives for Eni’s counterparties and supply-chain actors, and 

reinforcing the criteria for screening and selecting commercial counterparties. The framework is designed to be applied in all Countries where 

these projects are launched. Throughout the year, Eni supported its subsidiaries in the initial implementation of the framework, particularly 

in Kenya, the Republic of the Congo, Angola and Mozambique. This first application will offer valuable insights into which aspects of the 

framework may need to be refined or strengthened depending on contextual factors and the specificities of the Countries involved.

Management of any resettlement and related compensation procedures

The development of projects for the use of natural resources may require acquiring and/or using land from local communities. For all 

individuals who live or carry out activities within Eni’s operational areas, the adoption of fair, transparent and sustainable compensation 

measures is ensured (applying the international standard on involuntary resettlement)127, with the aim of minimising any loss of assets 

that could compromise income sources or livelihoods, even when local legislation does not provide adequate remedies for the impacted 

communities (Project Affected People). In 2025, no cases of economic or physical displacement occurred128. It is also specified that each 

action plan includes a monitoring plan, followed by an intermediate and a final evaluation to measure the effectiveness of the actions. 

It should also be noted that each action plan includes a monitoring plan, followed by an interim and a final evaluation to measure the 

effectiveness of the actions.

Local development projects

Alliances for Development are one of the five levers of Eni’s 

 Business Model. Eni’s commitment to generating positive impacts on local 

communities  through  local  development  projects  is  implemented  through  a  five-step  approach:  (i)  understanding  the  Country’s  socio-

economic, health, environmental and cultural context; (ii) engaging local stakeholders by analysing their requests (and any grievances), 

understanding local needs129 and expectations, and building mutual trust; (iii) analysing and mitigating the potential impacts of activities 

on  the  environment,  health  and  people,  including  human  rights,  to  identify  critical  issues,  opportunities  and  risks;  (iv)  defining  and 

implementing  Local  Development  Programmes  that  are  consistent  with  National  Development  Plans,  the  UN  2030  Agenda  and  local 

needs  assessments;  and  (v)  evaluating  and  measuring  the  local  development  generated  through  dedicated  tools  and  methodologies, 

such as the Eni Local Content Evaluation (ELCE)130 and the Logical Framework Approach (LFA)131. In this context, Eni works with numerous 

national and international institutions, cooperation agencies and local stakeholders. These collaborations help identify the most relevant 

interventions for addressing community needs and supporting their development. Among the main partnerships launched or strengthened 

(126) In addition to expanding checks on the working conditions of the crews of the vessels used for shipping activities.
(127)  IFC  PS5  -  International  Finance  Corporation:  international  guidelines  for  responsibly  managing  environmental  and  social  risks.  With  regard  to  any  economic  and  physical 
displacement related to temporary or permanent involuntary resettlement, Eni uses IFC Performance Standard 5 as a reference.
(128) The activities examined in this chapter are those directly managed by Eni on its operated assets. Therefore, resettlement operations carried out for business projects in which Eni 
holds an equity interest but are managed by a third-party operator, such as the activities conducted in 2025 in the Rovuma LNG project in Mozambique, are not covered.
(129) For further details on expectations and stakeholder engagement, please refer to the chapter 
(130) Eni Model, validated by the University Politecnico di Milano, that enables the quantification of the impacts of its activities on the host Country, measuring the benefits generated for 
the economy, society and local communities over the entire lifecycle of a project.
(131) Methodological approach used to plan, manage, monitor and evaluate initiatives or programmes/projects, and to define objectives and the actions to be taken. The core component 
of the LFA, known as the ‘Logframe Matrix‘, outlines the logic of the intervention. It sets out objectives, expected results and actions, while also considering risks and external conditions 
that could affect the implementation and outcomes of the planned activities.

in  2025  were  those  with  United  Nations  organisations  (UNIDO,  UNESCO,  IOM,  ILO),  national  cooperation  agencies  (AICS),  civil  society 

organisations (ADPP, AVSI, Banco Alimentare, Medici con l’Africa CUAMM, AISPO, EFCA – Eurasia Foundation of Central Asia, Elsewedy

Specific  tools  and  actions  are  used  to  identify  potential  impacts,  enhancing  positive  ones  and  preventing  negative  ones,  supported  by 

dedicated training for local sustainability teams. For the priority intervention areas identified, Eni implements specific actions: (i) community 

health: support for maternal and child health, strengthening of primary healthcare, prevention of communicable and non-communicable 

diseases, nutritional support, access to safe water and sanitation, as well as support for medical emergencies; (ii) education: restoration 

or construction of school buildings, distribution of materials, awareness campaigns on school attendance, training for teaching staff, and 

support for access to education at primary, secondary, vocational and university level; (iii) access to water: construction of wells, treatment 

systems,  strengthening  of  water  networks  and  distribution,  provision  of  sanitation  facilities,  educational  programmes;  (iv)  economic 

diversification: micro-entrepreneurship and job placement projects, entrepreneurial and vocational training programmes, mentoring and 

advisory programmes for small businesses and start-ups; (v) life on land: support and awareness-raising on waste management, tree 

replanting, biodiversity conservation, awareness campaigns; (vi) access to energy: development of micro-grids in rural areas, supply and 

installation of electrical components or solar panels, construction of transmission lines and connection to the national grid; support for 

access to improved and certified cooking systems, awareness activities on energy efficiency.

Security activities [ENTITY-SPECIFIC]

Eni  manages  its  security  operations  in  compliance  with  international  principles,  including  those  set  out  by  the  Voluntary  Principles  on 

Security and Human Rights promoted by the Voluntary Principles Initiative132 (VPI), and proactively collaborates with its Business Partners 

to ensure that such activities, carried out in synergy with and/or in the interest of Eni, are managed in full respect of human rights and 

fundamental freedoms of individuals. Eni has been a ‘full member’ of the VPI since 2022 and, in 2025, within the framework of human rights, 

carried out a series of actions aimed both at confirming its commitment and at increasing awareness and sensitivity in managing potential 

impacts on the communities where it is the operator. Since 2009, Eni has promoted a training programme for public and private security 

personnel in host Countries to disseminate corporate best practices in line with international principles. In 2025, the ‘Security & Human 

Rights’ Workshop was held in Congo, in Pointe Noire, with the participation of Eni’s Head of Global Security, the Italian Ambassador to 

Congo and the Congolese Minister of Justice. The initiative was aimed at raising awareness among Congolese public and private Security 

Forces not only on security and human rights issues, but also on violence against women, their rights and the behaviours expected of 

Security Forces. The workshop was a valuable opportunity for discussion among local authorities, Eni personnel, Congolese institutional 

security representatives, and the trainer. During 2025, commitment also continued in the implementation of the ‘In-Country Workshops 

on Security & Human Rights’ project, dedicated to the delivery, by subsidiary Security Managers, of training workshops on security and 

human rights to local private security forces. These workshops were held in Ghana, Indonesia, Algeria, Egypt, Iraq, Kenya, and Libya. In 

2025, the new Train the Trainers Project was also implemented, involving the Group’s subsidiary in Mozambique (Eni Rovuma Basin). The 

initiative aims to train public security forces on Security & Human Rights, through the involvement of an external consultant, so that they in 

turn acquire the necessary know-how to train other security forces in this area. This project represented the first application of a Training 

Module that can be replicated in other Eni operations worldwide. The initiative was carried out in collaboration with the Ministry of Justice 

and the National Human Rights Authority of Mozambique. The training lasted five days and involved 21 trainer candidates (including six 

women) from the Ministries of Justice, Interior and Defence, Penitentiary Police, and Immigration Police. The number of Countries in which 

armed guards are deployed to protect sites is 15.

METRICS [ESRS 2 MDR-M; ENTITY-SPECIFIC]

Local development investments and grievances

Among the main projects carried out in 2025 it is worth noting initiatives aimed at promoting: (i) access to energy in Congo, Côte d’Ivoire, 

Mozambique, Rwanda, Angola and Tanzania through the distribution of improved cooking systems, and in Tunisia through the installation 

of photovoltaic panels; (ii) economic diversification  through support for sustainable agriculture and/or fishing practices in Mexico, Egypt, 

Kazakhstan and Mozambique, and for the enhancement of cultural heritage in Italy; (iii) access to primary and secondary education in Mexico, 

Mozambique  and  Iraq,  and  vocational  and  tertiary  education  in  Côte  d’Ivoire,  Egypt  and  Libya;  (iv)  access  to  water,  through  interventions 

to strengthen water infrastructure in Mozambique and Iraq; (v) land protection through activities promoting biodiversity conservation and 

safeguarding public green spaces in Italy, Indonesia and Mozambique. As part of its healthcare development projects, in 2025 Eni has carried 

out 38 initiatives in 14 Countries through the strengthening of the skills of healthcare personnel (e.g. in Libya, Egypt, Angola and Indonesia), 

the construction and rehabilitation of healthcare facilities and equipping them (e.g. in Côte d’Ivoire, Indonesia, Mozambique and Italy), the 

information, education and awareness-raising on health topics of the populations involved (e.g. in Ghana and Mexico). 

For the next five years, Eni has allocated investments of approximately €401 million for local development. Finally, in 2025, 21 grievances were 

received and 18 were resolved133 (of which 13 were received in 2025). The main issues concerned matters related to relations with suppliers 

and requests for compensation for impacts on local activities; issues also emerged relating to the management of green areas near facilities, 

odour emissions and actions to mitigate the effects of climate change.

ANNEX

CUSTOMERS AND CONSUMERS

[ESRS S4] 

This chapter focuses on B2C customers of Plenitude - Benefit Corporation (Società Benefit), with a particular focus on the 10 million 

retail customers to whom Eni supplies energy, energy efficiency solutions and electric mobility services. Attention is focused on this 

customer segment due to the presence of an ongoing contractual relationship, which differentiates these customers from those of 

Eni’s other business lines.

POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; S4-1]

POLICY

KEY CONTENTS:

 Code of Ethics

•  Principle of transparent management of relationships with customers and  consumers, inspired by best practices and 

the principle of professional loyalty to customers and consumers.

•  Building commercial relationships focused on customer needs, enabling customers to choose freely and consciously.

  ECG Policy on Respect for 

•  Engagement of third parties in the prevention and mitigation of any adverse human rights impacts that their operations, 

Human Rights in Eni

products or services might cause or contribute to causing.

 ECG Policy on Privacy and 
data protection

•  Definition of roles and responsibilities for the collection, use, retention and deletion of data, including appropriate 

technical organizational measures for data protection.

•  Definition of operational procedures for the processing of personal data in compliance with applicable regulations, 

ensuring transparency, security and fairness in data processing activities.

•  Management of data subjects’ requests, procedures for reporting of any violation and control and periodic update 

activities for the  continuous and systematic protection of the personal information processed.

  ECG Policy on Consumer 
Protection & Green Claims

•  Compliance with rules and principles on consumer protection and on accurate environmental and sustainability 

communication (Green Claims and Sustainability Claims).

•  Tools aimed at preventing the risk of breaches, including ‘inadvertent’ breaches, of Consumer Protection Regulation.
•  Dissemination of a compliance culture in the area of consumer protection, contributing to fostering the identification 

and reporting by Eni people to identify and report any actions/behaviours that may constitute violations, in line with the 
company’s Internal Regulatory System.

Internal Regulatory System

•  Commitment to comply with all regulations aimed at safeguarding fair competition and consumers’ right to receive 

clear, accurate and comprehensive information on the products and services offered.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; S4-5]

The  defined  targets,  in  line  with  the  applicable  policies,  play  a  central  role  in  the  strategic  choices  and  the  commitments  to  build 

customer-centric  commercial  relationships,  enabling  customers  to  make  free  and  informed  choices,  also  through  correct  commercial 

communication.  To  this  end,  Plenitude  has  established  a  dedicated  organisational  function  responsible  for  verifying  compliance  with 

consumer  protection  regulations  across  all  its  business  initiatives  and  customer  communications,  with  the  aim  of  avoiding  unfair 

commercial  practices  and  providing  clear,  complete,  accurate  and  non-misleading  information.  Customer  service  performance  is 

monitored on a weekly basis through business review processes, using a set of metrics (both reported in the Sustainability Statement 

and other indicators monitored internally) tracking alignment with the defined targets. Meetings are also organised with national and local 

representatives of Consumer Associations on an annual basis to present corporate strategies and, periodically, for in-depth discussions 

on specific topics of interest to end consumers.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3; S4 SBM-3]

Material impacts 

Eni broadens its operational scope to reach end markets by offering gas, electricity and energy solutions to retail and business customers, 

as well as providing electric mobility services. The main material customer-related impacts primarily concern Plenitude, due to the existence 

of an ongoing contractual relationship. Eni is committed to enhancing customer satisfaction while simultaneously promoting a culture of 

responsible energy use, by offering appropriate and quality products and services. This commitment is supported by effective customer 

dialogue channels and by the adoption of transparent and fair commercial practices. At the same time, potential negative impacts may arise 

from accidental events, such as unclear advertising campaigns or misleading or aggressive commercial practices, which could mislead 

customers or unduly influence their purchasing decisions.

Material risks and opportunities

No material risks have been identified at Group level in relation to consumers (see   Process and results of the double materiality assessment), 

except for the potential loss of confidentiality of sensitive information related to cybersecurity risk, which is addressed in the   Cybersecurity 

section. In line with the identified target, the development of electric vehicle charging points134 represents a business opportunity for the 

expansion of services supporting electric mobility.

CUSTOMER ENGAGEMENT [S4 SBM-2; S4-2; S4-3]

Within  Plenitude,  there  is  a  dedicated  team  responsible  for  market  analysis  and  customer  listening135,  aimed  at  identifying  needs  and 

areas for improvement, consumption trends, socio-economic developments and main concerns. Each year, a wide range of qualitative 

and quantitative market research initiatives is carried out through multiple channels, including online, telephone and in-person surveys, 

with the support of specialised research institutes and companies operating in Italy and abroad, in line with recognised industry quality 

standards. In 2025, more than 120 research projects were conducted, involving over 190,000 current and potential customers. In addition, 

an initiative aimed at listening to calls made to the toll-free customer service number continued throughout the year. Customer satisfaction 

is monitored on a continuous basis and is measured as the percentage of customers assigning an overall satisfaction score above 7 out 

of 10 to Plenitude as an energy supplier. To evaluate the effectiveness of customer engagement channels, additional KPIs are monitored, 

including  the  Net  Promoter  Score  (NPS),  which  measures  the  share  of  customers  willing  to  recommend  Plenitude  and  the  complaint 

rate.  Ongoing  dialogue  with  Consumer  Associations  is  also  actively  promoted  to  enhance  customer  satisfaction  and  service  quality. 

(134) This opportunity is further analysed in the 
(135) For further details on expectations and stakeholder engagement, please refer to the chapter 

 Climate Change section.

 Stakeholder Engagement.

ENI ANNUAL REPORT 2025

--- PAGE 228 ---

MANAGEMENT

CONSOLIDATED 
FINANCIAL STATEMENTS

ANNEX

This  dialogue  is  supported  by  dedicated  channels,  such  as  the  Protocollo  di  Conciliazione  Paritetica  (Joint  Conciliation  Protocol),  an 

out-of-court dispute resolution mechanism between the Company and customers, in line with Alternative Dispute Resolution procedure. 

Consumer  Associations  are  also  enabled  to  report  potential  service  shortcomings  and  product  malfunctions  on  behalf  of  customers 

through a dedicated telephone service and a dedicated web area. Plenitude further engages with relevant Authorities and institutions at 

both national and local level, through consultations and hearings, including on matters related to the protection of vulnerable customers 

(for further details see   Actions). Operational responsibility for ensuring effective customer engagement and for integrating its outcomes 

into the Company’s approach lies with the Director of Italian retail market, in collaboration with the support functions.

Reporting mechanisms and remediation processess 

Plenitude has implemented consumer protection mechanisms that also extend to intermediaries carrying out commercial activities on its 

behalf. All sales operators are subject to a preliminary assessment aimed at verifying their financial soundness, sector-specific experience, 

the adequacy of their commercial structure and their technical and commercial training requirements. Following a positive outcome of 

this assessment, personnel of the operators authorised to operate on behalf of Plenitude are required to complete a mandatory training 

programme, designed to ensure compliance with the Company’s processes and quality standards. Monitoring of the activities carried out 

by commercial partners is also envisaged, based on continuous performance analysis and qualitative KPIs. These results are reviewed by 

management in periodic sessions, focused on customer protection, ensuring proactive and systematic oversight of the entire process. In 

Italy, Plenitude manages customer reports136 in compliance with the regulations of the Authority for Energy, Networks and Environment 

(ARERA). These reports are analysed both qualitatively and quantitatively to understand customer issues and to activate corrective actions. 

On a quarterly basis, the multidisciplinary customer protection Committee reviews indicators related to the quality of partners’ commercial 

performance and defines the related action plans. Specific sanctioning procedures apply to complaints concerning commercial partners, 

including  penalties  for  unsolicited  activations  and  preliminary  investigations  into  contractual  breaches  under  the  mandate,  which  are 

assessed by the Penalties Committee. Customers may submit reports through multiple channels, including remote channels (website, 

customer area/app, chat, call centre, e-mail and postal service), a dedicated assistance service in Italian Sign Language, as well as direct 

and  indirect  physical  channels  across  the  territory.  Information  on  available  contact  channels  is  provided  on 

  eniplenitude.com,  on 

bills and within commercial and contractual documentation. Regardless of the channel used, Plenitude ensures the receipt, analysis and 

handling of reports, including anonymous submissions, while guaranteeing the highest level of confidentiality. Plenitude also trains and 

informs its service providers, including customer care operators and call centres, by sharing internal procedures and delivering training 

sessions supported by quality-monitoring surveys. To ensure alignment with Plenitude’s standards and expectations, controls are carried 

out on call handling and customer management, including sample call listening and the sharing of structured feedback with partners. 

Complaints  and  information  requests  are  monitored  daily  through  a  dedicated  dashboard  and  managed  by  a  combination  of  internal 

resources and specialised external providers. Control systems are in place to verify the quality of practices and to identify training gaps and 

opportunities for improvement. The Customer Feedback programme relies on a dedicated platform to deliver surveys, measure customer 

satisfaction KPIs and interact with customers, integrating their feedback into the Company’s systems. The effectiveness of these tools is 

monitored in line with the commercial quality standards set by ARERA. In relation to information requests and complaints, response times, 

underlying reasons and any recurrence are analysed to identify root causes and define improvement actions. Finally, Plenitude monitors 

both the percentage of reports resolved at first contact (First Call Resolution) and the share of operations carried out autonomously by 

customers out of the total number of requested operations. 

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; S4-4] 

Plenitude provides for the implementation of several actions to remedy potential negative impacts on customers and consumers. The 

effectiveness of these actions is monitored through operational indicators (such as unsolicited activations, complaint rate, NPS, etc.) on 

a monthly or weekly basis. The actions implemented on the basis of the material impacts, risks and opportunities identified are outlined 

below and are grouped as follows: (i) actions for customer management; (ii) customer protection and fraud management; (iii) initiatives 

for vulnerable customers; (iv) customer engagement in the energy transition.

(136) For further details on the process for managing access to human rights remedy mechanisms, please refer to the 

 Human Rights for Eni chapter.
