ANNEX

227

Actions for customer management

To  manage  and  oversee  material  customer-related  impacts,  Plenitude  relies  on  dedicated  teams  with  specific  expertise  within  both 

the commercial functions (e.g. Customer Operations, Value Stream Customer Experience Management) and the legal functions (e.g. 

Data Protection, Corporate Liability Compliance and Ethics Code Values, Consumer & Brand Identity Protection). Dedicated budgets 

are  defined  to  support  these  activities  and  are  fully  integrated  into  day-to-day  business  management  and  operations,  covering 

customer  contact  services,  back-office  activities,  Customer  Relationship  Management  (CRM),  billing  and  metering  processes,  and 

credit  management.  Future  financial  resources  are  planned  and  allocated  in  line  with  the  evolution  of  the  customer  base  and  with 

the services required to support business development. In relation to actions aimed at strengthening positive impacts on customers, 

Plenitude continues its commitment to the ongoing updating of the CRM system to improve the overall customer and user experience 

by  simplifying  activities  for  both  operators  and  customers,  as  well  as  expanding  customer  oriented  functionalities,  while  ensuring 

multichannel  availability.  In  addition,  Plenitude  has  adopted  internal  procedures  to  mitigate  the  effects  of  any  service  disruptions 

attributable to the Company that may occur in the management of individual services (e.g. contractual operations such as switching, 

activations, terminations and product changes) or in the supply service (e.g. billing regularity137, payment recording, etc.), in addition 

to the compensation already provided for under sector regulation. Such procedures provide, for example, for compensation measures 

proportionate to the type and duration of the service disruption, as well as rebates or refunds of costs incurred by customers. Each case 

is assessed on an individual basis and, where appropriate, addressed directly with the customer to identify a mutually agreed solution, 

with the aim of preventing the issue from persisting.

Customer protection and fraud management

With regard to actions related to negative impacts, Plenitude has entered into the ‘unsolicited activations’ protocol with the consumer 

associations  affiliated  with  the  National  Council  of  Consumers  and  Users,  with  the  aim  of  strengthening  consumer  protection 

measures  and,  more  broadly,  addressing  conduct  attributable  to  unfair  commercial  practices.  Moreover,  a  joint  Alternative  Dispute 

Resolution (ADR) mechanism is also in place, providing a rapid, simple and out-of-court solution to disputes between consumers and 

companies.  Plenitude  is  committed  to  protecting  its  customers  against  potential  unfair  commercial  practices  by  third  parties,  such 

as unauthorised activations. To ensure continuous oversight of service quality, the trend in the activation of supply contracts, as well 

as other products (e.g. boilers, air conditioning systems) and services (e.g. insurance policies) is monitored, with particular focus on 

cases where activations do not occur. The trend in the activation of supply point contracts is reported, and any issues that may arise 

after the customer signs the contract preventing its effective activation are closely monitored. In the area of data protection, Plenitude 

adopts an integrated and interdisciplinary approach to the management of personal data and confidential information, ensuring that all 

processing activities are carried out in full compliance with the principles of lawfulness, fairness, transparency and data minimisation 

set out in Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR). The Company promotes a widespread culture of privacy, supported by specific training 

and awareness-raising initiatives for personnel, as well as by the adoption of procedures and tools aimed at strengthening data security 

and  protection  throughout  the  entire  information  lifecycle. This  commitment  is  reflected  in  the  continuous  monitoring  of  processes 

and  the  regular  updating  of  technical  and  organisational  measures,  in  line  with  regulatory  and  technological  developments  in  the 

sector. Plenitude has also appointed a Data Protection Officer, who performs this role also for the subsidiaries. Finally, in relation to 

fraud prevention, Plenitude has implemented a range of initiatives to support customers affected by attempted scams, by providing 

them with specific tools to protect themselves and to verify the identity of individuals who contact them. These include informational 

alerts on fraud attempts, a dedicated toll-free number to handle reports on suspicious calls, as well as a verification service allowing 

customers  to  check  whether  the  phone  number  from  which  they  are  contacted  is  attributable  to  a  Plenitude  operator. This  service, 

active  since  2020,  received  1,150  reports  in  2025,  all  relating  to  phone  numbers  not  registered  in  the  Single  Register  of  Call  Centre 

Operators and therefore in breach of the applicable legal framework and potentially fraudulent.

(137) During 2025, Eni Plenitude France received a fine of € 275,000 from the Directorate-General for Competition, Consumer Affairs and Fraud Control (DGCCRF) for billing errors 
affecting more than 77,000 customers between 2022 and 2023. During the investigation, in order to protect its customers, Plenitude voluntarily adopted compensatory measures in 
favour of the customers involved. Following the agreement reached with the DGCCRF, the Company accepted payment of the fine without contesting the decision.

ANNEX

Initiatives for vulnerable customers138

Following  the  end  of  the  regulated  gas  market  in  Italy  in  December  2023,  Plenitude  also  confirmed  for  2025  a  range  of  offers  with 

characteristics  and  price  levels  similar  to  those  of  the  former  regulated  regime.  Accordingly,  these  offers  continued  to  be  available 

not only to vulnerable customers, as defined by ARERA, but also to all other customers previously served under the regulated market 

who had not yet opted for an offer in the free market. Plenitude has also implemented specific measures to protect blind and visually 

impaired  customers,  hearing-impaired  customers  and  financially  vulnerable  customers.  All  app  functionalities  have  been  made  fully 

accessible to blind and visually impaired users. For hearing-impaired customers, in addition to chat and other digital contact channels, 

the  TELLIS  customer  service  has  been  available  since  2022,  enabling  communication  via  Italian  Sign  Language  through  qualified 

remote interpreters. Furthermore, Plenitude recognises young people among financially vulnerable customers. The Company is among 

the first 50 partner organisations of the National Youth Card, an initiative promoted by the Italian Department for Youth Policies and 

Universal Civil Service, aimed at European young people aged between 18 and 35, residing in Italy. The initiative provides benefits on 

the supply of gas and electricity from renewable sources covered by Guarantees of Origin, as well as a promotional offer on boilers and 

air conditioning systems.

Customer engagement in the energy transition 

With  regard  to  activities  related  to  efficient  energy  usage,  Plenitude  is  committed  to  guiding  customers  towards  energy  awareness 

by  providing  personalised  advice  based  on  their  energy  profile,  through  a  dedicated  web  area  and  app  reserved  to  customers.  To 

this  end,  also  in  2025,  Plenitude  made  available  to  its  customers  a  tool  enabling  them  to  estimate  the  electricity  production  of  a 

residential  photovoltaic  system,  ensuring,  at  the  offer  evaluation  stage,  the  possibility  to  estimate  potential  savings  based  on 

customers’ consumption habits. The ‘Plenitude Insieme’ loyalty programme, in addition to building a lasting and valuable relationship 

with customers, offers initiatives aimed at increasing awareness and knowledge of energy efficiency. In 2025, the participation rate 

of registered users remained high (over 80%), confirming the continued engagement of customers in the energy transition journey. In 

2025, in fact, 83% of enrolled customers interacted with the programme at least once, and more than 390,000 people deepened their 

knowledge of energy efficiency (an increase of 95% compared to 2024). In 2025, Plenitude launched the ‘Adopt a Panel’ service, enabling 

residential customers to virtually consume solar energy produced in real time by photovoltaic panels located at Plenitude-owned plants, 

without the need to install a domestic system. The service allows customers to benefit from a 100% discount on electricity charges 

for the energy produced by the panel and consumed by the customer at the same time. It also enables customers to monitor energy 

production and receive energy efficiency advice through the Plenitude app or their personal web area.

(138) The definition of vulnerable customers also includes Electricity and Natural gas customers as defined by Regulatory Authority for Energy, Networks and the Environment (ARERA- 
Autorità di Regolazione per Energia Reti e Ambiente).

BUSINESS CONDUCT

POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; G1-1]

POLICY

KEY CONTENTS:

 Code of Ethics

  ECG Policy on  

Anti-Corruption

  Management of 

Whistleblowing Reports 

received by Eni SpA and  

by its Subsidiaries 

•  Commitment to act in line with the values of responsibility, integrity and fairness, and with a culture of legality and 

transparency.

•  Prohibition and fight all forms of corruption, in favour of anyone, without exception, through the adoption of rules and 

controls to prevent and combat the risk of corruption in the performance of its activities.

•  Prohibition, without exception, all forms of corruption, active, passive, direct and indirect, in favour of and by anyone.

whistleblower and other subjects involved, protecting them from retaliatory consequences.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T]

Eni  has  defined  specific  commitments  on  business  conduct  matters,  shared  with  its  stakeholders,  relating  to  the  maintenance  of  ISO 

37001:2016 and ISO 37301:2021 certifications, the continuous improvement of its Anti-Corruption Compliance Programme and training 

on the Programme for personnel exposed to medium and high levels of risk. 

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3; G1 IRO-1]

Material impacts 

BUSINESS CONDUCT, CORPORATE CULTURE AND CORRUPTION PREVENTION

Eni’s  presence  in  multiple  Countries  may  entail  exposure  to  the  risk  of  potential  corrupt  practices  across  different  business  areas. 

Corruption, by its very nature, represents an obstacle to sustainable development; it can distort competition and undermine trust in the 

economic system and in institutions. The economic repercussions include financial losses, reduced business competitiveness and lower 

investment levels. Moreover, corruption can erode economic efficiency and the fair allocation of resources, leading to slower economic 

development. The occurrence of these events, also considering their significant reputational impact, may generate adverse consequences 

not only for employees but also for various categories of stakeholders who maintain economic, contractual and institutional relationships 

with  the  Company.  To  prevent  these  potential  impacts,  Eni  adopts  and  implements  specific  measures  and  actions  aimed  at  ensuring

adequate  safeguards  (see 

  Actions  taken  on  material  IROs).  In  doing  so,  the  Company  reaffirms  the  importance  of  conducting  its 

business with loyalty, fairness, transparency, honesty and integrity and in compliance with applicable laws, regulations and other binding 

requirements, as well as with international standards and guidelines, both national and foreign, to which it is subject.

INSTITUTIONAL ENGAGEMENT ACTIVITIES

As part of its partnerships and advocacy initiatives, Eni engages in dialogue with policymakers and national, European and international 

institutions, as well as with advocacy organisations, industry associations and business federations, highlighting its commitment to the 

energy transition in both its traditional activities and its new businesses. In this context, Eni contributes its experience as an international 

energy company to the development of policies and regulations aimed at supporting the Net Zero transition, taking into account the social, 

economic and environmental dimensions of the contexts in which it operates.

Material risks and opportunities 

No material risks or opportunities have been identified at Group level (see   Process and results of the double materiality assessment). 

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; G1-1; G1-3; G1-4]

Business Conduct, Corporate Culture and Corruption Prevention

Eni strongly believes in promoting, at all levels of the organisation, a culture grounded in legality, compliance with applicable rules, integrity 

values and behavioural and control principles adopted by the Company. The 

 Code of Ethics reaffirms integrity and transparency among 

the  values  that  characterise  the  commitment  of  Eni  people  and  all  third  parties  working  with  the  Company  (see 

  Code  of  Ethics  and 

Policies chapter). In this context, to prevent the potential material impacts identified, Eni develops appropriate ethical programmes and 

safeguards and adopts suitable measures to meet the expectations of different stakeholders. The actions implemented focus on: (i) the 

Anti‑Corruption  Compliance  Programme;  (ii)  training  and  communication  activities;  (iii)  anti‑corruption  initiatives  across  Eni’s  value 

chain; (iv) the role of the Internal Audit function.

THE ANTI-CORRUPTION COMPLIANCE PROGRAMME

Since 2009, Eni has adopted an Anti-Corruption Compliance Programme: a structured and continuously updated system of rules, controls 

and organisational safeguards designed to actively prevent corruption and money-laundering offences. The Programme has been developed 

in  accordance  with  national  and  international  regulations  and  is  aligned  with  applicable  best  practices  and  guidance.  From  a  regulatory 

perspective, the Anti-Corruption Compliance Programme is based on the 

 ECG Policy on Anti-Corruption, supplemented by a set of detailed 

regulatory  instruments  made  available  to  Eni  people,  which  govern  activities  at  risk  and  define  the  relevant  controls.  These  instruments 

govern activities exposed to corruption risk and define the related control measures. Eni requires its subsidiaries, both in Italy and abroad, 

to formally adopt these instruments through a resolution of their Board of Directors (or equivalent governing body). For companies in which 

Eni  holds  a  non-controlling  interest,  the  Company  encourages  compliance  with  the  standards  it  has  defined  through  the  adoption  and 

maintenance of an internal control system consistent with applicable legal requirements. Eni has also established a centralized organizational 

function that provides specialized support on anti-corruption and anti-money laundering matters to the Company and its subsidiaries, with 

particular reference to the assessment of the reliability of higher-risk third parties (so-called ‘anti-corruption and anti-money laundering due 

diligence’), the management of potential issues or red flags, and the definition of related mitigation measures. These include the formulation 

of contractual compliance safeguards and, for individually assessed higher-risk cases, requesting counterparties to adopt an anti-corruption 

compliance program. In 2025, neither the Company nor members of senior management were involved in criminal proceedings resulting in 

final convictions for violations of anti-corruption regulations (for further details on Group litigation, see 

 Note 28 ‘Guarantees, commitments 

and risks’ of the consolidated financial statements). Eni has implemented a structured Compliance Risk Assessment and Monitoring process 

aimed at: (i) identifying, assessing and tracking corruption risks within its business activities, as well as supporting the definition and updating 

of control measures set out in internal regulations; (ii) periodically analysing trends in identified corruption risks through control activities and 

the analysis of risk indicators, in order to ensure compliance with regulatory requirements and the effectiveness of the control framework; 

(iii) contributing to the identification of Eni employees most exposed to corruption risk, taking into account both the drivers defined under 

the methodology for the systematic segmentation of personnel for training purposes and the level of exposure of the relevant professional 

families to corruption-risk activities139. Based on the relevant operational and organizational context, activities identified as being exposed to 

(139)  The  segmentation  methodology,  aimed  at  identifying  the  recipients  of  the  various  training  initiatives,  does  not  identify  risk  functions  but  considers,  for  each  individual,  their 
qualification, the professional family to which they belong (e.g., Procurement, Commercial, CFO, etc.) and their relative exposure to corruption-risk activities, as well as the Country risk 
and the specific risk of the Company.

approach. During 2025, Compliance Monitoring activities focused on the risk areas of ‘Joint Ventures’, ‘Sponsorships and Non-Profit Initiatives’, 

and ‘Transactions for the acquisition, disposal and management of exploratory mineral rights’; the outcomes of the analysis confirmed the 

expected level of risk, as well as the overall adequacy and effectiveness of Eni’s anti-corruption compliance model. As in 2024, in 2025 the 

number of substantiated corruption cases140 relating to Eni SpA was 0 and, consequently, no dismissals related to such cases occurred. For 

information on ongoing proceedings and on the total number of significant cases of non-compliance with laws and regulations (including 

any anti-competitive behaviour and violations of antitrust and monopoly regulations), reference should be made to the 

 Legal proceedings 

section.

TRAINING AND COMMUNICATION ACTIVITIES [TAXO; G1 GOV-1]

Anti-corruption  awareness-raising  and  training  initiatives  are  identified  based  on  the  needs  defined  for  the  different  target  groups.  In 

particular, the Management and control bodies of Eni SpA receive a semi-annual report, which forms an integral part of the Integrated 

Compliance  Report,  focusing  on  the  key  activities  carried  out  under  the  Anti-Corruption  Compliance  Program  and  on  the  planning  of 

future activities141. Members of the Board of Directors and the Board of Statutory Auditors, during meetings of the Board Committees, 

receive  in-depth  sessions  on  strategic  topics  such  as  sustainability,  human  rights  and  the  fight  against  corruption.  Eni  personnel  are 

also required to be informed about applicable laws, the principles set out in the 

 Code of Ethics, and other relevant internal regulations, 

in  order  to  understand  the  different  risks,  the  potential  consequences  of  violations  and  the  actions  to  be  taken  to  prevent  corruption 

and  money  laundering. To  enhance  the  effectiveness  of  training,  Eni  segments  its  personnel  according  to  risk  level  and  assigns  each 

employee  a  specific  training  programme  (online  or  classroom-based),  with  the  aim  of  covering  100%  of  at-risk  resources142.  Training 

courses illustrate the applicable regulations, the tools to identify risks and the procedures for reporting violations. In line with the principle 

of  top-level  commitment,  members  of  the  top  management  of  Eni  SpA,  business  heads/directors  and  the  Chief  Executive  Officers  (or 

equivalent roles) of subsidiaries also participate in training activities. These roles generally take part in classroom workshops, highlighting 

the importance of - and the strong synergy required between - compliance and business. In addition, senior executives of subsidiaries 

in Italy and abroad attend a three-day training event aimed at supporting the development and consolidation of their role, with a specific 

focus  on  compliance  and  risk  mitigation.  In  2025,  training  activities  included  the  continuation  of  the  online  courses  ‘Code  of  Ethics, 

Anti-Corruption and Corporate Administrative Liability’, addressed to all Eni personnel in Italy and abroad, as well as the course on the 

Anti-Corruption  Compliance  Program  for  medium  and  high-risk  personnel.  Classroom-based  training  activities  were  also  delivered  in 

accordance  with  a  risk-based  methodology.  Moreover,  in  2025  the  Anti-Corruption  and  Anti-Money  Laundering  Unit:  (i)  delivered  two 

sessions of a new competitive classroom seminar to selected business compliance resources and senior executives, including the CEO, of 

a Group subsidiary operating in the wholesale trade of fuels and derivatives (Enimoov); (ii) piloted a new format of joint classroom training 

on Legislative Decree 231 and anti-corruption matters, delivered at the Gela Biorefinery and at Enilive; (iii) delivered a new joint training 

format on Human Rights, Corporate Administrative Liability and Anti-Corruption in Mozambique, Congo and Kenya.

(140) Final convictions relating to criminal proceedings for domestic and/or international corruption in which the occurrence of a corruption offence has been established on the merits.

ANTI-CORRUPTION INITIATIVES ACROSS ENI’S VALUE CHAIN

The ECG Anti‑Corruption Policy is shared with higher‑risk third parties through specific contractual clauses and compliance declarations. 

These  include,  among  other  things,  a  commitment  to  review  the 

  Code  of  Ethics,  the 

  231  Model  and  the 

  ECG  Policy  on  Anti-

Corruption (available on the company’s website) and to comply with their principles. Depending on the circumstances, related training and 

awareness‑raising initiatives are also promoted. As part of the qualification process for potential suppliers, described below, their ethical and 

reputational profile is assessed. In cases involving higher corruption risk, the adoption by such suppliers of an Anti‑Corruption Compliance 

Program  is  also  evaluated.  In  all  cases,  the  relevant  contracts  provide  for  the  inclusion  of  compliance  clauses  which,  in  addition  to  the 

above‑mentioned commitments, include contractual remedies in the event of violations and, for higher‑risk cases, audit rights in favour of Eni. 

Subcontractors are also subject to preliminary checks to verify their ethical and reputational reliability and are required to operate exclusively 

on the basis of a written contract that includes compliance commitments equivalent to those required of direct suppliers. As part of the 

training initiatives addressed to third parties, in 2025 Eni organized a training session for the personnel of a Tunisian Joint Venture (Sonatrach‑

Eni ‑ GSE) and continued the delivery of an online course for high‑risk suppliers. 

THE ROLE OF THE INTERNAL AUDIT FUNCTION

Eni’s Internal Audit function plays a primary role in ensuring compliance with business conduct (including the management of reports 

received regarding alleged violations), within its broader mandate of reviewing and assessing the Internal Control and Risk Management 

System  (ICRMS).  This  role  is  carried  out  through  the  verification  of  the  overall  effectiveness  and  adequacy  of  the  system  and  of  its 

operational implementation. In order to provide specialized support to top management and senior leadership on the Eni Risk and Internal 

Control Holistic Framework143, audit activities are defined within an Audit Plan. The Plan is developed on the basis of relevance criteria and 

coverage of the main risks and is approved at least annually by the BoD, following the opinion of the Control and Risk Committee (CRC) and 

after consultation with the Chair of the Board, the Chief Executive Officer and Eni’s Board of Statutory Auditors. The Head of the Internal 

Audit function may also initiate additional audit activities not included in the Audit Plan, at the request of the management, control and 

supervisory bodies, as well as of top management and senior leadership. During 2025, a total of 29 audit engagements144 were carried 

out in 16 Countries as part of overall audit activities. These included anti‑corruption audits to verify compliance with the provisions of the 

Anti‑Corruption Compliance Program and 15 supervisory interventions on the 231 Model and compliance systems of Italian and foreign 

subsidiaries. The appointment and removal of the Head of the Internal Audit function are governed by specific governance rules designed 

to ensure full independence. In addition to the recommendations set out in the Corporate Governance Code, the Head of Internal Audit is 

appointed by the Board of Directors, following the opinion of the Control and Risk Committee and the Nomination Committee and after 

consultation with the Board of Statutory Auditors, upon proposal by the Chair of the Board in agreement with the Chief Executive Officer. 

The Head reports directly to the Chair of the Board and also reports to the Board of Statutory Auditors, including in its capacity as ‘Audit 

Committee’ under U.S. legislation. Furthermore, in order to promote the evolution and progressive strengthening of risk management and 

control systems along the entire value chain, in 2025 Eni launched the initiative ‘G•row – Evolving through Risk & Control Governance’, 

with the support of several corporate functions. The initiative, which implements the principle underlying the SDG 17 of the United Nations 

2030 Agenda, aims to foster the growth of the corporate ecosystem and the progressive development of distinctive skills in the field of 

Risk & Control Governance through a collaborative approach involving partners, suppliers and stakeholders. G•row takes shape through a 

digital platform that provides tools and resources for self‑assessment, benchmarking against comparable organizations and the start of 

an organic and continuous improvement journey (for more information see 

 G•row).

WHISTLEBLOWING MECHANISMS AND VERIFICATION PROCESS FOR VIOLATIONS OF THE CODE OF 

ETHICS, ANTI-CORRUPTION RULES AND OTHER REGULATIONS [TAXO]

Since 2006, Eni has adopted an internal regulatory instrument, 

 Management of Whistleblowing Reports received by Eni SpA and by its 

Subsidiaries, which has been updated over time and, most recently, in November 2025, aligned with national and international best practices 

and with EU Directive 2019/1937 governing the process for receiving, analysing and handling whistleblowing reports. The regulation, available 

on Eni’s website and intranet together with a concise operational guide, enables Eni people, as well as anyone who works or has worked in 

(143) The Internal Control and Risk Management System reflects the Company’s culture and values. It consists of a coordinated set of tools, rules, procedures, organizational structures, 
data, systems, information flows and behaviours aimed at the effective and efficient identification, measurement, management and monitoring of the main risks. In doing so, it contributes 
to the Company’s sustainable success and to the enhancement of business opportunities, as set out in the 
(144) Audit engagements that also cover reviews of activities exposed to corruption risk, as defined under Eni’s Internal Regulatory System.

ANNEX

233

Italy or abroad in the name, on behalf, or in the interest of the Company, to report alleged violations arising in the work environment. To allow 

an effective and appropriate follow-up, whistleblowing reports should be substantiated and include, where possible, a detailed description of 

the facts, events or circumstances underlying the alleged violation, providing sufficient detail to enable the competent corporate functions 

to verify the accuracy of the reported elements. Activities following receipt of a whistleblowing report are managed by the ‘Whistleblowing 

Team’145,  which  operates  in  accordance  with  the  principles  of  objectivity,  competence  (also  through  periodic  training)  and  professional 

diligence, and ensures that the whistleblower receives appropriate feedback. After a preliminary analysis, the Team conducts in-depth reviews, 

analyses  and  assessments  to  establish  whether  the  reported  facts  are  substantiated,  and  monitors  any  corrective  actions  implemented 

in  the  relevant  corporate  areas  and  processes. The  Whistleblowing Team,  through  its  members  or  other  individuals  appointed  within  the 

relevant unit, also prepares the Quarterly Whistleblowing Report. This document is submitted to Eni’s Board of Statutory Auditors and, for 

the relevant whistleblowing files, subsequently forwarded to the Supervisory Bodies (national or international, depending on the subsidiary) 

and to the Boards of Statutory Auditors of the relevant companies, where applicable. Statistical information on whistleblowing files handled 

over  the  past  five  years  is  published  on  Eni’s  website.  All  functions  involved  in  the  whistleblowing  files  management  process,  including 

those dealing with anti‑corruption issues, ensure independence, absence of conflicts of interest, objectivity, competence and professional 

diligence. These  principles  are  consistent  with  international  standards  and  with  the  provisions  of  the 

  Code  of  Ethics. To  facilitate  the 

receipt of whistleblowing reports, both in written and oral form, Eni makes available a dedicated platform (https://whistleblowing.eni.com), 

operated by an external service provider, which guarantees the confidentiality of the identity of the whistleblower, the reported person and 

the contents of the communication. The platform, widely publicised on companies’ websites, provides separate channels for Eni SpA and 

for EU subsidiaries (so-called proximity channels), in line with and pursuant to national legislation implementing EU Directive 2019/1937. 

Regardless of the nature of the whistleblowing report or the Eni company involved, it is always possible to submit whistleblowing reports 

through the Eni SpA channel, which are managed in compliance with Italian whistleblowing legislation. Alternative whistleblowing reporting 

tools are also in place (such as dedicated mailboxes, where necessary to ensure accessibility in specific contexts, and a voicemail service 

integrated into the platform). In 2025, the investigation of 79 whistleblowing files relating to procurement and human‑resources matters was 

completed; for full details, see the dedicated page on 

 Eni’s website. Whistleblowing reports received during 2025, both from Eni employees 

and from the value chain, demonstrate awareness and effective use of the dedicated whistleblowing tool. All Eni personnel who receive or 

handle  reports  are  required  to  ensure  the  maximum  confidentiality  regarding  the  identity  of  the  whistleblower,  the  persons  involved  and 

any other mentioned individuals, as well as the content and related documentation. This obligation is based on the ‘need-to-know’ principle, 

requiring communication methods that protect the identity and reputation of the individuals concerned and ensure maximum confidentiality 

of the data processed. The identity of the whistleblower cannot be disclosed without their explicit consent, except where required by law, 

and any retaliatory or discriminatory actions, direct or indirect, against the whistleblower are strictly prohibited. Whistleblower are protected 

against any form of dismissal, demotion, suspension, threat, harassment or discrimination. Violations of this prohibition result in disciplinary 

action and the adoption of appropriate measures to support the parties concerned. Whistleblowers retain, in all cases, the right to contact the 

competent authorities and institutions to report any retaliation.

Institutional Engagement Activities [G1-5]

Below  are  the  actions  implemented  by  Eni  based  on  the  identified  material  impacts,  risks  and  opportunities,  through  the  promotion  of 

partnerships and advocacy and lobbying activities. Eni engages in dialogue with policymakers both directly and indirectly through trade 

associations, without providing political contributions.

ENI’S LOBBYING ACTIVITIES

In  2025,  Eni’s  main  engagement  activities  with  national,  international  and  European  institutions  focused  on:  (i)  participation  in  economic 

promotion initiatives, meetings and roundtables on topics of interest for business development, geopolitical and energy scenarios, sustainable 

development  and  new  technologies;  (ii)  presenting  Eni’s  positioning  on  energy  transition  and  decarbonisation  at  public  events  and  major 

international multilateral forums (e.g., B7, B20, COP30); (iii) engaging and maintaining dialogue with institutions — also through partnerships 

and memberships — as well as with think tanks, associations and international organisations on the definition of policies and regulations 

relevant  to  Eni’s  activities,  particularly  in  the  areas  of  energy,  the  energy  transition,  industrial  transformation,  supercomputing  systems, 

(145) A dedicated unit with the required levels of competence, independence and absence of conflicts of interest, composed of managers from the following functions of Eni SpA: 
integrated compliance, legal affairs, human resources and organisation, internal audit, and administration and finance.

innovation and sustainable mobility; (iv) presenting projects and organising visits for institutional delegations, political representatives and 

associations to industrial facilities, operational sites and research centres. In particular, Eni contributes to the development of strategies and 

regulations aimed at accelerating the transition to Net Zero, clearly and transparently conveying its positioning on climate change and related 

strategic  topics.  Eni  recognizes  the  value  of  active  participation  in  business  associations’  work  to  develop  and  share  best  practices  and 

advocacy positions aimed at promoting the energy transition. In this regard, Eni proactively commits to periodically addressing the positions 

of each association, particularly those whose positions diverge from Eni’s Climate Advocacy Principles, towards an approach consistent with 

the need to act effectively to tackle climate change. Eni’s Climate Advocacy Principles are:

•  Paris  Agreement:  Eni  supports  the  Agreement’s  objectives  and  policies  that  pursue  sustainability,  energy  security,  and  industrial 

competitiveness goals jointly on the path to Net Zero by 2050;

•  Role of gas: Eni recognizes the role of natural gas in the energy transition and supports the implementation of specific regulations to reduce 

methane emissions and routine flaring;

•  Carbon pricing: Eni supports the implementation of credible and cost-efficient carbon pricing mechanisms;

•  Energy efficiency and low-carbon technologies: Eni promotes actions and policies supporting energy efficiency and technologies necessary 

for decarbonisation, such as renewables (both electrons and molecules in liquid/gaseous state), CCS, Carbon Dioxide Removal, hydrogen, 

and other strategic technologies for the transition;

•  Sustainable  mobility:  Eni  supports  the  implementation  of  complementary  solutions  for  transport  decarbonisation,  such  as  sustainable 

biofuels  and  electric  mobility,  and  policies  based  on  a  technology-neutral  approach  that  promote  the  most  mature  and  cost-efficient 

technologies;

•  Role of carbon credits: Eni supports the development of enabling policies for investments in Nature and Technology-Based Solutions and 

the use of credits to offset hard-to-abate residual emissions;

•  Transparency and disclosure: Eni supports the development of best practices for transparent disclosure on climate actions and climate 

advocacy.

Activities and commitments related to Eni’s engagement with institutional stakeholders, including lobbying activities, are the responsibility of 

the Public Affairs Director (reporting directly to Eni’s Chief Executive Officer), who participates in the Management Committee and the Risk 

Committee meetings and regularly reports to the CEO on relevant matters.

POLITICAL CONTRIBUTIONS 

As provided by the Code of Ethics, Eni does not use corporate resources for electoral contributions or political advocacy activities, nor 

for  activities  directed  toward  non-governmental  organizations,  except  for  internal  costs  related  to  the  activities  of  the  Public  Affairs 

Department and any expenses to third parties for intermediation activities with European Union institutions. Furthermore, Eni does not 

make donations to political parties but supports a range of scientific, cultural and social initiatives worldwide: every request from such 

programs is subject to rigorous due diligence to ensure that Eni’s contribution is not misused and/or misinterpreted. Eni is also registered 

in the EU Transparency Register146 and adheres to its Code of Conduct, which governs its relationship with EU institutions. Through the  

 Register, Eni provides extensive information on its activities, including the organization’s objectives, membership in trade and business 

associations, and expenses related to activities covered by the Register in the previous year. In Italy, Eni is listed in the registers established 

at the Ministry of Enterprises and Made in Italy (formerly Ministry of Economic Development) and at the Chamber of Deputies. Expenses 

related to lobbying activities in Italy are reported in the transparency register of the Ministry of Enterprises and Made in Italy. The Chamber 

of Deputies publishes 

 annual reports on the activities of registered companies. In the United States, all activities and expenses falling 

under the Lobbying Disclosure Act are reported quarterly and made 

 publicly available. Furthermore, every public position submitted to 

stakeholders and USG regulatory bodies (e.g., SEC, BOEM - Bureau of Ocean Energy Management) is published on the relevant websites 

of those stakeholders and regulatory bodies.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM- 3; G1 IRO-1]

Material impacts

Eni’s  procurement  strategy  is  based  on  sharing  values,  commitments  and  objectives  across  the  supply  chain,  adopting  a  systemic  and 

inclusive approach. This approach seeks to engage all levels of the supply chain in a path of sustainable growth through the promotion of 

sustainability principles, promoting environmental and social sustainability principles, to raise awareness and fostering more responsible 

business practices. The positive impact of this strategy is reflected throughout the entire supply chain, enhancing its competitiveness, and 

Eni’s  own  operations.  In  an  industrial  landscape  increasingly  focused  on  sustainability,  Eni  strengthens  its  leadership  through  the  Open-

es initiative, a digital platform and system alliance aimed at building a more resilient supply chain and supporting a modern, competitive 

entrepreneurial ecosystem aligned with sustainable transition objectives that constitute a central pillar of Eni’s strategy.

Material risks and opportunities 

No material risks were identified at Group level (see 

 Process and results of the double materiality assessment). A material opportunity 

has been identified in improving the sustainability performance of Eni’s supply chain and the wider entrepreneurial system, with Eni taking

ANNEX

a leadership role through the coordination of the system alliance and the Open-es digital platform. By adopting a sustainable supply chain 

management strategy, Eni promotes more sustainable and responsible behaviours, transparency and innovation among suppliers, reducing 

potential inefficiencies and enhancing environmental, social and governance standards across the entire procurement cycle. This approach 

generates tangible value for both Eni — through more competitive, resilient partners fully aligned with corporate objectives — and stakeholders, 

who benefit from a more robust, inclusive and energy-transition-oriented productive ecosystem. Within this context, Eni’s leadership in Open-

es serves as a key enabler. Guiding this alliance allows the Company to accelerate the evolution of its supply chain toward more sustainable 

and higher-performing models while reinforcing its role as an innovator and catalyst for change in the market.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; S2 SBM-3; S2-4; G1-2]

The sustainable supply chain management strategy of Eni is based on the sharing of values, commitments and objectives with its supply 

chain and is articulated around three pillars, on which specific actions are implemented:

•  systemic and inclusive approach, involving the supply chain in a path of improvement and sustainable development, through common 

objectives and differentiated models depending on the ESG maturity of companies. In this context, Eni promotes 

 Open-es, launched with 

Boston Consulting Group and Google Cloud in 2021, to create a multi-stakeholder initiative among the industrial, financial and associative 

sectors to support companies in the process of measuring and improving their ESG performance. To date, more than 30 partners have 

joined, including large industrial players, financial institutions and associations, and over 40,000 companies have registered, of which over 

9,400 belong to Eni’s supply chain, both in Italy and abroad; 

•  development and enhancement of best practices, supporting suppliers in meeting ESG requirements, providing tools for their sustainable 

development pathway and, more broadly, for competitiveness. These include tools for: (i) measuring and improving ESG maturity through 

metrics  aligned  with  the  regulatory  context  and  benchmarking  against  sector  peers,  with  access  to  personalised  development  plans, 

solutions offered by specialised ESG providers and free events and training programmes; (ii) financial support through ‘Sustainable Supply 

Chain Finance’ (launched in 2023) to request early payment of invoices without impacts on credit lines, in order to incentivise a sustainable 

development pathway that integrates improved financial resource management with enhanced ESG performance. In 2025, invoice advances 

were granted for a total amount of approximately €240 million; (iii) offering products and services under favourable conditions, such as 

solutions for energy efficiency and the use of HVOlution biofuel in transport; (iv) recognition of excellence through the Supply Chain Award, 

to share best practices and reward the most distinctive and innovative companies in the ESG field; (v) support for the competitiveness 

of the supply chain in the context of the energy transition. In 2025, the ‘Energia di filiera (Value chain energy)’ programme was launched, 

a  development  initiative  dedicated  to  Eni  qualified  suppliers,  in  particular  SMEs,  operating  in  the  industrial  areas  most  impacted  by  the 

transformation of Eni’s businesses, with the aim of supporting them in defining an improvement and development plan in a continuously 

evolving market context. In 2025, the focus of the programme was the Versalis Transformation Plan, involving more than 200 SMEs, half of 

them contractors located in the Italian industrial districts of Brindisi, Priolo, and Ragusa; 

•  pervasiveness of sustainability in the procurement process, represented by the integration of environmental protection, social growth and 

economic development principles at every stage. Eni has adopted the ‘Sustainable Supply Chain Framework’, a governance mechanism that 

combines corporate objectives, legislative requirements, targets, and specific action plans that impact the procurement process and the 

supply chain more broadly. This framework is implemented through a cross-cutting oversight of the different sustainability dimensions, with 

a focus on priority ESG topics periodically identified based on the corporate strategic plan and the evolution of the regulatory framework. In 

particular, the cross-cutting oversight provides for: (i) the signing of the 

 Supplier Code of Conduct as a mutual commitment to recognise 

Eni’s values and the assessment of all new suppliers also according to social criteria147; (ii) periodic qualification updates and due diligence 

to  minimise  risks  along  the  supply  chain  through  verification  of  suppliers’  ESG  positioning  and  ethical-reputational,  economic-financial 

and  technical-operational  reliability,  and  the  application  of  safeguards  relating  to  health,  safety,  environment,  governance,  cybersecurity 

and human rights; (iii) contract award criteria also based on ESG characteristics relevant to the contractual scope; (iv) periodic monitoring 

of compliance with commitments undertaken and of supplier behaviour through the management of performance feedback; (v) sharing 

of improvement actions with the supplier in the event of critical issues at any stage of the relationship and limitation or exclusion from 

participation in tenders where minimum acceptability standards are not met by the supplier. In 2025, for certain ESG dimensions identified 

as priorities for Eni — such as climate change, supply chain governance, human rights, cybersecurity and safety — verification and in-depth 

review activities continued, using specific minimum criteria for the evaluation of bids, in addition to dedicated standard contractual clauses. 

(147) Assessment carried out based on information available from open sources and/or provided by the supplier and/or performance indicators and/or on-site audits, through at least 
one  of  the  following  processes:  reputational  due  diligence,  qualification  process,  performance  evaluation  feedback  in  HSE  or  compliance  areas,  feedback  process,  assessment  on 
human rights issues (based on the SA8000 standard or similar).

Particular attention was devoted to topics identified as material in the value chain (for further details see   Value chain and main impacts): 

(i) for suppliers with higher emissions, an engagement activity was launched to ensure the disclosure of their Scope 1 and 2 emissions. 

To support them — and, more broadly, the entire supply chain — a dedicated tool for quantifying company-level emissions was also made 

available free of charge on the Open-es platform; (ii) in the area of human rights, documentary and on-site checks were carried out at higher-

risk suppliers (for further details see   Workers in Eni’s value chain); (iii) for suppliers characterised by complex supply chains with frequent 

use of subcontracting, the monitoring of the level of oversight of their supply chains continued, with the aim of holding the main players in 

Eni’s chain accountable for implementing ESG due diligence within their own supply chains.

The expenses related to the implementation of Eni’s Sustainable Supply Chain strategy are fully integrated into ordinary management and 

business operations and fall within the broader structural costs incurred. In pursuing a more sustainable management of the supply chain 

Eni aims to strengthen all levels of the chain by providing tools that enable suppliers to adopt and replicate the Eni model, while maintaining a 

systemic and inclusive approach. The Company also aims to promote greater accountability among direct commercial partners, particularly 

major market players, encouraging them to regularly conduct due diligence on their third parties and actively oversee environmental and social 

sustainability topics throughout the entire supply chain. At the same time, Eni is committed to intensifying internal checks on subcontractors 

and all entities with which it maintains business relationships, with particular attention to critical or high-risk contexts, adopting a more rigorous 

approach. This pathway is designed to enhance the Company’s ability to identify, prevent and mitigate risks, strengthening transparency and 

shared responsibility across the supply chain in the short and medium-term.

Supplier payment practices [G1-6]

In line with an approach based on transparency and fairness in supplier management, Eni has adhered to the Italian Responsible Payments 

Code, established by Assolombarda in 2014. Generally, supplier payments at Eni are managed according to uniform criteria and standardized 

procedures, without distinction by type, size, or geographical location. Eni’s standards149 establish a supplier payment term of 60 days for 

contracts under private law and 30 days for those governed by the Public Contracts Code (Italian Legislative Decree No. 36/2023). Individual 

contracts  of  Eni  SpA  and  its  subsidiaries  adopt  this  payment  terms,  except  in  cases  where  applicable  regulations  or  specific  business 

requirements dictate otherwise. For the average payment terms150 of Eni SpA’s suppliers and those of Italian subsidiaries in 2025, see the 

 Management Report/Other Information. During the reporting period, no judicial proceedings were pending in Italy against Eni SpA or its 

Italian subsidiaries regarding late payments to their suppliers.

(148) The metrics refer to engagement activities carried out with suppliers managed within the scope of MSG Procurement by Eni SpA and its subsidiaries.
(149) Also valid for subsidiaries for which Eni SpA carries out centralized procurement activities.
(150) The average payment term of suppliers is calculated with reference to Eni SpA and the subsidiaries for which supplier payment activities are carried out in a centralized manner by 
Eni SpA. The number of legal proceedings for late payments is recorded with reference to cases relating to amounts recognized and not contested (in substance and/or in their amount) 
by Eni to the supplier and pending in Italy; the information includes disputes pending in the reporting year, even if initiated previously or concluded during the year. The data refers to 
proceedings concerning procurement contracts for the purchase of goods, execution of works and provision of services, within the internal regulatory and management framework 
on procurement (Management System Guideline Procurement) and entered into by Eni SpA and its Italian subsidiaries. The data also includes disputes related to contracts that are no 
longer active or that expired during the reporting year. The Company is structuring a phased process that will enable it to expand the scope of its analysis.

TAX STRATEGY AND PAYMENTS TRANSPARENCY

[ENTITY-SPECIFIC; TAXO]

POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P]

POLICY

KEY CONTENTS:

 Code of Ethics

•  Commitment to carry out its activities in compliance with the applicable regulations on economic and financial 

sanctions as well as on trade controls, in particular by carrying out prior checks on all counterparties and requiring the 
same level of diligence from contractual partners.

  Guidelines on Tax Matters 

(Tax strategy and tax control)

•  Contribute to the tax revenues of host Countries, supporting the economic and social development of the territories in 

which Eni operates.

•  Commitment not to establish or locate the registered office of its subsidiaries in States that do not adopt international 

standards with regard to transparency and the exchange of tax information or in low-tax jurisdictions, except in cases of 
justified operational needs.

•  Application of the regulations and instruments provided for by national legislation and by the ‘International Conventions 
for the avoidance of double taxation on income and capital and for the prevention of tax evasion and avoidance’, in line 
with the interpretative guidelines of the OECD or the United Nations.

•  Maximisation of tax transparency through the publication of the Country by Country Report.
•  Use of mechanisms aimed at avoiding or reducing tax disputes.
•  Establishment of the Tax Control Framework (or tax risk management and control system) within the internal control 

system and definition of Tax Governance.

•  Minimisation of tax risk, through specific controls to ensure the accuracy and timeliness of tax calculation and payment 

within a transparent and accurate compliance framework, in order to prevent potential disputes.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T]

Eni’s tax strategy, approved by the Board of Directors and published on the 

 Company’s website, is based on the principles of transparency, 

honesty, fairness and good faith set out in the Code of Ethics and in the ‘OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises’. Its primary objective 

is the timely and accurate fulfilment of tax obligations in the various Countries in which Eni operates, in the awareness that the Group makes 

a significant contribution to public revenues, supporting local economic and social development. In this regard, while no quantitative targets 

have been defined, Eni is continuously committed to adopting conduct consistent with the principles of transparency, integrity, fairness and 

good faith set out in its Code of Ethics. Eni’s tax strategy aims to ensure the correct determination of taxes in the different jurisdictions in 

which it operates, to mitigate the risk of juridical and economical double taxation of profits, and to prevent potential disputes with the tax and 

judicial authorities of the Countries of activities. As part of its internal control system, Eni has implemented a Tax Control Framework (i.e. a 

tax risk management and control system), the purpose of which is to provide reasonable assurance that business activities are managed in 

line with the principles and objectives of the tax strategy (  Actions taken on material IROs).

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3]

Material impacts 

In carrying out its business activities, Eni works in close coordination with the governments of numerous Countries worldwide, which are 

often recipients of significant economic transactions. Payments to governments represent socio‑economic support for host States and their 

proper and transparent management helps to prevent potential corrupt practices, which could have adverse impacts on local communities. 

In addition to direct transactions with governments (such as, for example, production rights and royalties), Eni’s activities make a significant 

contribution to tax revenues, supporting the social and economic development and welfare of the Countries in which it operates151. Given the 

importance of these financial flows for collective welfare, Eni adopts conduct consistent with the principles of transparency, integrity, fairness 

and good faith. Transparency is a core value for Eni, which is committed to the voluntary disclosure of payments to governments and to 

combating all forms of corruption, while promoting the responsible use of financial resources in line with Sustainable Development Goal 16 

of the United Nations 2030 Agenda.

Material risks and opportunities

No material risks or opportunities were identified at Group level (see   Process and results of the double materiality assessment).

(151) For further information, reference should also be made to the Report on Payments to Governments.

239

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERAIL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A]

Set out below are the actions implemented by Eni on the basis of the material impacts, risks and opportunities identified. These actions are 

grouped into two main areas: (i) tax strategy; (ii) transparency initiatives.

TAX STRATEGY 

Eni’s Tax Strategy provides for the payment of taxes in the Countries where operations take place, in compliance with local tax regulations, 

and  rejects  aggressive  tax  policy  choices,  including  locating  legal  entities  in  so-called  tax  havens.  Eni  has  implemented  the  Tax  Control 

Framework,  for  which  the  Chief Transition  &  Financial  Officer  is  responsible,  structured  in  a  three-phase  process:  (i)  tax  risk  assessment 

(Risk  Assessment);  (ii)  identification  and  implementation  of  controls  designed  to  mitigate  the  identified  risks;  and  (iii)  verification  of  the 

effectiveness of controls and the related information flows (Reporting). The control framework together with processes and procedures have 

been designed to reduce the risk of violations with significant financial or reputational impacts (tax risk) to a relatively low level. In 2025, Eni 

Spa and its subsidiaries were not involved in any material disputes for violations of tax law or tax fraud that resulted in a final conviction. 

For more information on the status of the Group’s tax litigation, please refer to the Notes of the consolidated financial statements, 

 Legal 

proceedings  section. These  disputes  relate  to  the  technical  interpretation  of  local  tax  rules  —  often  highly  complex  —  and  are  managed 

with a conciliation approach. As part of its tax risks management activities, Eni adopts a proactive approach of early engagement with tax 

authorities and maintains relationships based on transparency, dialogue and collaboration, participating — where appropriate — in cooperative 

compliance arrangements, such as the collaborative-compliance regime in Italy.

TRANSPARENCY INITIATIVES

To promote the responsible use of resources and prevent corrupt practices, Eni supports the Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative (EITI) 

and has been a member since 2005, confirming its commitment to enhanced governance and transparency in the extractive sector. Since 

2023, Eni has served as an Alternate Member of the EITI Board, the initiative’s main decision-making body, responsible for setting strategic 

priorities and assessing Countries’ progress in implementing the EITI Standard. The EITI framework establishes specific expectations for 

supporting companies which, since 2021, also constitute an assessment tool aimed at identifying good practices and areas for improvement. 

In 2025, as part of the periodic assessment of compliance with the Expectations for EITI supporting companies, Eni fully met 8 expectations 

and partially met 1, out of a total of 9, increasing the number of fully met expectations from 7 to 8 compared to the previous year. At local level, 

Eni actively participates in EITI initiatives in the Countries where it operates, both directly through the Multi-Stakeholder Groups established 

in EITI-implementing Countries and indirectly through industry associations. In addition, in accordance with Italian Law No. 208/2015, Eni 

prepares the Country-by-Country Report (CbCR) required under Action 13 of the OECD/G20 Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (BEPS) project, 

aimed at enhancing transparency for tax administrations on the allocation of profits, the taxable base declared in each jurisdiction and the 

underlying  economic  activity,  as  well  as  the  proportionality  between  taxes  paid  and  value  generated  locally.  With  the  objective  of  further 

strengthening tax transparency for a wider range of stakeholders, Eni voluntarily publishes this report. In 2024, Italy transposed EU Directive 

No. 2021/2101, which introduces mandatory public disclosure of selected CbCR information starting from the 2025 fiscal year. The voluntary 

publication of the CbCR has been recognised by EITI as a best practice. Consistently with its support for EITI, Eni has also published a position 

on  contract  transparency,  encouraging  governments  to  align  with  the  new  contract  disclosure  standard  and  expressing  support  for  the 

mechanisms and initiatives adopted by Countries to promote transparency in this area.

CYBERSECURITY

[ENTITY-SPECIFIC]

POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P]

POLICY

KEY CONTENTS:

 Code of Ethics

•  Commitment to compliance with laws of privacy, data protection and confidentiality, as well as best practices applicable 

in the jurisdictions where Eni operates.

•  Protecting the privacy rights of Eni People, customers, suppliers and business partners, and those with whom Eni 

establishes relationships, using personal data only for defined and appropriate purposes.

Internal Regulatory System

•  Procedures and operating manuals relating to principles for the management of cybersecurity in ICT and Operational 

Technology (OT(a)) systems, such as vulnerability management, third party risk management, relevance assessment of 
applications, guidelines for the management of OT systems, identity and access management, management of the data 
sharing tools lifecycle and data security in test environments.

(a) Operational Technology (OT) refers to the set of hardware and software dedicated to the monitoring and direct control of physical processes, machinery and critical infrastructures. 
Unlike IT, which manages data, OT operates in the physical world, covering sectors such as manufacturing, energy and transport, using systems and technologies to automate processes 
(for example PLC, SCADA, DCS).

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T]

Eni ensures the protection of information systems, networks, data and digital infrastructures in terms of confidentiality, integrity and 

availability,  against  cyber  threats  and  attacks.  Eni  is  committed  to  annually  updating  its  cybersecurity  strategy  by  capturing  inputs 

both  internal  and  external  to  the  Company,  such  as  emerging  threats,  the  regulatory  landscape  and  constantly  evolving  emerging 

technologies. Although no specific quantitative targets related to cybersecurity have been defined, Eni’s commitments are structured 

around five areas:

• Regulatory, to ensure the highest level of compliance with internal and external regulations and requirements, as well as alignment with 

international security standards and best practices;

• Cultural, to promote a cybersecurity culture and disseminate awareness behaviours across the corporate population;

• Zero  Trust,  to  implement  an  approach  based  on  the  principle  ‘Never  trust,  always  verify’,  enable  new  operating  models  and  ensure 

protection against emerging threats;

• Cyber  Resilience,  to  strengthen  the  capability  to  prevent,  detect  and  respond  to  cyberattacks  by  increasing  resilience  in  the  event  of 

incidents, also leveraging generative AI to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of operational security processes;

• Operational security, to strengthen security safeguards in the industrial domain, extending monitoring services (Detection & Response) 

and introducing specific solutions to protect OT assets.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3]

Material impacts 

In a digitally interconnected and technology-driven world, Eni’s activities are exposed, across the entire value chain, to the occurrence of 

potential cybersecurity incidents. Such events may lead to the loss of data confidentiality and/or integrity following the disclosure or 

alteration of information relating to employees, customers or business partners and may harm the financial community, representing a 

threat to the security and privacy of the parties involved. Similarly, the unavailability of information systems supporting the provision of 

services to customers and business partners could have significant impacts on these stakeholders. Finally, the potential propagation 

of a cybersecurity incident to the information systems of Eni’s suppliers and partners could also result in serious impacts for those 

parties.

Material risks and opportunities 

Cybersecurity risk refers to the occurrence of cyberattacks capable of compromising information and communication technology (ICT) 

systems  and  industrial  control  systems  (ICS),  as  well  as  facilitating  the  misappropriation  of  information  sensitive  to  Eni.  For  further 

information, see the section 

 Risk factors and uncertainty/Legal, IT and financial risks. No material opportunities were identified.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A]

Based  on  the  commitments  pursued  in  the  different  areas,  Eni  has  implemented,  following  a  risk-based  approach,  several  initiatives  and 

defensive measures aimed at preventing incidents and containing their impacts. In particular, in 2025 Eni:

•  continued the Cybersecurity Culture programme, with more than 150 initiatives, aimed at promoting a cybersecurity culture through actions 

designed to disseminate ‘cyber-aware’ behaviours to all employees;

•  enhanced resilience by launching the project to align with the NIS 2 Directive, namely the new European cybersecurity standard designed 

to protect critical infrastructures and digital services from increasingly sophisticated threats, transposed into Italian law through Legislative 

Decree 138/2024;

•  promoted initiatives aimed at mitigating risks arising from a profoundly changed threat landscape, made even more critical by the use of 

artificial intelligence, whose exploitation by cyber criminals has contributed to significantly amplifying both the volume and the sophistication 

of attacks, which reached 511 million, including automated attacks, against internet-exposed applications;

•  strengthened  collaborations  with  public  entities,  universities  and  institutions,  such  as,  for  example,  the  collaboration  with  the  SERICS 

Foundation  (Security  and  Rights  in  CyberSpace)  within  the  context  of  the  PNRR  and  with  the  National  Cybersecurity  Agency  (ACN), 

strengthening prevention and response capabilities and promoting the sharing of best practices at national level (HyperSOC Project152);

•  delivered awareness and basic training workshops on cybersecurity to the wider national digital ecosystem — including teachers, parents 

and students from primary, lower-secondary and upper-secondary schools — with 24 initiatives also covering topics related to generative 

artificial intelligence.

(152) HyperSOC is the platform designed and developed by the National Cybersecurity Agency (ACN) to support national Security Operations Centers (SOCs) in the proactive detection 
of risk situations (cyber threats, vulnerabilities, improperly exposed services, etc.) within the networks under their responsibility.

ANNEX

Reporting principles 
and criteria [ESRS 2 BP-1; BP-2]

The Sustainability Statement, prepared on a consolidated Group basis, is approved by the Board of Director and subject to limited assurance 

by the same auditor of the consolidated financial statements. To guarantee the reliability and traceability of the information, Eni relies on 

a dedicated IT tool that centralizes the collection of data sourced from the various company systems. This system ensures a structured 

and verifiable management of the information flow and also traces the entire authorization process. In parallel, to protect the integrity of 

such  information,  appropriate  cybersecurity  measures  are  adopted  that  integrate  all  the  software  applications  with  advanced  protection 

systems  against  cyber  threats,  thus  guaranteeing  the  security  and  availability  of  the  data. The  structure  of  each  thematic  chapter  of  the 

Sustainability Statement follows the layout of the standards: Policies; Targets and commitments; Material Impacts, Risks and Opportunities 

(IROs); Actions related to the material IROs; Metrics. The Minimum Disclosure Requirements (MDR), required by the standards, relating to 

policies are examined in-depth in the table below, while those on actions, targets and metrics are examined in the specific thematic chapters. 

As regards the OpEx and CapEx relating to the actions described, they are reported in the respective dedicated chapters, where they are 

relevant and specific to the topics addressed. By contrast, OpEx and CapEx associated with ordinary operational management (business as 

usual) are not included, as they fall within the broader structural or cross-cutting costs incurred by Eni regardless of the implementation of 

the actions described, such as those for the management of personnel, customers and suppliers. It is recalled that, in the thematic chapters, 

the term ‘target’ is used only when the objectives meet the criteria defined by the ESRS; in other cases, reference is made to ‘commitments’. 

The calculation methodologies of the individual indicators are reported in the footnotes to the corresponding tables in the Metrics section. 

The information included in the report is identified after the materiality analysis and is collected, on an annual basis, for the years 2025 and 

2024. In general, the trends relating to the performance indicators are calculated also using decimal places not reported in the document. The 

data relating to the year 2025 constitute the best possible estimate available at the time of drafting this document. Where estimates or time 

horizons differ from those set out in the ESRS, they are, where necessary, explained in detail in the footnotes to the tables. With regard to the 

period under reporting, no information is reported as omitted as it is the object of intellectual property, know-how or classified as sensitive. 

Some values, compared to what was published in 2024, have undergone changes following a methodological change or an update of the 

data for information received following publication. The specific reasons are reported in the Metrics section of the indicators concerned; 

in particular, the injury indices in the Health and Safety chapter and the E-PRTR pollutants in the Pollution chapter for the year 2024 have 

been updated. Compared to last year, it is noted that metrics relating to biofeedstocks used in the biorefineries in Italy for the production of 

biofuels have been added, connected to the material opportunity ‘Biorefining and Feedstock’. The double materiality analysis also includes the 

impacts, risks and opportunities (IROs) along the value chain (see   Process and results of the double materiality assessment); therefore, in 

the event that policies, targets, actions or metrics also refer to actors in the value chain, this is indicated in the relevant section. Finally, within 

 Content Index are detailed any: (i) disclosure of qualitative and quantitative information deriving from other legislations; (ii) entity-specific 

indicators, as provided for by the ESRS, inspired by the GRI Oil & Gas Sector standard and/or indicators linked to strategic objectives; (iii) 

disclosure of information contained in the Management Report and recalled through cross-reference; (iv) the use of any phase-in.

Reporting boundary

The reporting boundary of the Sustainability Statement is aligned with the financial reporting boundary153 and, where required by the standards, 

it is appropriately extended to entities under operational control154, as defined by the criteria set out in Annex II of the CSRD Delegated Act and 

the EFRAG guidance on value chain. Alongside the mandatory boundary required by the ESRS, additional boundaries are applied to ensure 

comparability with the sector. 

(153) This refers to the boundary of the consolidated financial statements. For investments owned by Eni SpA please refer to the section 
 Annex to the notes on consolidated financial 
statements as of December 31, 2025. Furthermore, for references to corporate classification categories (subsidiaries, fully consolidated entities, Joint Operations, etc.), please refer 
 Note 1 ‘Significant accounting policies, estimates and judgements’ of the consolidated financial 
to the definitions set out in the IAS/IFRS accounting standards, as described in 
statements.
(154) Among the most relevant criteria considered for identifying operational control are the existence of a contractual document, the full authority to direct operational activities, and 
the full authority to manage the relations of the company/site/asset and to introduce and implement operational policies.

The different boundary types used in the Sustainability Statement155 are described below:

•  Financial Boundary, corresponding to the consolidation area of the financial statements, which includes subsidiaries and Joint Operations 

(JOs), both operated and non-operated in Eni’s equity share (including corporate and contractual arrangements, typical of the Oil & Gas 

industry)156. Within the document, the financial boundary is also referred to as the consolidated boundary;

•  Operated  Boundary,  which  includes  subsidiaries,  JOs,  and  any  Joint  Ventures  (JVs)  and  Associates  operated  at  100%  (JOs,  JVs  and 

Associates not operated by Eni are excluded);

•  Equity Boundary, which includes all Eni shareholdings in Eni’s equity share (subsidiaries, JOs, JVs and Associates in Eni’s equity share);

•  ESRS Boundary for GHG emissions, corresponding to the financial boundary alongside the operated shares of JOs and any JVs/Associates 

that are not consolidated but are operated by Eni.

It should be noted that any Associates, Joint Ventures and other minority investments over which Eni SpA does not exercise operational 

control are not included in the Sustainability Statement, except for specific KPIs requiring value chain information (e.g., Scope 3 emissions). 

For  acquisitions  occurring  during  the  reporting  year,  only  data  relating  to  the  relevant  months  are  disclosed157;  similarly,  for  disposals/

divestments, information is reported only for the months in which the company was in scope. The table below summarises the reporting 

boundaries applied to each topic within the Sustainability Statement.

Code of Ethics and Policies [MDR-P]

To enable the concrete implementation of what is stated in the mission and to guarantee integrity, transparency, fairness and effectiveness 

in its processes, Eni adopts specific rules for the performance of company activities and the exercise of powers, ensuring compliance 

with the general principles of traceability and segregation. All Eni’s operational activities are attributable to a map of processes functional 

to the company’s activity and integrated with the needs and control principles set out in the compliance and governance models and 

based  on  the  Articles  of  Association,  the  Code  of  Ethics  and  the  Corporate  Governance  Code,  on  the  231  Model,  on  the  principles 

of  Eni’s  control  system  on  mandatory  financial  and  sustainability  reporting  and  on  the  CoSO  Framework  (Committee  of  Sponsoring 

Organizations of the Treadway Commission). The 

 Code of Ethics, renewed in 2020, expresses the company values: integrity, respect 

and protection of human rights, transparency, promotion of development, operational excellence, innovation, teamwork and collaboration. 

Together with these values, the Code contains general principles158 and concrete rules of conduct, which provide practical guidance in 

company operations, addressed to the members of the management and control corporate bodies and to the employees of Eni and the 

subsidiaries, to all third parties, such as suppliers, commercial and industrial partners; the Code was drawn up taking into account their 

perspectives. To ensure widespread understanding, the Code is disseminated and enhanced through various initiatives, including specific 

training activity and translation into the languages of the Countries in which Eni operates. The updated version of the document can be 

consulted on the internet and intranet sites of Eni SpA and the subsidiaries. The Code of Ethics was developed with the involvement of 

management and was approved by the BoD, on the proposal of the CEO in agreement with the Chairman, having heard the opinion of the 

Board of Statutory Auditors and the Control and Risks Committee. The Code of Ethics constitutes a reference for Eni’s Internal Regulatory 

System159, and the main contents are recalled within the Policies section of the thematic chapters in which other regulatory documents160 

are also mentioned, among which:

•  ECG Policies161: public documents (except for the Privacy and data protection Policy), applicable to Eni SpA and its subsidiaries, whose 

‘Fundamental Guidelines’ are approved by the BoD, while the ‘Application Methods’ are approved by the Process Owner162, who is responsible 

for the design and its adequacy over time. Management and all Eni people are required to apply the regulations and to implement initiatives 

to prevent and identify irregularities and/or fraudulent acts;

•  Public positionings: public corporate positions on specific topics, proposed by the competent Process Owners, with approval usually by the 

CEO or Top Management (for example by the Financial Reporting Officer or by specific technical tables) and applicable to Eni SpA and its 

subsidiaries163;

•  Management  System  guidelines  (MSG)  and  Global  Procedures:  documents  that  are  part  of  the  Internal  Regulatory  System  that  guide 

the various  sustainability  topics  (of  which the Global Procedure ‘Management of  whistleblowing  reports  received by  Eni  SpA  and by  its 

Subsidiaries’ is public), applicable to Eni SpA and its subsidiaries, whose approval generally lies with the Process Owner, and drafted through 

the involvement of all internal stakeholders, in line with the relevant areas of responsibility.

(158) The document underlines, among other things, Eni’s commitment to respect for human rights in its own activities and those of commercial partners, in line with the United Nations 
Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises, as well as in compliance with the Voluntary Principles on Security and Human 
Rights. Other references considered are the SDGs and the Paris Pledge.

requirements (compliance), regulations or international frameworks; the reference rules of corporate governance (governance).
